                               Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP                       i
EXECUTIVE SUMMARY


Introduction and background
The Government of Ghana (GoG), acting through the Ministry of Sanitation and Water
Resources (MSWR), is implementing the Greater Accra Metropolitan Area (GAMA)
Sanitation and Water Project (SWP) Additional Financing (AF) with funds from the World
Bank. The AF project is a continuation of the original GAMA project which was implemented
from 2014 to 2020. The original GAMA project was implemented in 25 Metropolitan and
Municipal Assemblies in the Greater Accra Region of Ghana which led to among others,
provision of some 426 units of sanitation facilities in over 200 schools and health centers ( a
unit of the sanitation facilities comprising gender friendly improved toilets, water supply and
waste management facilities); over 42,000 household toilets provided for people in low-
income communities and serving more than 294,000 people; rehabilitation and reconstruction
of 2 simplified sewerage treatment facilities and network systems to serve over 48,000 low-
income community people in Bankuman and Ashaiman, all of which are meant to improve and
expand access to improved sanitation and water supply in the GAMA with emphasis on low-
income urban communities.


Following the successful and satisfactory implementation of the original GAMA Project, the
Bank’s Additional Financing (AF) to the Ministry is aimed to extend the project to unserved
areas in the GAMA and to expand the original Project to additional eight (8) Metropolitan and
Municipal Assemblies in the Greater Kumasi Metropolitan Area (GKMA) in order to replicate
the gains of the original GAMA project in these assemblies.


Overall, the objective of the GAMA S&W Project is to increase access to improved sanitation
and improved water supply in the GAMA and GKMA, with emphasis on low-income
communities and to strengthen management of environmental sanitation in the GAMA and
GKMA.


In collaboration with the Kumasi Metropolitan Assembly (KMA), the Ministry of Sanitation
and Water Resources (MSWR) intends to apply part of the GAMA S&W Project AF funds to
undertake some priority works to rehabilitate the existing Asafo Sewerage Treatment System




Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP                                                   ii
in the Kumasi Metropolitan Assembly in Ashanti Region of Ghana which is currently plagued
with challenges.


The Existing Asafo Sewerage Treatment System
The Asafo Sewerage Treatment System is a simplified sewerage constructed as a pilot project
in 1994 and has been in operation since. The treatment facility is located at Abinkyi south of
Asafo community in the Kumasi Metropolitan Assembly of Ashanti Region of Ghana. The
system operates purely by gravity and comprises a network of PVC pipes (sewers) and masonry
chambers/manholes which are installed at connection points, junctions and at intervals of
between 25m to 50m. Effluent from the treatment ponds is discharged into the Subin River
located nearby through an outfall drain connection. Initially designed and sewered for the
Asafo community alone comprising then a population of 20,000 users, the ASS now serves
about 50,000 people, and extends to other nearby communities such as Fanti New Town, the
Kumasi Technical University, OA Travel & Tour Bus Terminal, the Lancaster Kumasi Hotel
and the Kumasi Nurses and Midwifery Training College.


Asafo is a major community in Kumasi, the capital of the Ashanti Region, which is the second
most populous region in the country with 17.6% share of the country’s population of
30,832,019 (GSS, 2021). The Kumasi Metropolitan area is the most populous district in Ghana
with a total population of 443,981 of which 213,662 (48.1%) are males and 230,319 (51.9%)
are females.


Presently, the treatment plant and sewer system are plagued with numerous challenges which
are hindering the ability of the facility to operate optimally and according to its standards and
design objectives, affecting environmental sanitation management in the KMA. To help
address the prevailing situation and as part of the GAMA Sanitation and Water Project AF, the
ASS has been earmarked for upgrading through rehabilitation and expansion to adequately
serve the populations and extend to cover feasible unserved areas to improve access to
improved sanitation in the GKMA. However, due to the need to match the scope of
rehabilitation with the available funding for the project, the objective of this subproject is to
undertake some priority interventions to improve on the existing treatment plant and its
efficiency, as well as strengthening critical sections of the existing sewer network that are
defective (i.e., sewer pipes, broken manholes/inspection chambers, screens etc.,) to improve
flow within the sewer network and flood protection measures for the treatment plant facility.


Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP                                                     iii
Alternative Considerations
Various alternatives to the subproject have been considered and the Priority Works subproject
presents the best option to improve the function of the ASSS under the present circumstances.


The Proposed Priority Works interventions and location.
The proposed priority works will involve a combination of the following activities grouped
into three lots as follows to ensure timely completion of the works, which is estimated to last
for 6 months. Summary of the proposed activities and their locations are presented in the table
below:

Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP                                                           iv
The rehabilitation/construction activities will include:

 Demolishing
 Construction/reconstruction and rehabilitation works.
 Excavation works and installation/replacement of new sewer pipelines
 Backfilling and compaction of fill material.
 Dredging and desludging
 Conveyance and disposal of waste materials.


Construction Materials and Equipment
The materials required for the subproject include building materials such as cement, cement
block, sand, gravel, stone chippings, concrete, iron rods, water, PVC/HDM pipes and
admixtures. Other accessories will include barbed wire, wire mesh, balustrade, etc. The
construction equipment to be used includes excavators, backhoes, shovels/ pickaxes, concrete
mixers, haulage trucks, dump trucks, concrete dispensing trucks, water pumps etc.


Construction Labor
It is estimated that between 50 to 75 persons will be engaged during the construction phase of
the subproject works. This will include unskilled labor, drivers, masons, carpenters, plumbers,
electricians, mechanics, plant operators, engineers, and administrators. Although the Asafo
and Abinkyi communities are endowed with youth who are currently unemployed and so will
be available for employment as unskilled labor, labor influx and associated risks such as social
conflict, increased sexually transmitted diseases (STD, HIV/AIDs) and gender-based violence
may occur. In this regard, adequate measures have been provided in this ESIA including Code
of Conduct to regulate the conduct of contractors and their labor force to ensure the risks
associated with labor influx are minimized. Labor camps will not be established to house
workers during construction, rather the workers will go to their various homes and return to
work the next day.


In line with environmental permitting requirements as provided under the Environmental
Assessment Regulations of 1999, Legislative Instrument (LI) 1652, KMA registered the
proposed subproject with the Environmental Protection Agency (EPA), Kumasi which
classified the proposed subproject as one which requires an Environmental and Social Impact
Assessment (ESIA) to be carried out to help understand the likely implications of the proposal



Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP                                                    v
in order to inform the environmental permitting decision-making prior to project
implementation. A Scoping Report was duly submitted to the EPA. This report is therefore the
revised draft Environmental and Social Impact Assessment Report to the EPA for review and
seeking environmental permit for the works.


Objective of the ESIA Study
The primary objective of the ESIA study is to identify key adverse environmental, socio-
economic and cultural risks and impacts associated with the proposed interventions at Asafo
and Abinkyi that can affect the subproject viability and sustainability, and to formulate
appropriate mitigation and management measures required to address any adverse
impacts/risks to acceptable limits, and therefore assure subproject success. The outcome of the
ESIA study is also to assist MSWR, KMA and GAMA-SWP PCU to consider the consequences
of implementing the current subproject designs and actions in the planning process, to choose
the most appropriate actions and to modify the designs where appropriate on environmental
grounds, and to adopt the proposed mitigation measures to minimize any remaining impacts.


Relevant Policies, Legal and Administrative Framework
Under the provisions of the Ghana Environmental Assessment Regulations 1999 (LI 1652) the
proposed subproject is classified under projects for which an ESIA is required. The EPA Act
490 (1994) established the Agency and entrusted it with the responsibility of ensuring
compliance with ESIA process and procedures in the planning and execution of development
projects.


The subproject is World Bank Funded and therefore it has to also conform to the World Bank’s
Operational Policies (OPs) which govern the funding agreement between the Bank and the
Government of Ghana. The objective of the laws and policies is to prevent or at least minimize
biophysical environment and socioeconomic-cultural risks and impacts while increasing the
environmental and the socio-economic benefits of such subprojects. The specific policies,
legal and institutional frameworks considered relevant to the proposed subproject are discussed
in detail in this Report.


The MSWR, KMA and the GAMA -PCU is fully aware of the need to adhere to the applicable
policies, laws and regulation requirements governing the implementation of the proposed



Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP                                                  vi
subproject during the planning and implementation of the priority works, and its operation and
will leave no stone unturned in this direction.


Stakeholder Consultations
Key stakeholders to the proposed priority works have been consulted, these include some
regulatory bodies, local government institutions and project affected persons. Stakeholder
consultation is a process and would continue throughout project implementation.
The main objective of the consultations with stakeholders was to discuss the proposed
subproject environmental and social implications and to identify alternatives for consideration.
Specifically, the consultations resulted in:
 providing information about the proposed subproject to stakeholders;
 providing opportunities for stakeholders to discuss their opinions and concerns;
 providing and discussing with stakeholders the alternatives considered to reduce anticipated
   impacts;
 identifying and verifying the significance of environmental, social and health impacts; and
 informing the process of developing appropriate mitigation and management options.


The key stakeholders consulted include the following:
Subproject Proponent
 Kumasi Metropolitan Assembly (KMA).
 Greater Kumasi Metropolitan Area- Sanitation and Water Project (GKMA- SWP).


Government Institutions
 Ghana Water Company Limited (GWCL)- Kumasi South Regional Office, Kumasi;
 Electricity Company of Ghana (ECG), Kumasi Bulk Supply Substation at Abinkyi;
 Ghana Grid Company (GRIDCo), Kumasi Office at Abinkyi;
 The Wildlife Division of the Forestry Commission (FC);
 Water Resources Commission (WRC)- Head Office, Accra;
 Environmental Protection Agency (EPA)- Head Office, Accra and Regional Office,
   Kumasi.
 Asokwa Municipal Assembly;
 Lands Commission, Kumasi; and
 Labor Office, Kumasi.



Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP                                                   vii
Other Stakeholders
 Asafo Unit committee members;
 Assembly Members of Asafo and Abinkyi areas;
 Project Affected Persons (PAPs) including Some Households and Businesses that are
    connected to the Asafo Sewerage System;
 Environment Engineering Limited (EEL);
 Asafo Traditional Authority (AsTA);
 Nhyiaeso Traditional Authority/ Council;
 Kumasi Technical University (KsTU);
 VIP Company Limited (VIP);
 Intercity STC Limited (STC);
 PROTOA at Abinkyi Station;
 Ghana Private Road Transport Union (GPRTU) at Abinkyi Station;
 Zoom Lion Company Limited;
 Abinkyi Traders Association;
 Residents including businesses and households connected to the existing sewer lines
 Mechanics at Abinkyi Station; and
 Residents/ squatters around the Asafo Sewage Treatment Ponds.


The following are highlights of the key issues/concerns raised by stakeholders/affected persons
during the consultations:
   Residents and pedestrians were concerned that if adequate health and safety measures such
    as signage are not put along the sewer route construction corridors, may of them will fall
    victims to injuries and accidents. They therefore were of the opinion that the environmental
    and social assessment activities would provide public health and safety measures for the
    contractors to observe during the works.
   Most of the traders and especially those selling on the pavements, have appealed to the city
    authorities to allow them back on the pavement once the project was completed so that they
    could continue to sell at their present location since that was their only means of livelihood.
   Most of the business operators and squatters at the treatment plant site feared being
    displaced and losing their livelihood. This they believe will have great impact on their
    socioeconomic lives and impact their families. They pleaded that the project find a suitable
    place to relocate them for shelter and be able to continue their businesses.



Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP                                                     viii
   Regarding compensation to affected persons, their expectation was that they will be paid
    adequate compensation for the losses they will suffer because of the subproject works.
   Shop owners also expressed concerns of road traffic when construction begins.


Adequate responses to the PAPs were provided by the Consultant to the effect that that their
concerns would be taken up in the Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA) and
the Resettlement Action Plan (RAP) study being carried out for the subproject (see Annex 4).


The Potential Environmental and Social Impacts and Risks of the Proposed Priority
works
The potential beneficial and adverse impacts of the proposed priority works have been
identified and discussed based on the nature of the proposed subproject and area of project
influence.


The potential positive impacts of the project include:
 Effective Wastewater Management: Wastewater from residences, businesses, and industries
    in the subproject’s Area of Influence (AoI) will be properly managed and transported wholly
    to the treatment plant through the sewer network thereby assuring proper sanitation and
    hygiene in the community.
 Pollution Prevention: The risk of wastewater pollution of the Subin River will be drastically
    reduced as all the wastewater will be discharged into the treatment plant and thus stop the
    direct release of untreated wastewater into the Subin River.
 Improved Sanitation and Hygiene: There would be improved liquid waste collection and
    treatment in the subproject catchment in the KMA. The rehabilitation and reconstruction of
    the sewerage system will create opportunities that will result in more household connections
    and improved sanitation.
 Improved Public Health: By appropriately disposing of the wastewater for treatment, it will
    assist in preventing the transmission of diseases and the contamination of water sources,
    hence lowering the risk of contracting waterborne diseases, and ultimately improve human
    health and general welfare, and indirectly ensure household cost savings.
 Employment Generation: The subproject will provide employment opportunities throughout
    its entire duration. Employing skilled labor such as surveyors, engineers and machine
    operations as well as unskilled labor (eg. digging trenches, etc.) will improve household
    incomes and ultimately their well-being. Employing community workers will further create


Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP                                                   ix
   a sense of ownership within the area and reduce chances of vandalism during the operational
   phase. Local consultants contracted to carry out activities during the various stages of the
   project will generate revenue for such local firms.


The key adverse environmental and social issues which could possibly arise from the various
stages of the subproject have been evaluated and presented in Chapter 8 of this Report.
They include:
 Waste generation and disposal;
 Air quality deterioration;
 Vibration and noise nuisance;
 Loss of vegetation/ habitat and impacts on flora and fauna;
 Water pollution/ siltation of stream channels and loss of aquatic organisms and
    biodiversity;
 Land degradation and loss of soil resources;
 Traffic congestion at the construction zones;
 Odor nuisance;
 Risk of damaging underground utility supply services and disruption of utility services;
 Displacement of people doing business along the sewer pipeline routes and those residing
    at the sewage treatment plant site and the resultant loss of their livelihoods;
 Exposure of workers to noise, dust, odor and workplace accidents e.g., slips, falls etc. as an
    occupational health and safety issue;
 Exposure of some community members to noise, dust, odor nuisance, fresh sewage, open
    trenches and accidents involving construction vehicles as a public safety and health
    concern;
 Flooding issues;
 Increased risk of Sexually Transmitted Diseases;
 Child labor; and
 Gender based violence including sexual harassment, child abuse and exploitation due to
    labor influx.


Proposed Enhancement And Mitigation Measures For The Potential Environmental
And Social Impacts And Risks Identified
Proposed Environmental and Social Management Plan (ESMP) which aims to ensure that the
potential environmental and social impacts and risks identified are reduced to the barest


Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP                                                    x
minimum, or completely eliminated during pre-construction, construction, operation and
decommissioning phases of the proposed priority works, are presented in Chapter 9 of the
Report. In doing so, the mitigation plans are provided separately for each of the three (3)
separate Lots in which the priority works will be executed on the ground i.e., Lot 1- Sewer
pipelines route/ chambers and manholes rehabilitation works; Lot 2- Treatment ponds
rehabilitation works, and Lot 3- Flood protection and perimeter fencing works, respectively in
Table 43, Table 44 and Table 45 in this Report. The decision to provide separate mitigation
plans for each of the three components rather than a single common mitigation plan, is based
on several key considerations including as follows:
 By providing separate mitigation plans, the plan tailors the mitigation measures to the
    specific risks and challenges faced by each contractor.
 A separate mitigation plan for each component enables more effective monitoring and
    compliance. It allows for targeted assessment of the environmental and social impacts
    relevant to each contractor's activities, facilitating better oversight and ensuring that
    mitigation measures are properly implemented and effective.
 It prevents important issues from being overlooked or overshadowed by broader concerns
    that may be addressed in a single, overarching mitigation plan. Separating the mitigation
    plans ensures clear accountability for each contractor. It delineates their respective
    responsibilities regarding environmental and social mitigation in the ESIA Report,
    reducing ambiguity and potential disputes over who is responsible for addressing specific
    impacts; and
 Separate mitigation plans allow for greater adaptability and flexibility in responding to the
    evolving needs and circumstances of each component of the subproject. It enables
    adjustments to mitigation measures based on ongoing monitoring and feedback, ensuring
    that environmental and social risks and impacts are effectively managed throughout the
    project lifecycle.


Environmental and Social Monitoring Program
To ensure effectiveness and compliance with sound environmental and social practices and
ensure sustainability of the sewerage system, a provisional environmental and social
management and monitoring program to help manage and monitor the risks and impacts and
which will help sustain environmental quality within acceptable guidelines/standards,
including monitoring roles and responsibilities have been provided in the Report. The
programme includes a proposed Monitoring Plans for monitoring the effectiveness of the


Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP                                                  xi
implementation of each of the ESMPs mentioned above i.e., Table 43, Table 44 and Table 45
are provided. The Monitoring Plans are contained in Table 46, Table 47 and Table 48
respectively in this Report.


Grievance Redress
Although the ESIA preparation process and methodology was carried out in a consultative and
participatory manner, yet a grievance resolution procedure which aims at addressing and
resolving grievances or complaints from Project Affected Persons (PAPs) promptly, fairly and
in a manner to the extent possible, acceptable to all parties during the proposed subproject
implementation is provided for in this ESIA Report and it is consistent with the Project’s GRM.
The membership and functions of the Grievance redress Committees are also proposed as part
of the grievance redress process.


Capacity Building Plan
All relevant stakeholders including contractors and their labor force, the KMA and GAMA -
PCU officials, community opinion leaders, NGOs, project affected persons etc. who will be
involved in the implementation, monitoring and supervision of the proposed subproject
implementation will undergo training to create understanding on the ESIA requirements, the
roles and responsibilities of the stakeholders in order to ensure compliance with the ESIA.


Estimated Cost for the ESIA Implementation
The estimated total cost for the ESIA implementation is GHC1,169,600.00. This involves:
    i.       Implementation of the environmental and social impacts mitigation measures
             implementation is estimated to cost GHS684,600.00 for the three Lots (this cost
             excludes some of the mitigation management costs already included in the BoQs)
    ii.      Monitoring of the mitigation measures implementation is estimated to cost
             GHS255,000.00 for the three Lots (this cost excludes some of the mitigation
             management costs already included in the BoQs)
    iii.     Grievance Redress: GHS80,000.
    iv.      Capacity Building: GHS150,000.


Conclusion
This ESIA study has identified issues of socioeconomic and biophysical environmental
importance, some are positive and others negative. The study has thus far confirmed the


Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP                                                   xii
potential impacts and risks associated with the implementation of the proposed subproject and
appropriate enhancement and mitigation measures have also been provided in the
Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA) report.


The implementation of the proposed ASSSPWP will comply with the relevant national
environmental protection laws and international good practice requirements especially the
triggered WB OPs. The proposed subproject will help improve the function of the ASSS and
avoid discharge of untreated wastewater into the Subin River.


The KMA will comply with all regulatory requirements by securing relevant statutory permits
for the construction and operation of the interventions. KMA and with the assistance of
GAMA-PCU will work with the Contractor to ensure compliance with the conditions of the
various permits and authorisations. The KMA will ensure that the ASSSPWP is implemented
in line with all relevant national as well as international policies, laws and standards/ guidelines
to enhance the intended benefits and reduce potential negative impacts to acceptable levels.

Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP                                    xxiv
1.0      INTRODUCTION


The Government of Ghana (GoG), acting through the Ministry of Sanitation and Water
Resources (MSWR), is implementing the Greater Accra Metropolitan Area (GAMA)
Sanitation and Water Project (SWP) Additional Financing (AF) with funds from the World
Bank. The AF project is a continuation of the original GAMA project which was implemented
from 2014 to 2020. The original GAMA project was implemented in 25 Metropolitan and
Municipal Assemblies in the Greater Accra Region of Ghana which led to among others,
provision of some 426 units of sanitation facilities in over 200 schools and health centers ( a
unit of the sanitation facilities comprising gender friendly improved toilets, water supply and
waste management facilities); over 42,000 household toilets provided for people in low-income
communities and serving more than 294,000 people; rehabilitation and reconstruction of 2
simplified sewerage treatment facilities and network systems to serve over 48,000 low- income
community people in Bankuman and Ashaiman, all of which are meant to improve and expand
access to improved sanitation and water supply in the GAMA with emphasis on low-income
urban communities.


Following the successful and satisfactory implementation of the original GAMA Project, the
Bank’s Additional Financing (AF) to the Ministry is aimed to extend the project to unserved
areas in the GAMA and to expand the original Project to additional eight (8) Metropolitan and
Municipal Assemblies in the Greater Kumasi Metropolitan Area (GKMA) in order to replicate
the gains of the original GAMA project in these assemblies.


Overall, the objective of the GAMA S&W Project is to increase access to improved sanitation
and improved water supply in the GAMA and GKMA, with emphasis on low-income
communities and to strengthen management of environmental sanitation in the GAMA and
GKMA.


1.1      Components of the GAMA AF Project


The AF Project has four components. They are:
Component 1 – Provision of Environmental Sanitation Services to Priority Low-Income
Areas in GAMA and GKMA The component aims to increase access to environmental
sanitation services in low-income areas of the GAMA and GKMA, with a strong focus on


Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP                                                   1
liquid sanitation (excreta disposal). About 42,000 additional household bio-digester toilets will
be constructed in selected low-income communities of GAMA (12,000) and GKMA (30,000)
for a total estimated 252,000 beneficiaries.


Component 2: Improvement in Operational Efficiency and Expansion of Water
Distribution Networks GAMA and GKMA. The component aims to improve and expand
the water distribution network in GAMA and GKMA to provide piped water to an estimated
300,000 people living in low-income communities of the two cities. About 120 km of new
pipeline will be laid and 10,000 new connections will be provided to low-income households.
Preparation of Urban Water supply masterplan will be financed, and a study will also be
conducted to assess and review feasibility of available options for efficient and sustainable
long-term supply of water to GKMA, in face of changing conditions like population growth,
increased economic activities, and climate change. This component will also support
operational efficiency improvements in GWCL and undertake Non-Revenue Water (NRW)
reduction activities in selected operational areas in GAMA.


Component 3: Planning, Improvement and Expansion of Environmental Sanitation
Services.
The objective of this component is to develop integrated GKMA-wide plans for environmental
sanitation and to finance critical elements of the city’s sanitation service chain to improve
wastewater and septic sludge management. Building on the success of the parent project, the
AF will include simplified gravity based decentralized condominial sewerage systems for
GKMA. This component will support the construction of processing plants for the biosolid
from the Household (HH) bio-digesters constructed under both the parent and AF projects in
order to convert them into useful end-use products for various purposes such as for horticulture
as a fertilizer replacement, in line with circular economy principles. The decomposed material
from the activities of the micro and macro organisms can also be used as compost.


Component 4: Project Implementation and Institutional Strengthening.
The objective of this component is to provide institutional strengthening and technical
assistance (TA) to municipal, metropolitan and national institutions, to facilitate horizontal
learning between the GAMA and GKMA stakeholders through knowledge sharing workshops
and production and dissemination of Behavioral Change Communication (BCC) materials, as
well as the promotion of private sector initiatives including in the operation and management


Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP                                                    2
of sludge processing plants. The project provides hygiene education, including awareness
programs for educating boys, girls, and other stakeholders on the importance of proper
Menstrual Hygiene Management (MHM). Learning from the implementation of the parent
project, the AF will also help improve coordination between various responsible agencies in
the sector to ensure sustainability of the services.


1.2      Proposed Intervention for Rehabilitation and Expansion of the Asafo Simplified
         Sewerage System


As part of the GAMA AF, the existing Asafo Sewerage Treatment Plant and its sewer network
system has been earmarked for upgrading through rehabilitation and expansion to cover
feasible unserved areas to improve access to improved sanitation in the GKMA. However, due
to the need to match the scope of rehabilitation with the available funding in the project, it is
proposed that the activities be limited to some priority interventions to improve on the existing
treatment plant and its efficiency, as well as strengthening critical sections of the existing sewer
network that are defective (i.e., sewer pipes, broken manholes/inspection chambers, screens
etc.,) to improve flow, be undertaken. It is however noted that this ESIA will be updated to
cover the full scope of activities proposed for the rehabilitation of the sewerage treatment
facility and future additional works beyond the current priority works.


The Asafo Sewerage System (ASS) is a simplified sewerage scheme constructed as a pilot
project in 1994 and has been in operation since. The system operates purely by gravity and
comprises a network of PVC pipes (sewers) and masonry chambers/manholes which are
installed at connection points, junctions and at intervals of between 25m to 50m.


The sewerage system which was initially designed for the Asafo community comprising then
320 tenement houses consisting of 4,000 households and a population of 20,000 users now
serves about 50,000 people, extended to Asafo Lorry Park, four (4) schools, public toilets, and
parts of other nearby communities such as Fanti New Town, the Kumasi Technical University,
OA Travel & Tour Bus Terminal, the Lancaster Kumasi Hotel and the Kumasi Nurses and
Midwifery Training College are connected to the treatment plant located in Abinkyi
Community about 1km south of the Asafo community.




Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP                                                       3
Asafo is a major community in Kumasi, the capital of the Ashanti Region, which is the second
most populous region in the country with 17.6% share of the country’s population of
30,832,019 i.e., according to the 2021 Population and Housing Census report (GSS, 2021).
The Kumasi Metropolitan area is the most populous district in Ghana with a total population
of 443,981 of which 213,662 (48.1%) are males and 230,319 (51.9%) are females.


Presently, the treatment plant and sewer system are plagued with numerous challenges which
are hindering the ability of the facility to operate optimally and in accordance with standards
and design objectives.


Safeguards Concerns of the Proposed Priority Activities
The proposed priority activities implementation has the potential to impact negatively on the
environment, and private properties and economic activities of people, especially those living
in proximity of the proposed interventions. The potential negative environmental and social
impacts and risks will include waste generation and disposal, water pollution, noise pollution,
air quality deterioration, odor, workplace accidents e.g., stepping on sharp objects and cuts,
slip and fall, disruption of utility services, disruption of human and traffic flow, flooding,
temporary economic and physical displacement and likely disputes/tensions in the affected
local communities/stakeholders.


In line with Ghana’s Environmental Assessment Regulations 1999 (LI 1652), the proposed
subproject falls in the category of undertakings (Regulation 2) for which an Environmental and
Social Impact Assessment is required. The Environmental and Social Impacts Assessment
(ESIA) will help understand the likely implications, alternatives, and mitigation measures to
consider, to ensure sound decision-making, and sustainable development of the subproject. The
proposed subproject also triggered the World Bank Environmental Assessment Policy (O.P.
4.01) and the World Bank Involuntary Resettlement Policy (O.P. 4.12). The O.P. 4.01 requires
assessment of the potential environmental and social impacts and risks and preparation of ESIA
report that adequately provides measures to mitigate the risks and impacts of the intervention
on the environment and people.


The Ministry of Sanitation and Water Resources (MSWR), in seeking to comply with the
Ghana National Environmental Regulations and other relevant national laws, and those of the
World Bank Safeguards Policy on Environmental Assessment (OP 4.01), has prepared this


Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP                                                  4
Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA) for the proposed subproject.             A
separate/standalone Resettlement Action Plan (RAP) has also been prepared for the proposed
priority works to address the potential social impacts related to loss of property and income
opportunities to satisfy the World Bank Safeguards Policy on Involuntary Resettlement (OP
4.12) and other relevant national laws.


Consistent with the Environmental Assessment Regulations 1999 (LI 1652), the preparation
of this ESIA first involved a scoping study. This ESIA report is produced through the
findings of the Scoping study and incorporates an Environmental and Social Management
Plan (ESMP) to obtain an environmental permit from the Environmental Protection Agency
(EPA) for the works. This ESIA Report presents the outcomes of the ESIA study.


1.3      Purpose of the ESIA Study


The aim and purpose of the ESIA study is to obtain an environmental permit for the execution
of the proposed subproject by satisfying both legal and institutional obligations given under
the Environmental Assessment Regulations 1999 (Legislative Instrument 1652), and the
Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) Act 1994 (Act 490).           It is also to satisfy the
environmental and social safeguard policies of the World Bank (WB) i.e. the Operational
Policies (OPs).


In line with statutory requirements, an environmental permit is required for the subproject
implementation, which involves the conduct of an ESIA study for the proposed subproject.
This revised draft Environmental and Social Impact Assessment (ESIA) is prepared in
fulfillment of the permitting requirements administered by the EPA and in accordance with the
World Bank’s OPs.


Accordingly, the Kumasi Metropolitan Assembly (KMA) with support from the Project
Coordinating Unit (PCU) of the GAMA Project registered the proposed subproject with EPA.
A Scoping Report to generate the relevant Terms of Reference (ToR) to guide a satisfactory
conduct of the ESIA study was prepared and shared with both the EPA and the World Bank.


1.4      Objectives of the ESIA Study




Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP                                                 5
The primary objective of the ESIA study is to identify key adverse environmental, socio-
economic and cultural risks and impacts associated with the proposed interventions at Asafo
and Abinkyi that can affect the subproject viability and sustainability, assess and evaluate the
potential risks and impacts, and formulate appropriate mitigation and management measures
required to address any adverse impacts/risks to acceptable limits, and therefore assure the
subproject success. The outcome of the ESIA study is also to assist MSWR/ GAMA-SWP
PCU and KMA to consider the consequences of implementing the current subproject designs
and actions in the planning process, to choose the most appropriate actions and to modify the
designs where appropriate on environmental grounds, and to adopt the proposed mitigation
measures to minimize any remaining impacts.


The specific objectives of the ESIA study are to:
a. Identify and assess potential environmental and social impacts and risks that are likely to
     emanate from the construction/provision of the various interventions;
b. Analyze alternative sites, infrastructure and interventions that may pose less environmental
     and social damage than the proposed interventions;
c. Recommend feasible and cost-effective measures and processes to mitigate potential
     adverse risks and impacts or enhance positive environmental and social outcomes that
     could emanate from the implementation of the interventions;
d. Prepare a Provisional Environmental and Social Management and Monitoring Plan
     (PESMMP)/ for mitigating the potential environmental and social impacts of the proposed
     interventions and for monitoring the effectiveness of the mitigation measures, and
e. Integrate environmental and social considerations into the technical engineering designs of
     the interventions and foster on the ground implementation of sustainable measures during
     execution of the proposed investments.


This ESIA study has been carried out in accordance with the requirements of Ghana’s
Environmental Assessment Regulations, 1999 (LI 1652) and the Ghana Environmental Impact
Assessment (EIA)1 Procedures as well as the World Bank’s Operational Policies, OP4.01 and
the World Bank Group’s Environmental Health and Safety Guidelines (EHSG).




1 The Ghana EPA considers EIA and ESIA to be the same as the definition of the Environment in Ghana encompasses both the
biophysical and socioeconomic as well as the institutional environments. The LI 1652 specifies the conduct of EIA and not ESIA
and submission of an EIS



Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP                                                                                  6
1.5      Justification of the ASSSPWP


The original Asafo Simplified Sewerage System (ASSS) which covers an area of
approximately 45ha consisted of sewer pipelines and four (4) stabilization ponds for treating
the sewage. The sewerage system was initially designed for 20,000 users but now serves circa
50,000 people covering Asafo community, Asafo Lorry Park, four (4) schools, a few public
toilets, a small section of the Fante New Town community including the Kumasi Technical
University (KsTU) and the Lancaster Kumasi City Hotel, formerly Golden Tulip, Kumasi
(30m³/day). The capacity of the treatment ponds is 2,250m³/day but currently handling
873m³/day and thus operating at about 40% capacity. There is the possibility of connecting
the flows from Bantama 4th Battalion (4BN) Army Barracks (200m³/day) and other areas to the
ASSS.


However, the ASSS is bedeviled with a number of Environmental and Social (E&S) challenges
including (i) discharge of effluent not meeting the required standards, (ii) diversion/ discharge
of untreated wastewater into drains, (iii) encroachment of portions of the sewer lines and the
site for sewage treatment by mechanics, trucks drivers and bulk coconut sellers, (iv)
encroachment of the buffer zone between the Subin River (the river channel is lined from
Kejetia to Kaase) and the sewage treatment ponds site and the encroached area is being used
for residential and business purposes (mostly wooden structures), and (v) ease of access to the
site due to lack of security and fence just to mention a few.


The proposed Priority Works Project (PWP) will therefore help to improve the sanitation
situation in the Asafo and Abinkyi areas and assist in improving the health and well-being of
the people when the ASSS is rehabilitated to its original function.


1.6      Methodology for the Preparation of the ESIA Report


The scope of the ESIA study included the following among others:
 Collection and review of available documentation relevant to the subproject and in
      particular the existing environmental (biophysical, socioeconomic, cultural and
      institutional) conditions;




Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP                                                    7
 Subproject site inspections and trekking along routes connecting subproject sites to confirm
    baseline environmental issues and conditions to be affected or likely to develop from the
    subproject implementation;
 Identification of potential environmental and social impacts of the various subproject
    alternatives;
 Assessment of the various subproject alternatives based mainly upon the opportunities and
    constraints to the bio-physical, socio-economic and cultural environments;
 Develop a proposal for mitigating potential adverse impacts, to be taken into consideration
    in the subproject design and execution;
 Consultations and discussions with relevant government institutions and beneficiary
    communities or the Project Affected Persons (PAPs) as well as other Interested and
    Affected Parties (I&APs); and
 Develop a Provisional Environmental and Social Management Plan (PESMP) describing
    mitigation, monitoring and institutional measures to be taken during preconstruction,
    construction, operation and maintenance, and decommissioning of the ASSSPWP to
    eliminate adverse impacts or reduce them to acceptable levels.


The approach and methodology adopted for the ESIA study included:
 Desk study of previous report and data review;
 Field work;
 Consultations; and
 Reporting.


1.6.1 Desk Study of Previous Reports and Data Review
Review of relevant documents through desktop studies, and other relevant wastewater
treatment reports and research, formed part of the methodology for the preparation of this ESIS.
Collection and review of available documentation relevant to the subproject and in particular
the existing environmental and social conditions. The 1:50,000 topographical map of the
subproject area was obtained from the Survey Department and studied to identify and confirm
existing water bodies within the subproject area, beneficiary communities, the topography and
relief of the area and other relevant geographical features. Some previous documents relating
to the assessment of the subproject have been reviewed and they include the following:
 Environmental protection Agency Act, Act 490 (1994);
 Environmental Assessment Regulation of Ghana LI 1652 (1999);


Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP                                                   8
 World Bank Environmental Assessment Policy (O.P. 4.01);
 GAMA SWP AF Final Environmental and Social Management Framework (ESMF), June
    2020;
 GAMA SWP AF Resettlement Policy Framework (RPF), April 2020;
 The Preliminary Assessment Report for the Asafo Sewerage Rehabilitation Priority Works
    (2023);
 Environmental and Social Screening Report of the proposed Asafo Sewerage Treatment
    Facility Rehabilitation Priority Works;
 2010 Population and Housing Census, District Analytical Report- Kumasi Metropolitan
    Assembly published by Ghana Statistical Service (GSS) in October 2014;
 2021 Population and Housing Census Reports;
 Scoping Report of the ASSSPWP, December 2023;
 Draft Resettlement Action Plan (RAP) for the ASSSPWP, January 2024; and
 Information obtained from www.ghanadistricts.com concerning the profiles of KMA i.e.
    the Medium Term Development Plan (MTDP).


1.6.2 Field Work
Field visits and field inspections were carried out at existing sewage treatment plant area and
trekking along the existing sewerage pipeline routes proposed for the priority works to assess
the current conditions of the treatment facility and the sewer lines and their catchments, and to
confirm baseline environmental issues and conditions to be affected or likely to develop from
the implementation of the priority works. This also included an assessment of the resettlement
needs of persons whose properties/ assets may be impacted adversely during the construction
phase of the subproject.


Also, a detailed sampling of the wastewater and sediments in the treatment ponds (anaerobic,
facultative and maturation) was carried out for in-situ measurements and laboratory analysis of
relevant water quality parameters. Other studies carried out included aquatic biology studies,
terrestrial flora and fauna studies, and ambient air quality and noise assessment of the
subproject Area of Influence (AoI).


1.6.3 Consultations
Consultations and discussions were held with relevant government institutions and opinion
leaders and residents of the Asafo and Abinkyi communities to confirm or otherwise,


Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP                                                    9
information obtained from the desk study or observations made from the field inspections and
during the Scoping study. The consultation activities provided the opportunity to gather
baseline data including environmental and social impacts, and enhanced networking.


The consultations included visitations to the offices of the stakeholders, face-to-face meetings,
focus group discussions and interviews.


The consultations held included engagements with the following key stakeholders:
 Colan Consult Limited, the design engineers of the Priority Works Project (PWP);
 The GAMA PCU i.e., Accra and Kumasi (GKMA);
 Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) Regional Office in Kumasi;
 Kumasi Metropolitan Assembly (KMA);
 Waste Management Department (WMD) of KMA;
 Wildlife Division of the Forestry Commission/ Kumasi Zoological Gardens;
 Electricity Company of Ghana (ECG), Kumasi Bulk Supply Substation at Abinkyi;
 Ghana Grid Company (GRIDCo), Kumasi Office at Abinkyi;
 Environmental Engineering Limited (EEL), current managers of the ASSS;
 Water Resources Commission; and
 Some Potential PAPs.


Annex 1 shows the Stakeholder Engagement Form used for the consultations.


1.6.4 Reporting
The reporting included:
 Data and Information Analysis; and
 Report Preparation.


The data obtained from the desk and field studies were analyzed and have been presented in
this revised draft ESIA Report, an outline of which is provided hereunder.


1. Executive summary;
2. Introduction;
3. Policy, Legal and Regulatory Framework;
4. Description of the Priority Works Project (PWP);


Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP                                                   10
5. The Existing Asafo Simplified Sewerage System (ASSS) and Challenges;
6. Analysis of Alternatives;
7. Environmental and Social Baseline Information;
8. Stakeholder Consultations and Public Participation;
9. Environmental and Social Impact and Risks Identification and Significance;
10. Proposed Enhancement and Mitigation Measures;
11. Provisional Environmental and Social Management and Monitoring Plan including
    Grievance Redress Mechanism (GRM), Code of Conduct for Construction Companies and
    Construction Workers and Plan for Training of Key Stakeholders on the ESIA
    Implementation;
12. Decommissioning Plan; and
13. Conclusion.

2.0      POLICIES, LEGAL, REGULATORY AND INSTITUTIONAL FRAMEWORK
         RELEVANT TO THE PROPOSED SUBPROJECT


Under the provisions of the Ghana Environmental Assessment Regulations 1999 (LI 1652) the
proposed subproject is classified under projects for which an EIA is required. The EPA Act,
1994 (Act 490) established the Agency and entrusted it with the responsibility of ensuring
compliance with EIA process and procedures in the planning and execution of development
projects.


The subproject is World Bank funded and therefore it has to also conform to the World Bank’s
OP 4.01. The objective of the laws and policies is to prevent or at least minimize the
environment and socioeconomic-cultural risks and impacts while increasing the environmental
benefits as well as socio-economic benefits of such subprojects. Specific policies, legal and
institutional frameworks considered relevant to the proposed subproject are discussed in this
section.


2.1      Relevant National Policies


The national policies identified to be relevant or applicable to the proposed subproject are
presented in this section in Table 1 and include:
 The Coordinated Program of Economic and Social Development Policies (CPESDP) -
      Agenda for Jobs: Creating Prosperity and Equal Opportunity for All 2017-2024, October
      2017
 National Environmental Policy (NEP), 2014;
 Environmental Sanitation Policy (ESP), 2010;
 National Land Policy (NLP), 1999;
 National Water Policy (NWP), 2007;
 Riparian Buffer Zone Policy (RBZP), 2013;
 National Health Policy (NHP), 2020;
 National Workplace HIV/ AIDS Policy, 2012;

 Ghana National Climate Change Policy (GNCCP), 2013; and

        o    Any other cost that is necessary for the compulsory acquisition; and

2.2.1 Ghana Standards on Environmental Quality
The Ghana Standards Authority (GSA) has in collaboration with the EPA and through various
National Technical Committees issued Ghana Standard (GS) requirements for Noise Control
and Measurements, and Air Quality, and Requirements for Effluent Discharge (General
Industry) as follows:
1. GS 1236:2019- Environment and Health Protection – Requirements for Ambient Air
      Quality and Point Source/ Stack Emissions;
2. GS 1222:2018- Health Protection – Requirements for Ambient Noise Control; and
3. GS 1212:2019- Environmental Protection Requirements for Effluent Discharge (General
      Industry).


2.2.1.1 GS 1236:2019

Table 3.

2.3      Relevant International Safeguard Policies/ Guidelines


The relevant international policies/guidelines applicable to the proposed subproject are
presented in this section, and these include:
 World Bank Operational Policies (OPs); and
 World Bank Group EHS Guidelines.


2.3.1 World Bank Operational Policies
There are a number of OPs established by the WB that the GAMA Project will have to meet
throughout the project life-cycle. However, those that are triggered by the proposed subproject
are the Environmental Assessment Policy (O.P. 4.01), Natural Habitats Policy (O.P. 4.04),
Physical Cultural Resources Policy (O.P. 4.11) and Involuntary Resettlement Policy (O.P.
4.12) which are summarized in Table 6.

2.3.2 WBG EHS Guidelines
The proposed subproject shall be carried out in accordance with the provisions of the ESIA and
any other applicable policies of the Government of Ghana and the World Bank including WBG
General EHS Guidelines as well as Industry Sector Guidelines for water and sanitation. In case
there is a difference between the World Bank and the GoG requirements, the World Bank
requirements take precedence.


The World Bank Group (WBG) Environmental, Health, and Safety (EHS) Guidelines (General
EHS Guidelines, April 30, 2007) are technical reference documents with general and industry-
specific examples of Good International Industry Practice (GIIP). The industry sector EHS
guidelines are designed to be used together with the General EHS Guidelines document. The
applicable EHS guidelines include:


Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP                                                                        31
 General EHS Guidelines; and
 EHS Guidelines for Construction Materials Extraction.


2.3.2.1 General EHS Guidelines
The General EHS Guidelines (April 30, 2007) provides guidance to users on common EHS
issues potentially applicable to all industry sectors. The general guidelines provide GIIP advice
relating to the following elements to protect human health and the environment:


Environmental
i. Air Emissions and Ambient Air Quality;
ii. Wastewater and Ambient Water Quality;
iii. Water Conservation;
iv. Hazardous Materials Management;
v. Waste Management;
vi. Noise; and
vii. Contaminated Land.


Occupational Health and Safety
i. General Facility Design and Operation;
ii. Communication and Training;
iii. Hazards- Physical, Chemical, Biological and Radiological;
iv. PPE Usage; and
v. Monitoring.


Community Health and Safety
i. Water Quality and Availability;
ii. Structural Safety of Project Infrastructure;
iii. Life and Fire Safety;
iv. Traffic Safety; and
v. Emergency Preparedness and Response.


The WBG EHS General Guidelines are relevant to the proposed subproject as they provide
internationally accepted GIIP for relevant EHS issues. .



Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP                                                   32
2.3.2.2 EHS Guidelines for Construction Materials Extraction
The EHS Guidelines for Construction Materials Extraction (April 30, 2007) include
information relevant to construction materials extraction activities such as aggregates,
limestone, slates, sand, gravel, clay, gypsum, feldspar, silica sands, and quartzite, as well as to
the extraction of dimension stone. It addresses stand-alone projects and extraction activities
supporting construction, civil works, and cement projects. Although the construction materials
extraction guidelines emphasize major and complex extraction schemes, the concepts are also
applicable to small operations.

  10    Traditional Authority
        In the 1992 Constitution, chieftaincy together with its traditional         The Nhyiaeso Chief confirms making
        councils is guaranteed and protected as an important institution in the     available the buffer zone between the
        country.    In Ghana, land is owned predominantly by customary              Subin River and the sewage treatment
        authorities (stools, skins, clans and families). Together they own about    ponds to the squatter and will be of
        78% of all lands while the State owns about 20% with the remaining          great influence to help relocate the
        2% owned by the state and customary authorities in a form of                squatters   for    the     subproject
        partnership (split ownership), (Larbi W O, 2008). Article 267 (1) of the    implementation
        1992 Constitution avers that all stool lands in the country shall vest in
        the appropriate stool on behalf of, and in trust of the subjects of the
        stool in accordance with customary law and usage. All revenue from
        stool lands are collected and disbursed by the Office of the
        Administrator of Stool Lands (OASL).


        Customary land represents all the different categories of rights and
        interests held within traditional systems and which include stool lands,
        skin lands, clan lands, and family lands. Any decision taken by the
        custodian that affects rights and interests in the land, especially
        disposition of any portion of the communal land to non-members of the
        land holding community, require the concurrence of the principal
        elders. The State exerts considerable control over the administration of
        customary lands. All grants of stool land to non-subjects of the stool
        require the concurrence of the Lands Commission to be valid. No
        freeholds can be granted out of stool lands. Foreigners cannot own
        more than 50-year leases in stool and state lands (Article 267(5) of the
        1992 Constitution).



Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP                                                                    36
 No.    Policy and Key Compliance Requirements                                    Applicability to Proposed Subproject


        The Kumasi Traditional Council (KTC) was established under section

        Nkosuo. The Asantehene serves as the President of KTC with the
        Bantamahene who is the Krontihene of Kumasi as the as the Vice-
        President.


2.5      International Conventions and Requirements


2.5.1 United Nations Sustainable Development Goals
The Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) are global problems defined into 17 goals with
169 specific targets. These goals recognize that ending poverty must go hand-in-hand with
strategies that build economic growth and address a range of social needs including education,
health, social protection, and job opportunities while tackling climate change and
environmental protection. They serve as a guidebook to drive the country’s specific focus
areas, policies, regulations, financing, stimulus programs, and many other activities aimed to
achieve sustainable development. The proposed ASSSPWP will develop sustainable sewerage
discharge infrastructure by ensuring that all related infrastructure such as the site office is
constructed, operated, maintained and decommissioned in a manner that ensures economic and
financial, social, environmental (including climate resilience), and institutional sustainability
over the entire life cycle of the subproject. The key SDGs that the proposed ASSSPWP will
pay attention to in the process of construction, operation and maintenance phase include Goal
3- ensure a healthy working environment by preventing the spread of infectious diseases such
as HIV/AIDS, Covid 19 and others, Goal 5- to give equal employment opportunity for both
men and women, Goal 6- to ensure availability and sustainable management of water and
sanitation for all, Goal 8- to ensure the employment of youth, consultants and local artisans
such as plumbers, electricians and many others, Goal 13- to build a climate change resilient
infrastructure and Goal 15- ensure environmental sustainability in the process of construction.




Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP                                                                 37
2.5.2 The Fundamental Conventions on Occupatiuonal Safety and Health (OSH): ILO
         Convention No. 155 (1981) on OSH and 187 (2006) on Promotional Framework
         for OSH
These Conventions are fundamental rights for a safe and healthy working environment. They
express a renewed collective commitment to the protection of life and health at work. The core
principles of Conventions Nos. 155 and 187 are fully complementary, and together they
constitute a blueprint for progressive and sustained improvements towards the provision of safe
and healthy working environments. Conventions Nos. 155 and 187 serve as the basis for
additional occupational safety and health measures provided in other specific OSH instruments
e.g., Convention No. 148 i.e., Working Environment (Air Pollution, Noise and Vibration).
Additionally, to progressively achieve a safe and healthy working environment, Members need
to take into account the principles set out in the ILO instruments relevant to the promotional
framework for OSH.


2.5.2 Convention Concerning the Protection of Workers Against Occupational Hazards
         in the Working Environment due to Air Pollution, Noise, and Vibration, 1977
         (ILO Convention No. 148)
The Convention encourages employers that in consultation with their workers, they should
understand project hazards related to air pollution, noise pollution, and vibrations. Under the
Convention No 148, national laws or regulations shall prescribe that measures be taken for the
prevention and control of, and protection against, occupational hazards in the working
environment due to air pollution, noise and vibration.; and provisions concerning the practical
implementation of the measures so prescribed may be adopted through technical standards,
codes of practice and other appropriate methods.


2.5.3 The United Nations Convention of the Child
The United Nations Convention of the Child is an important agreement by countries who have
promised to protect children’s rights. It defines a child as any person under the age of 18 years.
The Convention explains who children are, all their rights, and the responsibilities of
governments. All the rights are connected, they are all equally important and they cannot be
taken away from children. All children have all these rights, no matter who they are, where
they live, what language they speak, what their religion is, what they think, what they look like,
if they are a boy or girl, if they have a disability, if they are rich or poor, and no matter who



Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP                                                    38
their parents or families are or what their parents or families believe or do. No child should be
treated unfairly for any reason.


Children have the right to the best health care possible, clean water to drink, healthy food and
a clean and safe environment to live in. All adults and children should have information about
how to stay safe and healthy. The presence of children in the vicinity of the sewage treatment
plant at Abinkyi area does not support this convention and exposes them to fecal matter
pollution, risk of falling into the treatment ponds, risks of snake bites and diseases e.g., malaria,
diarrhea, typhoid, cholera etc.


2.5.4 GHANA’s Updated Nationally Determined Contributions (NDCs): 2020 – 2030
Ghana’s updated NDC is a strategy developed to build a resilient society that can adequately
withstand the impacts of climate change and contribute to mitigating global emissions.


The update covers 19 policy areas and translates into 13 adaptation and 34 mitigation programs
of action. The 47 climate actions together are expected to build the resilience of over 38 million
people, generate absolute greenhouse gas emission reductions of 64 MtCO2e, create over one
million jobs, avoid 2,900 deaths due to improved air quality by 2030.


For the 34 mitigation measures, Ghana aims to implement nine unconditional programs of
action that would result in 8.5 MtCO2e GHG reductions by 2025 and a further 24.6 MtCO2e
by 2030 compared to the 2020-2030 cumulative emissions in a baseline scenario.


The proposed ASSSPWP when completed will guard against flooding of the treatment plant
area and the discharge of raw sewage into the Subin River to contribute to the achievement of
the country’s NDC.




Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP                                                       39
3.0      DESCRIPTION OF THE PROPOSED ASSSPWP


3.1      Subproject Location


The proposed PWP of the ASSS is a subproject of the GAMA-SWP also referred to as GKMA-
SWP and it will be carried out within the boundaries of the existing sewerage network at Asafo
and the treatment plant site at Abinkyi, all in Kumasi. The treatment plant site is near the
Ghana Grid Company (GRIDCo) Substation within the Kumasi Metropolis. The treatment
plant site is accessible by an untarred road from Asafo or Adum (Central Business District of
Kumasi) through Abinkyi market/lorry station. The site can also be accessed via a footpath
connecting an access route that links the road from Ridge/ Georgia Hotel area (i.e., Georgia
Kusi Avenue).


3.2      Components of the ASSSPWP


The proposed priority activities will involve the following:


1. Sewers: The sections of the existing sewer network (totaling about 1.1 km of sewer length)
      will be replaced (see Figure 1).
2. Manholes/ Chambers: The priority works envisaged cover a total of 10 new, 57 existing
      but to be totally demolished and constructed anew and 78 existing manholes and chambers
      to be cleaned for rehabilitation (see Figure 2).
3. Treatment Plant: The activities at the Treatment plant site will include replacement and
      rehabilitation of Screening Chamber, Anaerobic Pond 1, Anaerobic Pond 2, Facultative
      Pond, Maturation Pond and construction of Sludge Drying Bed and Perimeter Fencing. It
      will also involve the realignment of the “Gee Stream” nearby the treatment plant.


Details of the activities are presented in Tables 8, 9, 10 and 11.


3.2.1 Sewers
The sections of the existing sewer network (totaling about 1. km of sewer length) to be
considered are presented in Figure 1 and the details presented in Table 8.

3.3      Overview of Subproject Activities


Each of the subproject components will comprise of preconstruction, construction, operations
and maintenance and decommissioning phases.


3.3.1 Subproject Preconstruction Phase
The description of the specific preconstruction phase activities to be undertaken for each of
the components is presented below.


Sewers and Manholes
 Surveying to identify the sewer network to be rehabilitated;
 Assessing the methods, taking inventory of what to be done and designing; and
 Clearing of the RoWs including relocation/resettlement of PAPs following compensation
      payment to make way for construction and related issues.


Treatment Plant
 Surveying to identify the components to be replaced at the treatment plant site including
      screens in the screen chamber;
 Assessing the methods, taking inventory of what has to be done and designing the facility
      to include a new maturation pond, a sludge drying bed, security fencing, lightning and flood
      protection of the site; and
 Clearing of the RoWs and treatment plant site including relocation/resettlement of PAPs
      following compensation payment to make way for construction and related issues.


3.3.2 Subproject Construction Phase
The description of the specific construction phase activities to be undertaken for each of the
components is presented below.



Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP                                                                   46
Sewers and Manholes
 Trenching and excavation of the sewer pipelines connecting the manholes to expose the
    damaged sections;
 Cutting of the damaged sewer pipes to be replaced with new ones. Sewer pipelines will be
    allowed to drain its contents in a chamber and sanitized before disposal for recycling;
 Opening of manholes/ chambers to reconstruct walls and demolition of damaged ones to
    reconstruct new ones either in concrete or with sandcrete blocks; and
 Backfilling of excavated areas and trenches.


The sewer pipeline rehabilitation will occur along 4 main routes in the Asafo community
namely (i) KsTU – OA Bus Terminal Route, (ii) Labor Roundabout Route, (iii) Asafo Market/
Lorry Stations – Pentecost Church Route, and (iv) Abinkyi – VIP Washing Bay Route.


Treatment Plant
 Construction of one new maturation pond to ensure coliforms meet the GS values and
    sludge drying bed to receive the wet sludge evacuated from a pond;
 Pumping of the content of a pond into the subsequent one for work to be done on it. Thus,
    no wet sludge or wastewater will be evacuated from the site to a disposal facility. Also, all
    aquatic organisms especially fish and reptiles inhabiting the ponds will be evacuated by
    officials of the Kumasi Zoological Gardens (they are officers of the Wildlife Division of
    the Forestry Commission);
 Construction of perimeter fence and storm drain around the treatment plant site, and
    provision of lights; and
 Diversion of the Gee stream upstream of the treatment ponds and constructing of box drain
    to the Subin River and construction of a levee between the Subin River and the treatment
    ponds for flood protection and management.


3.3.3 Subproject Operations and Maintenance Phase
The sewer network will be utilized in the long term while the treatment ponds will be operated
in the short to medium term pending availability of funds to reconstruct a new sewage treatment
plant.




Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP                                                   47
For the sewers, the operation and maintenance phase activities will include rodding, changing
of damaged pipelines by trenching and backfilling/ reinstatement and ensuring that all the
sewage flows into the treatment plant.


For the treatment plant, the ponds may be operated for short period to pave way for a more
robust and compact sewage treatment plant to be installed.


3.3.4 Subproject Decommissioning Phase
The sewer network will not be decommissioned but rehabilitated as a when damages occur.


The treatment ponds however, may be decommissioned pending availability of funds and in its
stead a new sewage treatment plant will be constructed.


3.4      Materials and Equipment


The materials required for the subproject include building materials such as cement, sand,
gravel, iron rods, water, pipe and admixtures. Other accessories will include barbed wire, wire
mesh, balustrade, etc. The construction equipment to be used includes excavators, backhoes,
shovels/ pick axes, concrete mixers, dump trucks, concrete dispensing trucks, water pumps etc.


3.5      Manpower


During the preconstruction phase, about 15 persons is estimated to be engaged for surveying,
designing and assessments of the PWP.


About 50 - 75 persons are estimated to be engaged during the construction phase and the types
of labor required will include unskilled labor, drivers, masons, carpenters, plumbers,
electricians, mechanics, plant operators, engineers, and administrators. Labor influx and
associated risks such as social conflict, Gender Based Violence (GBV) and associated
increased transmission of Sexually Transmitted Diseases (STDs) including HIV/AIDS may
occur. However, adequate measures have been provided in this ESIA report to ensure that the
risks associated with labor influx are minimized. Some of the management measures will
include ensuring that workers/laborers are sourced mostly from the Asafo and Abinkyi
communities. Thus, no camps will be established to house workers during construction.


Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP                                                 48
No more than 10 engineers and technicians are estimated to be involved in managing the sewer
network and the treatment plant site during the operation & maintenance phase of the ASSS.


3.6      Subproject Development and Management


Colan Consult Limited on behalf of the MSWR and KMA will supervise the construction of
the ASSSPWP.


KMA or its assigns will carry out the management of the rehabilitated ASSS and shall be
directly responsible for the operation and maintenance of the system.




Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP                                              49
4.0      THE EXISTING ASSS AND THE CHALLENGES


This chapter presents the existing situation of the ASSS.


4.1      Existing Sewerage Service Area


The Asafo Sewerage System is a simplified sewerage scheme constructed as a pilot project in
1994 and has been in operation since. The system operates purely by gravity and comprises a
network of PVC pipes (sewers) and masonry chambers/manholes which are installed at
connection points, junctions and at intervals of between 25m to 50m.


The sewerage system which was initially designed for the Asafo community comprising then
320 tenement houses consisting of 4, 000 households and a population 20,000 users now serves
about 50,000 people, extended to Asafo Lorry Park, four (4) schools, public toilets, and parts
of other nearby communities such as Fanti New Town, the Kumasi Technical University, OA
Travel & Tour Bus Terminal, the Lancaster Kumasi Hotel and the Kumasi Nurses and
Midwifery Training College are connected into the treatment plant located in Abinkyi
Community about 1km south of the Asafo community.


The existing ASSS currently services most parts of the Asafo Community and some institutions
in Fante New Town, all suburbs of Kumasi as shown in Figure 3. The land area of the serviced
part of Asafo is less than 1km2.




Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP                                                50
Figure 3:         Coverage of Existing Sewerage System Relative to KMA Boundary




Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP                                       51
4.2      Existing Sewer Network and Functionality


The ASSS operates purely by gravity and comprises a network of PVC (Class B) pipes
(sewers) and masonry chambers/manholes, which are installed at connection points,
junctions and at intervals of between 25m to 50m. The sewers are laid in lanes at the
backyard of buildings/parcels of land and along roads. Users connect to the sewers through
100mm pipelines from their Water Closet toilets to either 100mm sewers in a majority of
the sections or in few instances, connect to 150mm sewers. Most connections are by 135o
Tees (Saddles) with a few connecting to manholes or chambers. The sewer network
collects and conveys mainly blackwater from residential users in the Asafo community.
Some institutional and commercial/public users discharge both black and grey water to the
sewer network, including Lancaster Kumasi City Hotel (LKCH), OA Bus Terminal Public
Toilet, Two (2) Public Toilets in the woodcraft industrial enclave in Fante New Town,
KATH Nursing and Midwifery College and hotel facilities connected to the system within
Asafo.


The existing sewer network excluding the main sewers from KATH and Lancaster Kumasi
City Hotel is approximately 8km comprising uPVC Class B pipes of 100mm, 150mm, 200mm,
250mm and 300mm, internal diameters as shown in Table 12.


Table 12:         Existing Sewer Network
 Size of Sewer                     Length (m)        Sewer Category
 100mm                             3,446             Lateral
 150mm                             1,751             Laterals and sub-main sewer
 200mm                             642               Main sewer
 250mm                             1,115             Main sewer
 300mm                             993               Trunk sewer


The upper sections of the sewer network especially the KsTU/ OA Bus Terminal i.e., along the
OA Street at Fante Newtown and part of the Asafo Community i.e., east of the Asafo Awere
Street are mostly intact (100mm) and convey sewage freely to the lower sections around the
Asafo Market Area (200mm and 250mm) which discharge to the trunk sewers that convey
sewage to the treatment plant (300mm). The flows in the main sewers in the lower reaches of
the network are impeded by silt and other floatable objects (see Plate 1). Majority (over 90%)
of the sewage flow from connecting facilities in Asafo community and Fante New Town is



Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP                                                  52
discharged into the Subin River from a manhole located on the trunk sewer (see Plate 2), which
was intentionally broken to prevent flooding of the system upstream.




Plate 1: Manhole on the Trunk Sewer Along the Abinkyi Stretch Filled with Refuse




Plate 2: Sewage Discharge into Open Storm Drain (Subin River) from Choked and Damaged Manhole




Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP                                                     53
All sewers in the Asafo sewer network are buried at an average depth of 1m and at slopes
ranging from 0.1% to 0.4% (0.001 to 0.0044) with an average of 0.8% (0.008) along
backyard lanes or access and arterial roads based on field measurements. Reportedly, the
sewers were laid at slopes of 1 in 100 (0.01) and 1 in 167 (0.006).


Majority of the manholes and inspection chambers are in fairly good condition, but a
significant number of manholes (80No.) are aged and structurally weak. 5No. of the
manholes are completely broken and in a state of disrepair. Only one (1) is in very sound
structural condition. Majority of the manholes have broken ladders, scoured walls and
deteriorated grout bed or benching. Table 13 provides a summary of functional conditions
of the chambers and manholes.

 Choked/ Blocked                23
