 Free flowing                   214
 Overflowing                    1
 Ponded                         5



3.3      Existing Treatment Plant


The existing treatment plant is located at Abinkyi, a commercial area in Kumasi, about 1km
south of the Asafo community and to the south-west of the Subin Sub-metro of KMA. The
plant is a waste stabilization pond system comprising 2 Anaerobic, 1 Facultative and 2
Maturation ponds. One of the anaerobic ponds is filled with sludge and it is dysfunctional,
while the other ponds are functional.


The treatment plant site is geographically located around 6o40’21.75”N and 1o36’48.60”W and
bounded by the Subin River to the east, the Gee stream to the west, the Abinkyi market to the
North, Best Concrete Products Ltd. to the southwest and temporary Mechanic Shop to the south
east. Inhabiting the buffer zone between the treatment plant site and the Subin River is a
community of squatters and the structures are mainly made up of temporal structures made of
wood and Aluzinc roofing sheets. There are GRIDCo high tension overhead power lines



Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP                                                 54
traversing the treatment plant site with one of the pylons sited between Anaerobic Pond 2 and
Maturation Pond 2.


The site acquired by KMA is 17.98 acres and the treatment plant occupies about 9.78 acres
(54% of the site). The remainder of the land is currently occupied by squatter - auto-mobile
mechanic shops, squatter settlement and for commercial activities (see Annex 2 for the Site
Plan).


The treatment plant is a Waste Stabilization Pond which operates purely on natural biological
degradation processes influenced by solar, microorganism and algae. It is an energy efficient
technology with little operation and maintenance requirements. Sewage is treated in a series
of ponds (anaerobic, facultative and maturation ponds) and the treated wastewater (effluent)
discharged into the nearby Subin River.


The plant receives low flow due to flow diversions from the trunk sewer and KATH sewers.
Predominantly, current flow to the treatment plant is from Lancaster Kumasi City Hotel.


The embankments of the ponds are structurally stable, but some concrete walls of the inter-
connecting chambers are scoured. The portions of the initially provided fencing are largely
damaged or non-existent. There is no operational office structure at the plant site. The access
road to the site is motorable but requires upgrading. The site including access is heavily
encroached by auto-mechanics shops and use of the open spaces for truck parking and storage
of coconut for resale to vendors.


The treatment plant operates fully as a gravity system which consists of the following
components:


 1No. Screen Chamber.
 1No. Distribution Chamber.
 2No. Anaerobic Ponds installed in parallel.
 1No. Facultative Pond.
 2No. Maturation Pond.


The layout of the treatment plant is presented in Figures 4 and 5.


Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP                                                 55
Figure 4:         Layout of Treatment Plant




Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP   56
Figure 5:         The Sewage Treatment Plant Location at Abinkyi


Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP                        57
Screening Chamber


The screening chamber is the first component of the treatment plant. It is a 2.5m x 2.1m
uncovered masonry chamber with a depth of 1.43m equipped with a longitudinally framed
inclined screen with 20mm iron bars with spacing of 25mm as shown in Plate 3. The screen
is rusty and some bars are missing.




Plate 3: The Screening/ Degrit Chamber


Distribution Chamber


A distribution chamber receives sewage from the screening chamber and distributes to 2No.
anaerobic ponds via 300mm and 250mm uPVC pipes. The chamber also has a connection to a
by-pass chamber with a 300mm uPVC pipe.


Plate 4 is the sewage distribution chamber showing the low flow situation into the treatment
ponds.



Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP                                               58
Plate 4: The Distribution Chamber Showing the Low Flow to the Treatment Plant




Anaerobic Ponds


The plant has 2No. anaerobic ponds constructed to operate in parallel. The second pond was
constructed in 2004 with the aim of expanding the capacity of the plant. Treatment in these
ponds is expected to be anaerobic digestion of the organic components of the sewage. The first
anaerobic pond (ANP1) is an earth-lined rectangular shaped, trapezoidal section excavated
pond with dimensions of 66m x 35m and the second pond (ANP2) has a measurement of 70m
x 16m and approximate depth of 3m. The inlet pipe to ANP1 is 300mm uPVC while the inlet
pipe of ANP2 is 250mm. Both ponds have two outlet chambers and two outlet pipes of 300mm
and 250mm, respectively. ANP1 is dysfunctional and full of consolidated sludge and weeds



Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP                                                59
(see Plate 5) while ANP2 which receives flow from the distribution chamber also has
considerable sludge build up.




Plate 5: The Dysfunctional First Anaerobic Pond of the Treatment Plant


The 2No. connecting chambers for ANP1 are constructed with concrete of 200mm wall
thickness and have dimensions of 1.6m x 1.6m x 1.35m (length x width x depth). The walls of
the connecting chambers, particularly internal, are scoured with exposed reinforcement steel
bars as shown in Plate 6.




Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP                                              60
Plate 6: Anaerobic Ponds and Connecting Chambers ( ANP1 – Top left, ANP2 – Top Right, Connecting
         Chamber to ANP1 - Bottom Left, and ANP2 - Bottom Right




Facultative Pond


The plant has 1No. facultative pond that connects to the anaerobic ponds via 2No. 300mm
pipes. It has dimensions of 100m x 40m and approximate depth of 1.5m. The pond currently
receives flow from only ANP2 for treatment by both anaerobic and aerobic processes to further
reduce the organic matter (reduce BOD). Pathogen removal is also expected to take place in
this pond. The pond has 3No. connecting/ discharge chambers; 2No. connecting to the
Maturation Pond and 1No. by-pass to final effluent chamber (see Plate 7).




Plate 7: Facultative Pond with a Connecting Discharge Chamber


Maturation Ponds




Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP                                                        61
The treatment plant has 2No. maturation ponds that connect to the facultative pond. The two
ponds are located parallel to each other but connected in series to improve final effluent quality.
Treatment in the ponds is by aerobic processes and mainly for polishing of effluent by removal
of pathogens and nutrients. The ponds have dimensions of 100m x 40m and approximate depth
of 1m. They are connected by one connecting chamber and pipe diameter of 300mm. The
capacities of the ponds are currently reduced by weeds as shown in Plate 8.




Plate 8: Maturation Ponds and Effluent Chamber




Effluent Chamber and Final Discharge


A concrete chamber connects to a by-pass pipe from the facultative pond and effluent pipe
from the second maturation pond to receive effluent from the treatment plant for final
discharge. This chamber links to a discharge chamber for final effluent discharge to the Subin
River. The chambers were found to be structurally sound however, the internal walls of the
final discharge chamber are scoured (see Plate 9).




Plate 9: Effluent Chamber (Left) and Discharge Chamber (Right)


Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP                                                     62
4.4       Management Arrangement


The system is owned by KMA and the Waste Management Department (WMD) is responsible
for overall management and oversight functions. The system is operated and managed by
Environmental Engineering Limited (EEL), a private company incorporated in Ghana, under a
Franchise Agreement. The Operator is responsible for daily operation, routine maintenance
and collection of revenue. Major plant maintenance is the responsibility of KMA.


4.5       Organization of the System Operator


The Operator has its head office in North Kaneshie in Accra. In Kumasi, EEL operates from a
temporary structure (moveable kiosk) in Asafo near the Chief’s Palace. The operation office
is headed by a System Manager with one Assistant. The Operator reportedly has 1 Revenue
Collector, 4 laborers at the treatment plant site, 2 casual plumbers, and 1 security guard as
operational and maintenance staff.


4.6       Operation and Maintenance


Operation and maintenance activities normally carried out on the system include minor repair
of pipes, replacement of concrete cover slabs for manholes and chambers, rodding of sewers
and bush clearing at the treatment plant site. Reportedly, the anaerobic ponds have been
desludged by KMA only once in 2004, since commissioning.


Major bottlenecks to operation and maintenance of the existing system include:
 Encroachment on the sewer lines and chambers (see Plate 10);
 Frequent blockage in the main sewer lines at the downstream sections of the network;
 Sludge accumulation in chambers;
 Siltation of sewers;
 Solid waste disposal in chambers (see Plate 11); and
 Lack of sufficient revenue due to low service charges and defaults as well as transparency
      in accounting for the little revenue collected.




Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP                                               63
The increased demand in the discharge of waste through the existing sewer network is largely
limited since some of the chambers are broken and some of the channels have been blocked
and encroached upon. This has compelled majority of the inhabitants to resort to discharge of
untreated wastewater directly into the Subin River.


The ASSS is therefore faced with a number of Environmental and Social (E&S) challenges
including:
 discharge of effluent not meeting the required standards,
 diversion/ discharge of untreated wastewater into drains,
 encroachment of portions of the sewer lines and the site for sewage treatment by mechanics,
     trucks drivers and bulk coconut sellers,
 encroachment of the buffer zone between the Subin River and the treatment site for use as
     residences (mostly wooden structures) and
 ease of access to the site due to lack of security and fence just to mention a few.


The challenges and bottlenecks of the treatment plant have arisen as a result of the lack of
proper maintenance mainly due to lack of adequate financing for maintenance operations of
which some are listed hereunder:
 Routine flushing or rodding of sewers;
 Timely repair of sewer and manhole/chambers to ensure integrity of the system;
 Desludging and removal of floatable solids at the screen chamber at the treatment plant;
 Periodic desludging of ponds;
 Administration of a complaints register and timely response to customer complaints.


Currently, the service charges for the ASSS as presented in Table 14, which was last reviewed
in 2018 by KMA has been found to be woefully inadequate and cannot sustain the maintenance
of the sewerage system.

 SN     Description of Structure/User                    Service Charge/ month in GHS

The poor and deteriorating state of the sewage system has resulted in the need for major
rehabilitation of the existing treatment ponds by GKMA in order to ensure continuity of waste
water treatment prior to discharge hence the PWP.

5.0      ANALYSIS OF ALTERNATIVES


This subproject is intended to rehabilitate the existing Asafo Sewerage Treatment System in
order to improve on the existing treatment plant and its efficiency, as well as strengthening
critical sections of the existing sewer network that are defective. No new treatment options
were considered due to budgeting constraints. The alternative analysis centers on the
following:
 Site Selection Option;
 Choice of Pipe Material;
 Routing of pipelines;
 Methods of laying pipes across roads;
 Gee Stream Diversion and Flood Management Options; and
 Do nothing scenario.


5.1      Site Selection Option


Generally, the sites to be utilized for construction/ rehabilitation of the subproject facilities
(sewers and treatment plant) are located in already acquired right of ways for utilities and public
accesses. The sewage treatment site is already in use for the purpose. For the sewers, the
existing right of ways will be maintained. Thus, maintaining the current locations for the
proposed subproject will be more prudent to avoid excessive payment of resettlement/
compensation amounts for acquisition of new land/ right of ways. Again, acquiring new sites/
right of ways will also involve vegetation clearing compared with using the existing sites.


5.2      Choice of Pipe Material


High Density Polyethylene (HDPE) or Unplasticized Poly vinyl chloride (uPVC) may be used
for the sewer lines. These pipes are appropriate for the proposed subproject. PVC pipes were
utilized for the existing sewer network. The pipe types will not impact on treated sewage
quality. No Asbestos Concrete (AC) pipes will be used. Table 15 presents a comparison of the
pipe materials. The main difference between PVC and uPVC is that PVC has Bisphenol A
(BPA) and phthalates. These two are the kind of plasticizers in PVC. uPVC is stronger and is
known as a rigid plastic.




Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP                                                     67
Table 15:           Comparison of uPVC/PVC and HDPE Pipes
                      HDPE Pipes                                         uPVC/PVC Pipes
 Can be obtained locally for the subproject but up to   Can be obtained locally for the subproject but up to
 1000mm diameter                                        500mm diameter
 Material technically durable and robust                Material technically durable
 Need to be buried to ensure safety and security        Need to be buried in the ground to ensure safety and
                                                        security
 Have excellent corrosion/rusting resistance            Resistant to wear and corrosion/rust. No coatings or
 properties. No need for internal or external           lining needed
 protection
 Longer life span                                       Long life span
 Resistant to scum formation                            Resistant to scum formation
 No influence on wastewater quality                     No influence on wastewater quality
 No immediate direct environmental impact               No immediate direct environmental impact
 Can withstand higher pressure. Frictional losses are   Can withstand high pressure
 minimal or negligible
 Small broken section can easily be repaired. Has       Small broken section may render longer pipeline
 excellent welding properties                           section replacement or sections can be replaced
 Minimum maintenance and economical                     Minimum maintenance and economical
 Ideal for saline and aggressive soils                  Ideal for saline and aggressive soils
 Good elasticity and flexible                           Limited elasticity and not flexible



It is recommended to use both uPVC and HDPE for the sewers.


5.4      Routing of Sewer Pipe


The proposed sewer pipes along road reservation would pose minimal impact on the
environment. This will also be in line with provisions in the Road Reservation Management
Manual for works undertaken within the road reservation. Any other alternative routes could
disturb the Asafo / Fante Newtown planning. The sewer pipes would be completely buried in
the ground as was done previously compared with leaving them on the surface.


5.5      Method of Laying Pipes Across Roads


Two methods considered are:
 Direct cutting across road to be followed by reinstatement; and


Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP                                                                    68
 Thrust boring.


Option 1– Direct Cutting Across Road
The roads in the subproject area would be cut across using an excavator or manual digging to
enable the pipes to be laid across. Reinstatement of the damaged road sections would be
undertaken after pipe laying.


The advantage of this method is that it is much easier to carry out, especially for shallow
installations of pipelines. The main disadvantage is the temporary obstruction to traffic along
such sections of the road, and life span of road may be adversely affected if proper
reinstatement is not done.


Option 2– Thrust Boring
This method involves providing access in the ground for the water pipes under the roads
without destroying the road surface. It involves the use of horizontal thrust boring machines
to drive holes under the road for the pipes.


The advantages of this method include:
 Less traffic disruption;
 Low impact on surroundings, that is, environmentally friendly;
 No reinstatement of road required, i.e. saving cost of reinstatement; and
 Deeper installation is possible.


The disadvantages include:
 The cost of importing and use of thrust borers for few jobs may not be cost-effective; and
 Pipes not easily accessible for maintenance purposes.


Thus, for asphalted and major roads e.g., Otumfour Osei-Tutu Boulevard, thrust boring will be
used while direct cutting will be used for the untarred/ minor roads e.g., Abinkyi market road
to the treatment plant site.


5.6      Gee Stream Diversion and Flood Management Option




Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP                                                 69
It is proposed to divert the course of the Gee stream to prevent the perennial flooding of the
treatment ponds. A hydrological assessment has been carried out as part of the technical
assessment to establish the best options to prevent flooding of the sewage treatment plant site.
The study tested three scenarios to identify the best option with low-risk flood vulnerability
within the treatment site. In the natural regime, the Gee River flows along the boundary and
cuts through the middle of the treatment site before joining the Subin River just below the
tributary (see Figure 6). As illustrated in Figure 6, all the scenarios are related to the point
where the Gee River joins the Subin River:
 Scenario-1: Changing the direction of the Gee River to join the Subin River from the upper
    section of the treatment site.
 Scenario-2:Changing the angle at which the Gee River flows into the Subin downstream.
 Scenario-3: combining scenario-1 and the natural regime such that 50% of the flow in the
    Gee River joins the Subin River upstream of the treatment site and the remaining 50% flow
    through the natural regime


In each case, an approximate excavated depth of 1.0m and 1.5m was considered from the point
of diversion of the Gee River to the Subin River.




Figure 6:         Natural River Network and Scenarios Considered in the Study




Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP                                                  70
Scenario 1 has been found to be the preferred option as it presents a solution to the flooding of
the treatment plant site, and it also affords in the design an added advantage of using the original
channel to construct a u-drain around the treatment plant site to receive runoff into the Subin
River.


The design has provided a levee along the Subin River as an additional flood mitigation
measure (see Annex 5).


5.7      Do Nothing Scenario


This will mean maintaining the status quo, that is no rehabilitation and no expansion of the
ASSS. The existing channelling of some sewage into primary drains/ stream courses will
persist. Thus, the discharge of untreated wastewater into municipal drains will continue.
Artisans/ mechanics, truck drivers and squatters may take over the entire treatment plant site.
Proliferation of vermin and possibility of outbreak of an epidemic will be significant. Thus,
water related diseases will increase and the health of the people will be grossly affected.




Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP                                                      71
6.0      ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL BASELINE INFORMATION


This chapter presents the environmental and social baseline information of the subproject area,
i.e., the study area, which covers the entire area under baseline investigation, where impacts
may occur as a result of the ASSSPWP. The subproject area of influence includes the
immediate area of the Asafo Community, Fante Newtown and the sewage treatment plant at
Abinkyi and the large area which, covers the Kumasi Metropolis. The environmental and social
information used to draft the baseline has been obtained through literature reviews, publicly
available information and observations made during the field visits. The key literature
reviewed include the GAMA SWP AF Final Environmental and Social Management
Framework (ESMF), June 2020, GAMA SWP (Parent Project and AF) Updated Resettlement
Policy Framework (RPF), April 2020, The World Bank OP 4.01, The Ghana Environmental
Impact Assessment Procedures (EPA 1995), Medium-Term Development Plan (MTDP) and
the Service Charter 2018 of KMA and the 2021 Population and Housing Census Reports
published by the Ghana Statistical Service (GSS).


The ASSSPWP is in an already developed urban location where most of the natural features
have already been converted or developed. The chapter is subdivided into three major headings
as follows:
 Physical Environment;
 Biological Environment; and
 Social Environment.


6.1      Physical Environment


6.1.1 Climate
The study area falls within the wet semi-equatorial climatic region of Ghana characterized by
high rainfall, medium temperature and high humidity. The climate of the proposed subproject
area is determined by the movement of air masses which differ in air moisture and relative
stability rather than temperature.            Like most parts of the country, two main physical
phenomena, the equatorial trough and the associated Inter Tropical Boundary (ITB) or Inter
Tropical Convergence Zone (ITCZ) influence the climatic conditions of the area. The ITB or
ITCZ influences the attraction of alternate air masses from the north and the south called the
northeast trade winds and the southwest monsoon winds respectively. The northeast trade


Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP                                                  72
winds are associated with a dry cool wind known as the harmattan, which affects this part of
Ghana during the months of November to March.


Ghana’s vulnerability to Climate Change impacts is high and the country has been ranked
109 out of 181 countries in the 2020 ND-GAIN Index (Climate Change Country Profile for
Ghana-World Bank 2021).


In Kumasi, the climate change challenges, particularly flooding issues, are significant due to a
combination of environmental, socio-economic, and infrastructural factors.              Kumasi
experiences heavy rainfall during the rainy season. Heavy rainfalls often overwhelm the city’s
inadequate drainage systems and leads to frequent flooding, especially in the low-lying areas
and informal settlements. Rapid urbanization, and inappropriate waste disposal clogging the
drainage and waterways exacerbate this problem, and pose severe threats to lives, income, and
properties in many poor communities including portions of the Asafo and Abinkyi
communities, which are principally drained by the Subin River. The rapid urbanization
contributes to increased surface runoff and soil erosion, also contributes to flooding and
sedimentation of water bodies in and around the metropolis. Climate change effects are thus
manifest in the Kumasi Metropolis.


Concerning this subproject and from field observations, surface run-offs exposed some sewer
pipes, and carried sediments/ waste into some manholes or chambers in addition to direct
human deposition of wastes into these manholes and chambers.


6.1.1.1 Rainfall
The rainfall events in the study area are often intense, torrential and stormy. The resultant
runoffs are very intense and short lived and accompanied by massive erosion of loose soil
material. The proposed subproject area is characterized by two well defined wet seasons, a
main wet season and short wet season. The main wet season begins in March and ends in July
with the month of June experiencing the highest rainfall in a year. The short wet season
commences in September and ends in November. The main dry season, having been desiccated
by the harmattan, occurs in December to March. The short dry season is in August i.e., between
the two wet seasons.




Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP                                                  73
Data for analyzing the rainfall of the subproject area is based on a 10-year (2013 – 2022) daily
rainfall records for Kumasi obtained from the Ghana Meteorological Agency (GMet) at the
Kwame Nkrumah University of Technology (KNUST) weather station. The 10-year annual
rainfall and annual rainy days data for 2013 – 2022 is shown in Tables 16 and 17 respectively.
The data revealed that, Kumasi has a mean annual rainfall of 1,302.3mm and ranged from a
1,054.8mm in 2015 to 1,770.9mm in 2018. The Annual rainy days data followed the same
trend with a minimum of 35 rain days in 2015, a maximum of 56 rainy days in 2018 and an
average of 42 rainy days.


Monthly, the average rainfall amount peaks in September, which is supposed to be the start of
the minor wet season with a value of 246.5mm for 8 rainy days. January, representing the dry
season recorded virtually no rainy days (0.4) with an average rainfall of 12.5mm for the 10-
year period.


Table 16:           10-Year Annual Rainfall for Kumasi (2013 – 2022)

The minimum average temperature is 21.4°C recorded in August, which is a transitional period
into the minor wet season.

6.1.2 Relief and Topography
The landscape of the proposed subproject area can be described as generally undulating with
hills rising up to about 250m – 300m above mean sea level. The low-lying areas have gentle
slopes ranging from 1% to 5% whilst the uplands have slopes of over 10%.


6.1.3 Geology
The Kumasi Metropolitan area is dominated by the Middle Precambrian Birrimian Rock. This
unique geological structure is responsible for the development of the construction industry and
small-scale mining. This geological formation is also suitable for agriculture.


The Kumasi area is characterized by mainly two of the geological types, namely Cape Coast
Granite Complex and Lower Birrimian. The Cape Coast Granite Complex consists of Biotite
and Muscovite Granite.            The Lower Birrimian consists of phyllites, schists, tuffs and
greywackes.


6.1.4 Soils
The Greater Kumasi area is covered by 4 types of soils namely (i) forest ochrosols, (ii) forest
lithosols, (iii) mix of forest ochrosols and lithosols and (iv) forest gleisols.


The forest ochrosols are deeply weathered soils found in the semi-deciduous forest zones. They
consist of thin (about 20 cm), dark grayish brown, humus-stained, sandy loam and silt loam
topsoil which are usually moderate fine granular in structure and friable in consistency.


The major type of soil constituting the top soil of the Kumasi area is the forest ochrosols. forest
ochrosols are rich in nutrients mostly needed for agricultural purposes. The forest lithosols are
thin and brashy soils exist on steep slopes of hard rock.


The gleisols spread on river terrace and are rarely affected by the present river floods. The
soils, due to their alluvial nature, vary in texture, color and reaction.


In general, the soils in the low lying areas of the Kumasi metropolis including the subproject
area are prone to erosion as well as flooding due to high intensity rainfall and soft surface soil
conditions. The flooding has already become quite serious in KMA. With regard to flooding,


Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP                                                     78
the Asafo area among other areas are identified as flood-prone areas. This phenomenon is
attributed to the construction of buildings in waterways, dumping of refuse in gutters and drains
and the inability of existing culverts to receive large volumes of water whenever there is a
heavy downpour. The effect has been loss of valuable property of residents in the affected
communities.


6.1.5 Seismic Activity and Earthquake Zoning
Ghana is not located close to any of the world’s well-known seismic zones. Generally, Ghana
is a stable land mass, and features very low seismic activity. However, significant earthquake
activity has been reported in southern Ghana, especially the coastal region of the country, that
is, along the Gulf of Guinea, where earthquakes up to magnitude 5.5 to 6.5 on the Richter-scale
have been historically recorded (in 1906 and 1939) and occur on repetitive periods of between
50 and 140 years.


Seismic activity in southern Ghana is believed to be caused by movement along two active
fault systems; the Akuapim Fault along the Akuapim mountain range which trends
approximately NE-SW and is located about 20km west of Accra and the Coastal Boundary
Fault which lies some 3km offshore and runs almost parallel to the coastline in the vicinity of
Accra.


For all the earthquakes which have occurred in the country, the epicentres were located along
the coast, in the area surrounding Accra, the Regional Capital, and Cape Coast and
surroundings and these areas suffered comparatively minor to medium damages. Accra is on
record to have experienced comparative earthquakes which caused some damages in 1862,
1906 and 1939.


Figure 8 shows the seismic risk map of southern Ghana (Kutu, 2013). The earthquake zoning
is related to the probability of occurrence.     Zone 0 areas have the least occurrence of
earthquakes and zone 5 areas have the most. This means that the higher the zone the more
likely the areas under it will experience higher earthquake loads. According to the seismicity
map of southern Ghana, the subproject area/Kumasi lies in Zone 1 of the seismic risk
assessment map, which is a lower seismic hazard zone. Adequate resistance against
earthquakes in zones 0 to 2 is provided by adhering to the basic building code as well as good
construction practices.


Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP                                                   79
 Source:          Kutu (2013)
Figure 8:         Seismic Risk Map of Southern Part of Ghana (Source: Kutu, 2013)

and 01° 36' 51.61" W (see Figure 10).

    Health Organization Guidelines (WHO) for drinking water.


6.1.7 Surface Water (Drainage)
The subproject area falls within the Subin River sub-catchment of the Oda River catchment (see
Figure 9). The subproject area is principally drained by the Subin River and the treated effluent
from the treatment plant is discharged into the Subin in the vicinity of the treatment ponds. The
subproject site specifically is drained by the Subin River which lies east and its tributary, the
Gee Stream which lies west of the site.


The Subin River rises out of a spring at an abandoned racecourse (6o 45’N; 1o 38’W) north of
Kumasi and runs southwards through the city Centre (Kumasi Zoo, Kejetia, Central Market,
Asafo etc.) and discharges into the Oda River at Asago (6o 45’N; 1o 36’W), a rural farming
community near Kumasi. The Subin has a catchment area of about 230km2 with a mean flow
rate of 0.243m3/s. Its other tributaries include Tuantam and the Sisa into which the Wiwi and
Aboabo also discharge.


Within the KMA, the river channel has been lined with concrete, which has distorted its natural
form. This situation and other human factors including dumping of waste in the river, have
caused the river to lose its aquatic life.


The Gee Stream is a minor sub-catchment of the Subin River and rises from the KATH/ 4BN
Military Barracks area, draining portions of the Central Business District (CBD) and discharges
into the Subin at the treatment plant site. Its flow at the treatment plant site was measured to be
circa 0.03m3/s




Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP                                                     82
Figure 9:         The Pra Basin Showing the KMA

6.1.8 Subin River and Gee Stream Quality
The water quality assessment of the Subin River and the Gee stream were undertaken as part
of the baseline studies on 28th August 2023. The sampling locations were (i) Gee Stream at
Treatment Plant Site Before the Confluence with Subin (1) (ii) Subin Upstream at the Kumasi
Zoological Gardens (2), (iii) Subin Downstream at Kaase (3) and (iv) Subin at Treatment Plant
Site (4), and they are shown in Figure 10.


Methodology, Analysis and Results
Grab samples were collected into 1 liter bottles at each sampling location by means of a clean
container and kept in an ice chest. The water samples were sent to the Ecological Laboratories
at the University of Ghana (UG) for the analysis. Specified methods as laid in “Standard
Methods for the Examination of Water and Wastewater” published jointly by the American
Water Works Association (AWWA), American Public Health Association (APHA) and the
Water Environment Federation (WEF) 23rd Edition, 2017 were followed. The results are as
shown in Table 22.

The results in the Maturation Pond 2 (Final) - S1 have been compared with the Ghana Standards (GS) for Discharges from Sewage Treatment
Plant to a natural water body administered by EPA and the WBG EHS Guidelines Indicative Values for Treated Sanitary Sewage Discharges. A
description of the water quality characteristics of the maturation pond where discharges to the Subin River occur is presented.


The Maturation Pond 2 is closest to the Subin River. Discharges to the Subin is from this Pond via the final effluent chamber. The results show
that the effluent quality for Total Coliforms (3,000cfu/µl) was not compliant with the respective WBG EHS Guidelines value of 400cfu/µl.




Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP                                                                                                                    90
Conclusion on the Wastewater Quality in the Ponds
The quality of the wastewater in the treatment ponds show that the sewage gets treated before
discharge into the Subin River except for Ammonia-Nitrogen and Coliform concentration. The
current challenge however, is that not all the sewage generated gets into the treatment system
since copious volumes of the sewage gets discharged into the Subin River without reaching the
treatment ponds. Indeed, the Subin River quality before the discharge from the sewage
treatment plant showed concentrations of TSS(82.2mg/l), BOD (135mg/l), COD (240mg/l) and
Total Coliforms (8,000cfu/µl) in excess of the WB EHS Guideline values of 50mg/l, 30mg/l,
125mg/l and 400cfu/µl respectively.

6.1.12 Noise Assessment
As part of this ESIA study, ambient noise assessments were carried out at the air quality
monitoring locations in the subproject area in August 2023.


6.1.12.1 Methodology, Analysis and Results
The noise monitoring was carried out for daytime and nighttime. The sampling locations are
same for that of the ambient air quality (see Figure 11). Noise measurements at the sites were
determined using the portable General DSM403SD and the portable Pulsar Nova Integrating
Average Sound Level Meters in monitoring the noise level at the sites, which enables real-time
monitoring of the noise and provides the time-weighted average values over the monitoring
period- 24- hour averaging time at the points monitored. The results provided are from the
General DSM403SD portable noise level meter (see Plate 13).


Measurements were done in line with GS 1253:2018, and Noise Levels were captured in situ
in decibels on the A scale, i.e., dB(A). The noise monitoring results are provided in Tables 28
and 29 for the nighttime and daytime respectively, and compared with the GS value for Mixed
Used Areas.

    noise emanating from vehicles/ motorbikes that ply the main road.


6.1.13 Land Use
The Asafo community has a relative high percentage of mixed-use development with the trend
of development shifting from a predominantly residential to a mixed-use development which
mainly consist of a mixture of residential and commercial use within a structure (see Table
30). Asafo is experiencing transformation from it purely residential character into mixed use
development. Tenement houses are being converted to multi-storey buildings to accommodate
the demand for space for competing uses, especially commercial and light industrial activities.
A number of transport services operate in the Asafo Market area including OA Transport, VIP
Services and Intercity STC Coaches limited


The changing face of the nature of development ongoing in Asafo can be attributed to a number
of factors including the proximity to the Central Business District (CBD) and presence of key
educational institutions and state agencies and departments. The presence of the Kumasi
Technical University (KsTU) especially has contributed to the springing up of hostels and the
conversion of residential buildings into commercial use, particularly Printing and Book
Publishing. Figure 12 presents the landuse of the Asafo community. The areas shaded blue
and hatched brown represent commercial and mixed uses (commercial plus residential),
respectively.


Table 30:           Land Use Types at Asafo
 Landuse Types                                Number of Parcels      Percentage

 Residential                                  157                    29.6

 Commercial                                   73                     13.8

 Civic & Cultural                             25                     4.7

 Educational                                  8                      1.5



Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP                                                 99
 Landuse Types                                Number of Parcels      Percentage

 Mixed use (Residential/Commercial)           259                    48.9

 Open space                                   8                      1.5

 Total                                        530                    100



Among the land-uses in the Abinkyi area where the treatment plant is located are the GRIDCo
high tension pylons, mechanics shops, metal fabrication shop, open spaces for storage and
resale of coconut and informal settlement (squatters living in mainly makeshift wooden
structures) occurring along the Subin drain and the fence line of the existing treatment ponds.




Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP                                                 100
Figure 12:        Landuse of Asafo Community


Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP    101
6.2      Biological Environment


6.2.1 Terrestrial Flora
The Metropolis lies in the transitional forest zone specifically within the moist semi-deciduous
South-East Ecological Zone. Predominant species of trees found are Ceiba, Triplochlon, Celtis
and other exotic species. The soil in this ecological zone is rich in nutrients for crop cultivation.
This vegetative cover partly explains the reason why Kumasi had the accolade the “Garden
City of West Africa”. Albeit, the city has lost a sizeable stretch of its vegetative cover to
physical construction due to urbanization. Certain segments of the Metropolis e.g., the Kwame
Nkrumah University of Science and Technology (KNUST), Kumasi Zoological Gardens,
Manhyia Gardens and Nhyiaso still have green environments which need conscientious
conversation plans.


The Asafo community however, has virtually no vegetative cover. Again, the treatment plant
site has little vegetative cover, mostly grass cover around the waste stabilisation ponds. The
few tree species along the Subin River at the treatment plant site is dominated by Leucaena
leucocephala- an invasive species that require some management strategy to avoid its
dominance downstream. Already, the few tree species along the Gee Stream at the treatment
plant site are controlled due to the presence of the high-tension power lines at the site.


The terrestrial flora and fauna survey was carried out in the project area as part of this ESIA
study. The field survey was conducted on 28th August 2023.


6.2.1.1 Methodology
The detailed flora survey methods are provided in Annex 3, and consisted of literature reviews
and field surveys. The field survey sample location coordinates and profiles are presented in
Table 31.


The sample site data gathered and analysis are respectively presented in Appendices 1 and 2 of
Annex 3. Specimens of species that could not readily be identified in the field were identified
in the Ghana Herbarium. Nomenclature follows Flora of West Tropical Africa (Hutchinson
and Dalziel, 1972). The conservation status of the species encountered were defined using the
Star Rating system adopted in the Forest Reserves of Ghana Geographic Information Exhibitor
manual (Hawthorne, 1995) and the IUCN Red list of threatened species categories.


Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP                                                      102
Table 31:         Flora and Fauna Survey Sites
                                                  Elevatio
                                                  n                                                  Habitat

                                                             Built up Gee stream bank with
                                                             isolated      thicket/       managed
 1 Abinkyi           N06.67227       W001.61393   245        grassland/wetland                       Modified
                                                             Stream           overgrown       with   Semi-

The terrestrial flora and fauna survey, as part of the ESIA requirements for the ASSSPWP, was
conducted with the following objectives:
 Identify and describe the main plant communities, habitats of ecological interest (forests,
    wetlands, less disturbed habitats, habitats that may contain special-status species, etc.) and
    plants species found;
 (Near Threatened (NT), Vulnerable (VU), Endangered (EN) and Critically Endangered
    (CR));
 Determine the presence of invasive species;
 Identify ecosystem and cultural services provided by habitats found inside the project area
    and provide a list of plant species with their associated uses by local communities; and
 Survey of terrestrial fauna- mammals, birds, reptiles and amphibians in the community.


6.2.1.2:          Flora Survey Results and Analysis
The detailed results of the flora survey carried out are summarised as follows:
 The project area is generally built-up with undulating topography and drained by Subin
    River and its tributaries. The Subin River has been converted into a large drain along most



Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP                                                             103
    of its course through the project area. There is no significant forest vegetation in the project
    area. A small patch of gallery forest occurs upstream of the river in the Kumasi Zoo
    enclave, while patches of aquatic vegetation, dominated by Thalia geniculata, occur
    wetlands along the banks of the Gee stream and Subin River. A mosaic of farmlands and
    farm re-growths/secondary thickets occur in well drained areas within the project site.
 The farm re-growths/thickets have species such as Ficus exasperata, Alchornea cordifolia,
    Solanum        verbascifolium,       Mimosa     pudica,   Ipomoea     involucrata,   Sporobolus
    pyramidalis,Carica papaya, Broussonetia papyrifera and Chromolaena odorata


The species lists for the 9 samples analysed are presented in Appendix 1 of Annex 3 while the
floristic composition and analyses of the project sites are presented in Appendix 2. Appendix
2 shows that the survey recorded a total of 77 species in 71 genera belonging to 36 families.
The families with high species representation were the Fabaceae (10), Poaceae (10) and
Euphorbiaceae (5). These 3 families (out of total of 36) accounted for about 32.5% of the
species recorded. The rest of the families were poorly represented with less than 5 species.


The Life form composition of the flora showed a dominance of the Tree Life form (about
37.7%) followed by the Herb, Shrub and Climber life forms with 28.6%, 24.7% and 9.1%
respectively of the flora. Disturbance due to land clearance and regeneration in the farm
regrowth and thickets could explain the observed diversity of life forms.


The     ecological      guilds     of    the   species,   which   gives   an   indication   of   the
developmental/successional stage of the vegetation, shows a dominance of the pioneers. The
dominance of pioneers (about 48%), which are secondary species, confirms that the vegetation
of the project area is highly disturbed. The primary species (shade-bearing and non-pioneer
light demanders) constituted only 2.6% of the flora, a further indication of the disturbed nature
of the vegetation. Swamp species constituted 15.6% of the plant species recorded.


6.2.2 Terrestrial Fauna
Deforestation activities and hunting have affected the fauna in the subproject area. Animal
species have depleted over the years. There are however a number of snakes, lizards, frogs and
toads at the treatment plant site and the insects include butterflies, dragonflies and
grasshoppers. A few birds including hawks and doves were sighted around the treatment site.



Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP                                                      104
The small mammals are dominated by rats at both the treatment plant site and the Asafo
community.


An assessment of the fauna in the project area was carried out at the same sampling locations
for the flora study and it showed that:
 in general, 13 species of animals including (2) species of amphibians, four (4) species of
    reptiles, four (4) species of birds, and three (3) species of mammals were recorded.
 some of the species reported to occur in the area were reported to be rare. This included the
    Nile Crocodile (Crocodilus niloticus), Grasscutter (Thryonomys swinderianus) and the Nile
    Monitor Lizard (Varanus niloticus).


6.2.3 Aquatic Flora and Fauna
A number of aquatic flora were observed in the waste stabilization ponds especially in the
Facultative and Maturation Ponds and they include the following: Ipomea aquatica;Paspalium
sp.; Lemnea; Rhynchospora corymbose; Ludwigia decurrens; Polygonum salicifolium;
Echinocloa stagnina; Leersia sp.; Limnocaris flava; and Typha domingensis.


Ichthyofauna in the maturation pond include Catfish (Arius gigas) and Tilapia (Tilapia semi-
fasciatus and Oreochromis niloticus) (see Plate 14).




Plate 14:         Fish Types in the Maturation Ponds


Also present at the treatment plant site was the Cattle Egret (Egretta gazerta), Common Bulbul
(Pycnonotus barbatus) and Pied crow (Corvus albus).




Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP                                                105
Presence of Crocodiles in the Wastewater Treatment Ponds
According to the anecdotal evidence, three (3) crocodiles were last seen in 2019. Mr Issaku
(an attendant at the treatment plant site), attributes the crocodiles’ absence to the sprawling
wooden structures/ human encroachments around the sewage treatment ponds. There is
increased pollution and human disturbance of the aquatic habitat for vertebrates including the
crocodiles.


Currently, the crocodiles are not in the habitat as they are likely to have moved on to more
suitable habitats.


6.3      Socio-Economic Environment


The Kumasi Metropolis is richly endowed with human and natural resources, particularly soil
and timber species, mineral deposits, tourist attraction sites, good climatic conditions.


There are concentrations of economic activities in the city. The first and most important
location is the Central Business District (CBD), which embraces the Kejetia Lorry Park, the
Central Market and the Adum Shopping Centre. The other economics nodes include the
Kaase/Ahinsan Industrial Area and the Sokoban Wood Village. Most industries which deal in
timber processing, logging, food processing and soap making are concentrated at the
Kaase/Ahinsan Industrial Area. There are also a number of satellite markets in the metropolis
and they include Asafo Market and Bantama Market.


6.3.1 Location and Size
The proposed subproject is located in the Asafo community in the jurisdiction of KMA of the
Ashanti Region of Ghana. Kumasi covers a total land area of approximately 78.28km² and
shares boundaries with Afigya Kwabre, Suame and Tafo to the north, Asokore Mampong and
Oforikrom to the east, Asokwa to the south – east, Atwima Kwanwoma to the south and
Kwadaso to the west.


6.3.2 Land Ownership (Treatment Plant Site)
The subproject sites include the RoWs at Asafo, Fante Newtown and Abinkyi area as well as
the treatment plant site also at Abinkyi, which is 17.98 acres. Portions of the land for the
treatment plant has been used for installation of the GRIDCo pylons as the high-tension power


Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP                                                 106
lines pass over the site. The treatment plant site falls under the Nhyiaeso Traditional Council
(ATC) of the Kumasi Traditional Council (KTC). The land for the treatment plant has thus
been formally acquired by KMA with the issuance of the certificate of allocation including site
plan by the Lands Commission (Annex 2).


6.3.3 Educational Institutions in the Subproject Area of Influence
The subproject area hosts a number of educational institutions from the basic, secondary and
tertiary levels. Prominent among the institutions are KsTU, Kumasi Anglican Senior High
School (Super KASS) all at Fante Newtown and the New Asafo Basic School.


6.3.4 Population Characteristics
The KMA is the most populous district in Ghana according to the 2021 population and housing
census report with a total population of 443,981 of which 213,662 (48.1%) are males and
230,319 (51.9%).


6.3.5 Sanitation
The Waste Management Department (WMD) of KMA is responsible for environmental
sanitation in the metropolis. The department is mandated to provide facilities, infrastructural
services and programs for effective and efficient waste management for improvement in
environmental sanitation and protection of the environment.


The main components of solid waste generated in the metropolis are organic waste and plastics.
The most common means of transporting such refuse is by the use of motorized tricycle (Borla
Taxi) due to the inability of waste garbage trucks to access most residential areas to collect this
refuse. The tricycles dispose the waste at a designated site (transfer station, see Plate 15) in
the community for management and final disposal by Zoom Lion trucks to the Oti Landfill
Site. Dumping of solid waste indiscriminately is also practiced by some households in the
community.




Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP                                                    107
Plate 15:         The Solid Waste Transfer Station at Asafo



For liquid waste disposal, the common practices are disposal of sewage into drains or gutter,
or throwing of grey water/ sullage into gutter or onto the compound are the most common
practices by households in the Metropolis. In the Asafo community, most of the chambers and
manholes have been damaged and encroached upon, and circa 60% of the sewage is discharged
directly into the Subin river.


6.3.6 Availability and Conditions of Toilet Facilities
According to the Feasibility Studies/ Assessment Report (Draft) for the Proposed System
Development/ Improvement in Kumasi (November 2022), 99.7% of the households
interviewed for the socioeconomic study confirm access to toilet facilities with only 0.3% of
them practicing open defecation. Also, 16.4% of the respondents use public toilet. The shared
compound toilet is the most commonly used toilet facility. Only 7 percent of the households
in Asafo do not have dedicated toilets. The study further confirmed that 80% of the houses in
the Asafo community are connected to the sewerage system and that 40% of the houses
connected are for mixed uses while only 29% are for residential use only. The existing
sewerage system users also include commercial facilities, offices, schools, hospitality and
healthcare facilities etc.




Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP                                              108
Within the subproject’s catchment, there is a significant number of public toilets, mostly used
by commuters and people in transit. Table 32 provides an overview of Public Toilets in the
subproject communities.

6.3.7 Water Supply
The main sources of potable water supply to residents in Kumasi are from the Barekese and
Owabi water treatment plants. Out of the over 1,000km of pipelines for water transmission
and distribution in the region, Kumasi alone has circa 704km of the water supply network. This
notwithstanding, some households have installed mechanized boreholes with overhead poly
tanks to augment the supply of potable water delivered by Ghana Water Company Limited
(GWCL).




Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP                                                              109
In the Asafo community, the two main sources of drinking water are sachet water and GWCL
Tap, with the two accounting for about 97% of the sources of drinking water for the households.
At the treatment site however, the squatter community uses groundwater (shallow wells) for
washing and ablution purposes.


6.3.8 Cultural Value
Asafo is in the jurisdiction of KMA in the Ashanti Region of Ghana. The social structure is
composed of the chieftaincy institution, lineage and systems of inheritance, ethnicity, festivals,
language, religion and customs among others. The Chief of Asafo, the Asafohene is the head
of the Akwamu Division of the Kumasi Traditional Council (KTC) headed by the Asantehene,
King of the Asante Kingdom. The traditional jurisdiction of KTC covers the whole of Kumasi
Metropolis and some areas in the Ashanti, Ahafo, Bono and Bono East Regions of Ghana. It
comprises of Krontire, Akwamu, Adonten, Benkum, Gyaase, Ankobia, Kyidom, Oyoko,
Mawere and Nkosuo divisions. Each of these divisions has its own Divisional Council.


The subproject area is noted for the matrilineal system of customary inheritance. Among the
many festivals celebrated in the subproject area include the Akwasidae. Akans are historically
the traditional ethnic group in the subproject area but are mainly of the Asante extraction.
Others include Mole Dagbons and Ewes. Almost all the other ethnic groups in Ghana are
residents in the Metropolis. Although, ethnic and cultural diversity abounds tremendously in
the Metropolis, the population is closely–knit together in a harmonious relationship due to the
presence of a strong traditional administrative set-up that endeavors to foster cohesion among
the diverse ethnic groups.            Three main religious groupings predominate, namely the
Traditional, Christianity and Islam.


6.3.9 Tourism
Kumasi Metropolis is considered one of the important centers of African culture and traditions.
This has made the Metropolis a vibrant tourist destination in Ashanti Region. Notable site of
tourist attraction includes Manhyia Palace, which is the seat of the Asante Kingdom, dating
back to the 17th Century. The Palace also houses the Royal Museum, which is stocked with
rare and unique royal paraphernalia and life-size effigies of notable Asante Kings and Queens.


The Center for National Culture, the first of its kind to be established in Sub-Saharan Africa in
1952 is another tourist attraction in the Metropolis. It houses the Prempeh II Museum, craft


Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP                                                   110
center, center for cultural display and tutorials and a gift shop. These facilities showcase the
rich history and culture of the Asante Kingdom, traditional craftsmen displaying their skills in
kente weaving, pottery and blacksmithing, teaching and learning of traditional dances,
traditional leatherworks, carvings and other Ghanaian artifacts. Other places of interest in the
Metropolis include the Okomfo Anokye Sword, Fort St. George (War Museum), Kumasi
Central Market and Kumasi Zoological Gardens. All these tourist sites attract people of all
walks of life especially from the diaspora.


6.3.10 Occupation
According to the socioeconomic data provided in the Feasibility Study/ Assessment Report
(November 2022), 88.1% of households engage in some form of income generating activity,
be it formal or informal. The various occupations explained in Table 33. Figure 13 show

6.3.12 Traffic Assessment
As part of this ESIA study, a traffic survey was carried out in the subproject area in August and
September of 2023 to establish the baseline traffic conditions and to evaluate the impact of the
proposed ASSSSPWP on the existing road network in the Asafo and Abinkyi communities as
shown in Figure 15.




Figure 15:        Locality Map of Traffic Survey Points



The selected road network evaluation comprised Yaa Asantewaa Road, O.A. Street, Subinso
Road and Osei Tutu I Avenue. The intersections assessed comprised Yaa Asantewaa Road /


Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP                                                  113
O.A. Street intersection and Osei Tutu I Avenue / Subinso Road intersection. The Yaa
Asantewaa Road, O.A. Street, Osei Tutu I Avenue are asphalt paved whereas Subinso Road is
a gravel road along the Subin Storm drain at the Abinkyi Market.


6.3.5.1 Current Traffic (Base Year- 2023)
The results of the Annual Average Daily Traffic (AADT) is presented in Table 34.


Table 34:         Results of AADT
 Road Name               Location of Study                  Annual Average Daily Traffic

 Yaa Asantewaa Road       Near Amakom SDA School Junction              13,857
 O. A. Road               Near KSTU Gate                               3,854
 Osei Tutu I Avenue       Near Abinkyi Lorry Station                   19,924
 Subinso Road             Near Abinkyi Lorry Station                   3,076
Source: Consultants Study, August/ September 2023


The through traffic and turning movement counts were compared with the Level of Service
(LOS) rating to establish the baseline traffic situation of the surveyed roads. The LOS grading
system is a rating scale ranging from LOS A to LOS F, where LOS A represents free-flow
conditions and LOS F represents congested or jammed conditions.


After conducting the traffic surveys, the following conclusions have been drawn:
 There are significant delays at the Osei Tutu I Avenue / Subinso Road intersection near the
    railway and storm drain crossing at Abinkyi Market. Base year LOS F recorded. Railway
    track across Osei Tutu I Avenue on the East Approach (from VIP Bus Parking Terminal)
    slows down traffic through the intersection thus affecting the throughput. The negative
    impact of left-turning traffic contributes to delays experienced by motorists.;
 Yaa Asantewaa Road / O. A. Road Intersection near Amakom SDA operates within
    capacity with recorded LOS D. This intersection is also affected by left-turning traffic.
 Osei Tutu I Avenue and Yaa Asantewaa Road are relatively high-volume roads whereas
    O.A. Street and Subinso Road carry less traffic;
 The execution of the works under the subproject may impact negatively on the identified
    intersections and some links due to certain activities such as excavation across and along
    roads and works within the influence zone of the intersections, narrowing of lanes, lane
    closures and reduced speed areas; and



Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP                                                 114
 Pedestrian activity within project area is significant especially at the Kumasi Technical
    University.


From the foregoing, the following mitigation / management measures have been
recommended:


 The executing contractor should develop a traffic management plan to guide the works and
    reduce disruption to traffic flow on adjoining roads;
 Significant construction activities may be planned for off-peak periods such as weekends
    and night times where possible;
 Provide adequate signage for diversion routes and cordon off deep excavations
    appropriately;
 Channelise pedestrian movements within construction zones to prevent jay-walking and
    ensure safety;
 Deploy a flag man or traffic warden to direct traffic;
 Post speed limits and other safety signs within the construction zone; and
 Educate the public through mass media such as local radio stations and community social
    centres before and during the works especially when roads are to be cut and / or sealed off.




Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP                                                 115
7.0      STAKEHOLDER CONSULTATIONS AND PUBLIC PARTICIPATION


The Ghana Environmental Assessment Procedures for the conduct of ESIA studies in the
country require the involvement of all relevant stakeholders in the process. This is aimed at
providing opportunities for especially Interested and Affected Parties (I&AP) e.g., PAPs and
all public and private groups including local non-governmental organizations (NGOs) with
interest or concern for various aspects of the subproject to participate in the successful
formulation and implementation of the subproject.


7.1      Stakeholder Engagement Objectives


Stakeholder participation in project planning, design and implementation is widely recognized
as an integral part of environmental and social impact assessment in order to assure project
success. Local communities, their representatives, government, national and international non-
governmental organizations (NGOs) may all be able to contribute to (and benefit from)
dialogue directed at identifying and resolving key project-related issues.


The objectives of the engagement exercise are to:
 provide information related to the activities of the proposed subproject;
 facilitate and maintain dialogue;
 seek participation of all interested parties;
 identify stakeholder interests as well as issues including community concerns and
   expectations;
 support participation in the subproject decision-making process and design;
 create solutions for addressing any concerns and integrating them into subproject design,
   operations, and management; and
 enhance the subproject by learning from, and incorporating the expertise of individuals,
   professionals, communities and organizations.


7.2      Stakeholder Engagement Methodology


During the stakeholder engagement process the following information dissemination and data
gathering were adopted:
i. Focus Group Discussions (FGDs): FGDs were carried out with Asafo, Fante Newtown


Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP                                               116
   and Abinkyi community members, and KMA Officials to explain the aims and objectives
   of the proposed subproject and also the ESIA study, including roles and responsibilities of
   the stakeholder. Each group was engaged separately and asked series of questions regarding
   the environment and social concerns and expectations that they may have about the proposed
   ASSSPWP. The meetings were also used to clarify/ answer issues related to their concerns.
ii. Key Informant Interviews (KIIs): Key informants interviewed included MSWR staff, the
   Subproject Engineers at Colan, and EEL Officials at Asafo and the treatment plant site.
iii. Telephone Interviews: Some of the stakeholders were able to comment on the proposed
   subproject using telephone conversation e.g., individual environmental consultants who
   have stayed in Asafo previously and have knowledge about the ASSS.


7.3      Stakeholder Identification and Engagements Activities


The Consultants activities were used to identify the relevant stakeholders mostly the
institutions and PAPs to gauge their levels of interest as well as their concern for the
environment and any social considerations. Mainly formal/ informal discussions were used
when engaging with individuals representing institutions. Key Person Interviews (KPI),
meetings of PAPs, Focus Group Discussions (FGD) were also used in the consultation process.
In addition, formal correspondences with other stakeholders were used.


The groups consulted include among others, officials of:
Subproject Proponent
 Kumasi Metropolitan Assembly (KMA).
 Greater Kumasi Metropolitan Area- Sanitation and Water Project (GKMA- SWP).


Government Institutions
 Ghana Water Company Limited (GWCL)- Kumasi South Regional Office, Kumasi;
 Electricity Company of Ghana (ECG), Kumasi Bulk Supply Substation at Abinkyi;
 Ghana Grid Company (GRIDCo), Kumasi Office at Abinkyi;
 The Wildlife Division of the Forestry Commission (FC);
 Water Resources Commission (WRC)- Head Office, Accra and Pra Basin Office in Kumasi;
 Environmental Protection Agency (EPA)- Head Office, Accra and Regional Office,
   Kumasi;
 Asokwa Municipal Assembly;


Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP                                                  117
 Lands Commission, Kumasi; and
 Labor Office, Kumasi.


Other Stakeholders
 Asafo Unit committee members;
 Assembly Members of Asafo and Abinkyi areas;
 Project Affected Persons (PAPs) Project Affected Persons (PAPs) including some
   Households and Businesses that are connected to the Asafo Sewerage System;
 Environment Engineering Limited (EEL);
 Asafo Traditional Authority (AsTA);
 Nhyiaeso Traditional Authority/ Council;
 Kumasi Technical University (KsTU);
 VIP Company Limited (VIP);
 Intercity STC Limited (STC);
 PROTOA at Abinkyi Station;

 Zoom Lion Company Limited;
 Abinkyi Traders Association;
 Mechanics at Abinkyi Station; and
 Residents/ squatters around the Asafo Sewage Treatment Ponds.


The engagement process allowed for the stakeholders to discuss and raise any concerns they
may have regarding the scope of the subproject. It is significant to note that not all the
stakeholders consulted:
 provided comments on the proposed subproject;
 completed a stakeholder engagement form provided; and
 could be captured photographically.


7.4      Stakeholder Analysis and Prioritization


The stakeholders are grouped according to their roles, interests and influence on the subproject,
as well as to the extent to which they will be negatively or positively impacted by the proposed
subproject. The degree to which the identified stakeholders will be impacted by the subproject



Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP                                                  118
and the level of influence of the stakeholders on the subproject outcome are rated as low,
medium or high as defined hereunder:


Degree of Subproject Impact on Stakeholders
The impact of the subproject on the stakeholder is the extent of benefits or losses/ damages that
the affected stakeholder will gain/ suffer due to subproject implementation and are categorized
as low, medium and high as provided below.


Low: The subproject is assessed to have an insignificant (positive or negative) impact on the
stakeholder.


Medium: The subproject will have measurable (positive or negative) impact on the
stakeholder.


High: The subproject will have a significant (positive or negative) impact on the stakeholder.


Degree of Stakeholder Influence on Subproject Outcome
The degree of stakeholder influence on subproject outcome is the extent, ability or capacity of
the stakeholder to positively influence subproject outcome (i.e., promote, facilitate or enable
subproject implementation etc.) or negatively influence subproject outcome (i.e., delay, halt,
prevent subproject implementation etc.).


Low: The stakeholder has minimal capability to positively or negatively influence the
outcome of the subproject.


Medium: The stakeholder has measurable capability to positively or negatively influence the
outcome of the subproject.


High: The stakeholder has significant capability to positively or negatively influence the
outcome of the subproject.


The frequency of engagement and management of these stakeholder groups will then depend
upon the level of priority placed on them. High priority stakeholders should be properly or
carefully managed, engaged more often during subproject development and implementation


Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP                                                  119
than moderate and low priority stakeholders. Table 35 describes the criteria for determining
priority levels and Table 36 provides the stakeholder analysis in more detail as far as the
ASSSPWP is concerned.

                                              Low               Medium             High
 Level of Influence       Low                 Low priority      Moderate           High priority

                          Medium              Moderate          Moderate           High priority

7.5      Stakeholder Engagement Strategy


7.5.1 Guiding Principles of the Stakeholder Engagement Strategy
The stakeholder engagement strategy for the proposed subproject is in accordance with the
requirements of WBG’s basic principles of good practice in stakeholder consultation, which
states that a good consultation process should be:
 Targeted at those most likely to be affected by the subproject;
 Early enough to scope key issues and have an effect on the subproject decisions to which
   they relate;
 Informed as a result of relevant information being disseminated in advance;
 Meaningful to those consulted because the content is presented in a readily understandable
   format and the techniques used are culturally appropriate;
 Two-way so that both sides have the opportunity to exchange views and information, to
   listen, and to have their issues addressed;
 Gender-inclusive through awareness that men and women often have differing views and
   needs;
 Localized to reflect appropriate timeframes, context, and local languages;
 Free from manipulation or coercion;
 Documented to keep track of who has been consulted and the key issues raised;
 Reported back in a timely way to those consulted, with clarification of next steps; and
 Ongoing as required during the life of the subproject.


7.5.2 Engagement Strategy and Approach
The stakeholder engagement process began at the preliminary stages during the preparation of
the Scoping Report and has continued through to the ESIA phase. It will continue as part of
the disclosure of the ESIA and during the subproject implementation. Table 37 summarizes
the proposed approach for stakeholder engagement at key stages of project implementation.
