The following are highlights of the key issues/concerns raised by stakeholders/affected persons
during the consultations:
    Most of the traders and especially those selling on the pavements, had doubts that they
     would be allowed back on the pavement once the project was completed. They pleaded
     with the authorities to allow them to continue to sell at their present location since that was
     their only means of livelihood.
    Most of the business operators and squatters at the treatment plant site feared being
     displaced and losing their livelihood. This they believe will have great impact on their
     socioeconomic lives and impact their families. They pleaded that the project find a suitable
     place to relocate them for shelter and be able to continue their businesses.
    Business operators/squatters also noted that it is difficult finding new locations for their
     businesses. They were afraid they were going to lose the customers they have built over the
     years with the change in location.
    Regarding compensation to affected persons, they feared of not being adequately
     compensated when the Assembly gets involved.
    Shop owners also expressed concerns of road traffic when construction begins.


Adequate responses to the PAPs were provided by the Consultant to the effect that that their
concerns would be taken up in the Resettlement Action Plan (RAP) study being carried out for
the subproject (see Annex 4).




Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP                                                                 134
Emphasis have been placed on the following stakeholders to elicit the right responses to address
the listed environmental and social issues as part of the ESIA study:
 Wildlife Division (the issue of the presence of crocodiles in the ponds and the mitigation
    measures);
 MTTD (they are key in traffic diversions planning and implementation of the subproject
    and will assist in managing traffic impacts on busy corridors e.g., Osei-Tutu II Avenue at
    Asafo);
 National Communication Authority (The NCA is the focal point of TELCOS when it comes
    to assessing impacts on their facilities and if reinstatements would have to be carried out.
    Consequently, the TELCOS are dealt with through their umbrella -NCA and the Ghana
    Chamber of Telecommunications)
 Department of Urban Roads (DUR)/ Ministry of Roads & Highways (cutting of
     roads/thrust borings that will disrupt traffic flow on busy roads would require preparation
     of traffic management plans, which must be approved by the Ministry or DUR prior to its
     implementation. Consequently, consultations have been held with the DUR)
 DUR (cutting of existing drains especially lined drains must be sanctioned by the urban
     roads. Hence in the event that roads will be cut into Subin, then the DUR has to be
     involved)
 Leadership of Transport Associations/Unions discharging and loading goods/passengers
     inside and frontage of the Asafo Market where sewer lines will be excavated and replanted.
     (disruption of access to/at parking areas during subproject works and possible temporary
     restrictions have been the focus of the consultation and their concerns have been
     considered in the action plan/mitigation plan)
 Transport Companies/Associations plying the routes that are likely to be blocked/cut/
     thrust bored, which may affect the flow of traffic in the subproject corridor (leadership of
     transport associations at major lorry stations linking the Asafo route corridor have been
     engaged and their concerns considered- STC, VIP/VVIP, GPRTU, PROTOA, etc)
 Water Resources Commission (the Gee stream diversions issues and the need to apply for
     a permit has been clarified by WRC. KMA/ GAMA-SWP PCU has applied for such a
     permit from WRC.
 Solid wastes will be generated and collected and disposed. The specific type of solid
     wastes and their nature have been clearly discussed i.e. sewer pipes with flowing liquid,
     demolished concreted, etc. In this regard, the study has identified the specific areas where



Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP                                                  135
     these materials will be disposed i.e., the Oti Landfill that is under the jurisdiction of the
     KMA and they are ready to receive such wastes
 Liquid waste in the ponds will not be evacuated for disposal but will be pumped into
     leading ponds. Management of the sludge in the proposed drying beds and use of the
     stabilised sludge will be taken up by GKMA-SWP PCU and KMA.
 PAPs have been consulted widely and their concerns addressed in the RAP; and.
 GRIDCo have confirmed the need to consult further with their head office in Accra.


Plate 16 show consultations with some mechanics at the treatment plant site while Plate 17
shows the consultations with the Chief of Nhyiaeso, Nana Nana Osei Yaw Frimpong II.




Plate 16:         Consultation with Some Mechanics at the Treatment Plant Site




Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP                                                   136
Plate 17:         Consultation with the Chief of Nhyiaeso, Nana Osei Yaw Frimpong II




Social Acceptability of Subproject
The people within the subproject area are generally receptive and open for discussion. All
stakeholders from both government agencies and local groupings and community leaders the
consulting team interacted with showed strong need for improvement of the ASSS and are very
willing to work and cooperate with the KMA and GKMA- SWP on the proposed intervention
to improve the sanitation situation in the community. They are therefore prepared to make
concessions and sacrifices that may be necessary during the subproject construction.




Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP                                            137
8.0      POTENTIAL ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACT AND RISKS
         IDENTIFICATION AND SIGNIFICANCE


This chapter discusses the potential environmental and social impacts and risks that have been
identified through field visits, field inspections, baseline studies, analysis, use of professional
expertise and results of stakeholder engagements. Overall, the preconstruction, construction,
operation and decommissioning phases of the proposed ASSSPWP may result in a number of
potential environmental and social impacts and risks in the biophysical and social
environments. These potential impacts could be positive, negative or neutral for which the
adverse ones should be mitigated, and the positive ones enhanced.


8.1      Area of Influence


This ESIA study refers to the subproject Area of Influence (AOI) as the area where water
resource or land is required for construction of any component of the ASSSPWP and its actual
footprint; and the surrounding vicinity and environment/ institutions where the subproject can
affect receptors even if there are no direct interventions taking place there.


The geographical, biophysical environment, socio-economic/cultural and institutional
influences of the subproject are foremost identified and briefly described hereunder.


8.1.1 Geographical Area of Influence
The immediate geographical area of influence of the intervention is the Asafo and Abinkyi
communities and the neighboring suburbs e.g., Adum/ CBD, Amakom, Fante New Town and
Ridge/ Georgia Hotel environs.


8.1.2 Environmental Media to be Influenced
The main environmental media to be impacted are land (landscape), water and air. The
landscape features include soil, local flora, and fauna at the subproject sites. Air quality may
also be impacted by dust and gaseous emissions from equipment and machinery used at the
subproject site during the construction phase.


Runoff from Asafo and Abinkyi empties into the Subin River via drains in the communities
and thence to the Oda River and finally into the Ofin River, then to the Pra River, which


Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP                                                    138
subsequently enters the Atlantic Ocean/ Gulf of Guinea.           Management of solid wastes
(constructional waste), wastewater/ storm water etc. will be required to ensure that the
subproject area is not adversely impacted.


The environmental media to be influenced will include the Oti Landfill, which has recently
been reengineered with World Bank funding. The landfill was commissioned in January 2004,
under the Urban Environmental Sanitation Project (UESP I) financed by the World Bank.
There are three landfill phases at the site. The facility occupies 40 Ha of land. The site
reportedly receives 1200 – 1300 metric tons of waste per day. It is projected that the Oti
Sanitary Landfill Site has a total available capacity of 4.5 million m3 and will last until 2028.


Location
The Oti Landfill is an engineered sanitary landfill facility located at Oti near Dompoase on the
outskirts of Kumasi in the recently created Asokwa Municipality in the Ashanti Region of
Ghana (see Figure 16). The landfill is within 10km of the Central Business District (CBD) of
Kumasi.




Figure 16:        Location of the Oti Landfill in Kumasi


Access Roads to the Oti Landfill




Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP                                                    139
There are two main routes to the site. The first route is via the Bosumtwi Lakeside road and
the second route is through Kaase. Figure 17 shows the two routes and expected travel times.




Figure 17:        Access Roads to the Oti Landfill Site




The Management of the facility has confirmed that it has the capacity, in terms of space,
personnel and equipment, to receive and manage the construction waste that will be generated
at the various subproject sites.


8.1.3 Socio-economic Influence
The proposed interventions in Asafo and Abinkyi have a bearing on the economic and socio-
cultural conditions of the communities.



Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP                                              140
The direct impacts of the proposed works (e.g. nuisance of works, pollution) would be located
close to the area of construction works for the sewer network, Gee stream diversion and the
treatment ponds site.


Project Affected Persons (PAPs) include those who reside or do business in the communities
including squatters, petty traders/ business owners, auto mechanics operating in kiosks
/makeshift structures and transport operators. Businesses and livelihoods of these individuals
may be temporarily or permanently impacted.


The indirect impacts are felt in a wider area. For example, construction works on the Gee
stream channel will lead to reduced flooding in area and the wider Subin River channel.


8.1.4 Institutional and Organizational
The public institutions and other organizations to be involved in the proposed subproject
include:
 Ministry of Sanitation and Water Resources (MSWR);
 Environmental Protection Agency (EPA), Head Office and the Ashanti Region Office;
 Water Resources Commission (WRC);
 Kumasi Metropolitan Assembly (KMA);
 Lands Commission (LC);
 Ghana Water Company Limited (GWCL);
 Department of Urban Roads (DUR);
 Department of Factories Inspectorate (DFI);
 Electricity Company of Ghana (ECG);
 Ghana Grid Company (GRIDCo);
 Wildlife Division of the Forestry Commission of Ghana;
 Telecom companies- Vodafone, Airtel-Tigo and MTN, and
 Ghana National Fire Service (GNFS).


8.2      Specific Project Activities of Environmental and Social Concern


The subproject activities and interventions are grouped into four phases where there are
potential for environmental and social impacts. The phases are as follows:


Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP                                               141
 Pre-construction Phase;
 Construction Phase;
 Operational Phase; and
 Decommissioning Phase.


8.2.1 The Allotment of the Civil Works
The key construction works have been grouped into three lots as follows to ensure timely
completion of the works, which is estimated to last for 6 months:
Lot 1
 The activity under this Lot will concentrate on the existing sewer lines. It will involve
    Construction / Rehabilitation of Sewer Network and Manhole Related Works.


Lot 2
Under this Lot, the activities will involve the following:
 Desludging of Ponds and Rehabilitation of Connecting Chambers;
 Construction of 2No 30m x 70m Sludge Drying Bed; and
 Construction of 1No New Maturation Pond.


Lot 3
 Construction of 900mm Concrete U-Drain of Approximate Length of 890m;
 Stream Diversion and Construction of Covered Concrete Drain of Approximate Length of
    100m;
 Construction of Concrete Retaining Wall of Approximate Length of 900m to be located on
    the side of the treatment plant area abutting the current Gee stream channel (see Annex 5);
    and
 Construction of Perimeter Fence Wall of Approximate Length of 1.8km around the
    treatment facility location.


8.2.2 Preconstruction Phase
The activities to be carried out at the preparatory or pre-construction phase prior to the
implementation of the proposed subproject include:
 Clearing of the RoWs including site preparation, collection and disposal of solid wastes to
    make way for the actual construction and related activities;



Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP                                                142
 Relocation/resettlement of PAPs (through the implementation of the RAP which has been
    prepared for the proposed works) in order to give way for the construction activities;
 Mobilization of construction materials and equipment to the construction sites;
 Continued stakeholder engagement and sensitization activities;
 Statutory permitting activities; and
 Pegging the exact boundaries of the subproject construction activities; and
 Installation of appropriate signage at appropriate sections of the works areas.


8.2.3 Constructional Phase Activities
The major constructional phase activities to potentially impact on the biophysical and social
environments include the following among others:
 Clearing of the subproject area/sites;
 Excavation and civil works involving trenching to lay new sewer pipelines and dredging of
    existing sewage treatment ponds) ,
 Haulage of construction materials to the subproject sites; and
 Collection, transportation, and disposal of construction waste i.e., solid and liquid waste.
 Restoration of disturbed areas on the sewer lines
 Restoration of landscape at the treatment facility site
 Traffic management operations.
 Environmental and social impacts mitigation measures implementation and monitoring
    including health and safety measures, Gender Based Violence (GBV) and Code of Conduct
    for the construction workers.
 Grievance redress


8.2.4 Operational Phase Activities
The operational activities that have potential to result in environmental and social impacts
include the following:
 Safe handling and disposal of both influent and effluent flows in accordance with the
    applicable Ghana Standards;
 Environmental quality assurance/monitoring (effluent quality monitoring, water quality
    monitoring of the Subin River);
 Facility Maintenance and repair works;
 Materials management and storage;
 Provision of security services;


Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP                                                  143
 Solid waste management- to avoid blockage of drains; and
 Strom water/ runoff management.


8.2.5 Decommissioning Phase
The main decommissioning phase activities to potentially impact on the environment consist
of the following:
 Post-construction activities including the demobilisation of construction equipment,
      dismantling of construction site offices/ work camps, etc, and
 Post operational activities including abandoning or removal of sewer pipelines, sewage
      treatment ponds and flood management structures.


Decommissioning of the treatment ponds following the expiration of its design life or for
massive improvements may impact on public and occupational health and safety, noise and air
quality.


8.3      Impact Assessment/ Evaluation Approach


8.3.1 Impact Identification and Characterization
Impacts are described in terms of their characteristics, including the impact’s type and the
impact’s spatial and temporal features (extent, duration, scale and frequency). The definitions
of the terms used are described in Table 39.

8.3.2 Determining Impact Magnitude
Once an impact’s characteristics are defined, the next step in the impact assessment phase is to
assign each impact a ‘magnitude’. Magnitude is typically a function of some combination
(depending on the resource/receptor in question) of the following impact characteristics:
 Extent;
 Duration;
 Scale; and
 Frequency.


Magnitude (from small to large) is in practice a continuum, and evaluation along the spectrum,
requires the exercise of professional judgment and experience. Each impact is evaluated on a
case-by-case basis, and the rationale for each determination is noted. The universal magnitude
designations, for negative effects, are: negligible, small, medium and large. The magnitude
designations themselves are universally consistent, but the definition for the designations varies
by issue. In the case of a positive impact, no magnitude designation has been assigned as it is
considered sufficient for the purpose of the impact assessment to indicate that the subproject is
expected to result in a positive impact.


8.3.3 Determining Receptor Sensitivity
The other principal step necessary to assign significance for a given impact is to define the
sensitivity of the receptor. There are a range of factors to be taken into account when defining
the sensitivity of the receptor, which may be physical, biological, cultural or human. As in the
case of magnitude, the sensitivity designations themselves are universally consistent, but the




Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP                                                                       146
definitions for these designations will vary on a resource/receptor basis. The sensitivity of
receptor used is low, medium and high as shown in Table 40.


Table 40:         Sensitivity Criteria

  Value         / Low                          Medium                                  High
  Sensitivity

Biological and Species Value / Sensitivity Criteria

Criteria          Not protected or listed      Not protected or listed but may be      Specifically      protected
                  as common/ abundant;         a species common globally but           under Ghana legislation
                  or not critical to other     rare   in    Ghana     with    little   and/or         international
                  ecosystem       functions    resilience to ecosystem changes,        conventions e.g., CITES
                  (e.g., key prey species      important        to     ecosystem       listed as rare, threatened
                  to other species).           functions, or one under threat or       or endangered e.g., IUCN
                                               population decline.

                  adapt with relative ease difficulty and maintain pre-                able to adapt to changes
                  and maintain pre-impact impact status but only with a                and continue to maintain-
                  status.                      degree of support.                      pre-impact status.

                  pre-impact status.           time naturally or through specific      development.

8.5      Potential Negative/Adverse Environmental and Social Impact Evaluation


The potential negative/adverse impact evaluations for the preparatory/pre-construction,
construction, operational and decommissioning phases of subproject implementation are
assessed below.


8.5.1 Potential Adverse Environmental and Social Impact Evaluation for Pre-
Construction Phase
8.5.1.1 Subproject Land and RoW Usage
The proposed interventions to be undertaken under the ASSSPWP include the replacement of
about 1.1km of damaged sewers, (ii) construction of 10 new manholes/ chambers, demolition
and construction of 57 existing manholes/ chambers anew and 78 existing manholes and
chambers to be cleaned for rehabilitation, (iii) replacement and rehabilitation of Screening
Chamber, Anaerobic Pond 1, Anaerobic Pond 2, Facultative Pond, Maturation Pond and
construction of Sludge Drying Bed and Perimeter Fencing and (iv) the realignment of the “Gee
Stream” nearby the treatment plant. However, it is noteworthy that the subproject is an existing
one and the priority works activities are limited to the existing routes and sites whereby no new
land will be taken from private individuals. Moreover, to minimize the impacts on all project
affected persons on the right of way of the subproject, the PAPs have been consulted and
profiled for adequate, fair and prompt compensation for their losses, etc. in the Resettlement
Action Plan (RAP) prepared for the subproject.


The Impact Evaluation is summarized below:
                                  Subproject Land and RoW Usage
 Activity

 Right-of-Way acquisition

 businesses)

                                                     Major


8.5.1.2 Lack of Awareness and Anxiety on the Part of Potentially Affected Persons/
          Institutions and Facility Users
Inadequate dissemination of information with regards to the scope, schedule and impact of the
proposed subproject, as well as measures in place to safeguard the interests of commuters, users
of the facility and potentially affected persons could result in some resistance to the
implementation of the ASSSPWP. This could be expressed in terms of obstruction of workers
from carrying out their respective services, lack of cooperation from landowners/ RoW users
during RAP implementation activities, public demonstrations, and PAPs rushing to the
media/local FM stations to lodge complaints and be heard. Failure to reach an amicable
agreement with the PAPs could result in recurrence of these issues. To address this, an
extensive stakeholder consultation has been embarked on during the screening and preparation
of this ESIA. Further stakeholder engagement will be undertaken during the project
construction and operation to ensure continuous stakeholder awareness and information
exchange. This will include public disclosure of the ESIA to key stakeholders including project
community members and project affected persons.


The impact evaluation is summarised below:
                            Anxiety on the part of PAPs and Facility Users

8.5.1.3 Risk of not Acquiring all Relevant Permits for Subproject Implementation
The KMA with support from GAMA- SWP/GKMA- SWP is required to acquire all necessary
environmental permits and ‘No Objection”                  for implementation of the ASSSPWP from
relevant regulatory bodies and project financier (the World Bank) and the Water Resources
Commission.


The subproject has triggered EPA EAR 1999 (LI 1652) and World Bank safeguards policy on
Environmental Assessment Failure to acquire all these approvals/ permits will amount to
breach of national laws and World Bank safeguards policies the GAMA AF financing
agreement. However, the preparation of this ESIA is to respond to the regulatory requirements
of the proposed subproject.

8.5.1.4 Occupational Health & Safety Concerns
Exposure of technical teams carrying out topographical, geotechnical, and environmental
baseline surveys to stepping on sharp objects e.g., broken nails and bottles, and bites from
insects and dangerous reptiles e.g., snakes, scorpions and insects e.g., bees and ants is possible.
Also, the technical teams and contractor staff may be exposed to agitation from persons who
might have grievances about the project arrangements or the conduct/behaviour etc. of the



Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP                                                      153
project technical team on the ground. However, these impacts can be addressed or minimized.
Provision of appropriate PPEs to the technical teams and wearing the PPEs can assuage the
degree of harm on the affected personnel. Furthermore, the GAMA Project has a Grievance
Redress Mechanism (GRM) which it has successfully implemented to address grievances
amicably on its projects. This GMR will be applied to address grievances of project affected
persons on this subproject.


A summary of the risk evaluation is provided below.
                               Occupational Health & Safety Concerns

                                                  Moderate


8.5.1.5 Pre-construction Waste Generation and Management
Preconstruction will involve site preparation such as clearing and gathering of grass, pieces of
wood and metal remnants left behind by mechanics. Other waste materials to be encountered
during site preparation phase would include air-borne materials such as rubber sheet, food
packs, water sachet etc. If left behind, this waste could lead to obstructions at the project sites
and routes and can expose the subproject community people to harm and injury. However, the
waste can be managed through their collection and disposal to KMA approved Oti Landfill
site.

8.5.2 Potential Adverse Environmental and Social Impacts Evaluation for
Construction Phase
8.5.2.1 Air Quality Deterioration
The baseline air quality of the proposed subproject area was measured against GS values, WHO
guideline values and WBG EHS guideline values. The baseline TSP results were below the
GS of 150μgm-3 five (5) out of the seven (7) places monitored ranging from 11.2µg/m³ at
Treatment Plant Site close to the Facultative Pond to 223.2µg/m³ at the KsTU Entrance
Opposite IBS Print House, O. A. Street. Only the baseline PM10 result of the KsTU Entrance
Opposite IBS Print House, O. A. Street of 126.6µg/m³ was above the GS value of 70µgm-3 and
together with the STC Yard (68.3µg/m³), they were above the WBG EHS/ WHO guideline
value of 50µgm-3 – this is mainly because these sections are busy road corridors. The PM2.5
values ranged from 5.2µg/m³ at Treatment Plant Site close to the Facultative Pond to 47.5µg/m³
at the STC Yard compared with the GS 35µgm-3 and WBG EHS/ WHO guideline value of
25µgm-3. Noxious gases (NO2, SO2 and CO) emissions were all within the respective GS values
and the WHO guideline values.


At the sewer routes, the background air quality is likely to be affected negatively by
construction dust to be generated from digging and excavation of sewer lines routes and
unpaved surfaces, demolition of old structures e.g., manholes and chambers and gaseous
emissions from project construction machinery/equipment such as ramping machines, concrete
mixers and construction trucks delivering sand and stone, and dust spraying water. Loading,
haulage and dumping of sand/stone aggregates as well as cement handling will also generate
dust that can increase air borne particulates in the vicinity and along the transport routes. Wind-
blown dust may be generated from unpaved work sites, roads, exposed soils and material
stockpiles.


At the treatment facility rehabilitation site and perimeter fencing and drainage works site, dust
may emanate from movement of construction equipment such as excavators; haulage and
dumping of construction materials i.e., sand and stone; mixing of cement and sand during



Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP                                                    155
concrete works; and haulage of construction waste. Emissions may result from use of
improperly serviced/smoky or old construction machinery/equipment such as excavators,
concrete mixers and haulage trucks. Dust is the major air quality problem from construction
sites, and is a problem for a variety of reasons, including:


Dust could be a source of inconvenience for residents of proposed subproject areas along the
sewer areas and the treatment facility sites as well as the construction workers this may include
irritating eyes and worsening the health of people with asthma conditions.
 In large amounts, dust can reduce visibility for drivers on roads and other road uses. It can
    also be blown for long distances by wind; and cause aesthetic nuisance.


Use of construction vehicles, trucks and generators will generate fumes/gaseous emissions
from combustion of diesel engines of such equipment. Inhalation of fumes and gaseous
emissions such as carbon monoxide, sulphur oxides and nitrous oxides can affect the health of
persons exposed to these gases for prolonged periods.


The impact evaluation is summarised below:
                                      Air Quality Deterioration
 Activity
 Site clearance/ preparation, general construction, transportation of materials and equipment
 construction to and from construction sites, excavation, loading and dumping of aggregates/ soil/
 sand, demolition of old structures, construction waste disposal and operation of construction
 equipment/machinery.
 Impact characteristics

 Type              Duration        Extent     Frequency      Likelihood     Scale         Magnitude
 Direct,           Short-term      Local      Intermittent   Likely         Moderate      Medium

 Receptor                                                                                 Sensitivity
 subproject affected communities, offices/ businesses near the construction sites,        High
 construction workers; pedestrians etc.
                                          Significance of Impact
                                                 Major



Though major, the potential dust pollution impacts and risks can be managed by regular
dousing of the disturbed / dusty areas at the construction sites can suppressed the release of
dust into the atmosphere. When sand and stone in haulage or stored at construction sites are


Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP                                                             156
covered with tarpaulin would prevent release of dust. Again, construction workers should be
provided with nose masks to wear during dusty activities. Also, use of well-serviced
construction equipment (i.e. excavators, ramping machines, concrete mixers, haulage trucks
etc. ) will prevent or minimize excessive emissions into the atmosphere.


8.5.2.2 Vibration and Noise Nuisance
The baseline results of Equivalent Noise Levels (Leq) for the night time (2200hrs-0600hrs)
ranged from 39.7dB(A) at the KsTU Entrance Opposite IBS Print House, O. A. Street to
63.5dB(A) at the STC Yard compared with the GS value of 55dB(A) for a Mixed Use Area
while the daytime (2200hrs-0600hrs) results ranged from 47.2dB(A) at Treatment Plant Site
close to the Facultative Pond to 74.1dB(A) at the STC Yard. Thus, for the nighttime only the
KsTU Entrance Opposite IBS Print House, O. A. Street and at the entrance of the Treatment
Plant Site close to the Anaerobic Pond were compliant with the GS value of 55dB(A) and for
the daytime only the results for Treatment Plant Site close to the Maturation Pond, Treatment
Plant Site close to the Facultative Pond and at the entrance of the Treatment Plant Site close to
the Anaerobic Pond were compliant with the GS value of 60dB(A). The non-compliant ones
could attributable to the vehicular movement, noise from blowing sound systems and noise
from people. The use of construction equipment/ machinery and general construction activities
including masonry, carpentry, concrete mixing etc. will all generate noise of varying intensities
will thus exacerbate the noise levels in the subproject communities.


The impact evaluation is summarised below:
                                       Vibration and Noise Nuisance

 Excavation, construction and installation works in general and transportation activities- site
 preparation, transportation of materials/ equipment, piling, blowing of horns from vehicles/
 equipment etc.

Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP                                                            157
                                        Significance of Impact
                                               Major



Though major, it is expected that most of the excavation works will be undertaken by
manpower and also the excavation activities will be avoided during nightly hours to minimize
noise and vibration.


8.5.2.3 Loss of Vegetation/ Habitat and Impacts on Flora and Fauna
The areas where the existing sewer lines will be excavated and replaced is well developed and
urbanized and free of vegetation. However, the treatment facility site where the rehabilitation/
reconstruction activities will take place have weeds and sparse vegetation. There is GRIDCO
high- tension pylons traversing the site and as a result the vegetation in the RoW is well
controlled, which has further contributed to the low vegetative cover at the site. The vegetation
to be affected is generally grass found along the embankments of the existing ponds and the
“Gee” drains proposed for improvement works.


From the baseline ecological study carried out, most of the species (97.5%) recorded are of no
conservation concern, being mostly Green Star. The floristic analysis showed that only 1
threatened species i.e., Cedrela odorata was recorded during the survey and another species,
Mitragyna stipulosa, is listed as Near Threatened by IUCN (2023). All the species were either
Data Deficient, Least Concern or Not Assessed and do not merit conservation action globally.
Cedrela odorata is an exotic species introduced as forest plantation species in Ghana.
However, proposed works nor the working spaces required for the works are not near these
identified florals.


The clearing, excavation and construction activities will destroy/displace limited habitats of
fauna such as small mammals, rodents, reptiles, insects and nesting birds. No large mammals
will be affected.


During the construction works, all disturbed areas will be replanted to restore any vegetation
or trees lost and control erosion effects by the construction activities.


The impact evaluation is summarised below:




Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP                                                  158
 Loss of vegetation/ habitat and impacts on flora and fauna/ risk of spread of invasive plant
 species
 Activity
 Treatment ponds excavation, land preparation, drainage works, perimeter fencing works

 Impact characteristics

 Direct       Temporary     Local             Once           Likely       Minor    Small
 Receptor                                                                          Sensitivity
 Flora and fauna within RoW and other work sites                                   Medium
                                        Significance of Impact
                                                     Minor


8.5.2.4 Surface Water Pollution/ Siltation of Stream Channels and Impact on Aquatic
           Organisms and Biodiversity
Site preparation, comprising excavation works for the construction would result in loosening
and exposure of soil, which could facilitate erosion/ sediment transport via runoff into the
nearby Subin River leading to siltation and increased turbidity.


The Subin River and the Gee Stream are generally polluted, evidenced by indicative fauna such
as chironomids and polychaetes. Nevertheless, the presence of life from productive plankton
and plants all the way up the aquatic food chain to fish and birds, indicate that the water bodies
maintain some minimum ecological integrity. Disturbance of aquatic life will occur due to
excavation works and other construction activities that result sediment transport and
consequently increased turbidity and pollution.


Excavation works, desludging of the treatment ponds, Gee stream diversion and lining are
likely to release sediment contaminants and create benthic habitat loss in the Subin and Gee
stream temporarily. The diversion and lining of the Gee stream could hinder the movement of
some aquatic life.


Discharge of contaminated or polluted wastewater into the streams could adversely affect water
quality. Other impacts and risks include the improper disposal of wastes generated from the
construction works.         Cumulative impacts may occur due to pollution from municipal
wastewater discharges and surface runoffs.




Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP                                                      159
The impact evaluation is summarised below:
        Water Pollution/ Siltation of stream channels and impact on aquatic organisms and
                                               biodiversity
 Activity
 Excavation works, Gee stream diversion and lining; waste storage and disposal

 Impact characteristics

 Type          Duration       Extent          Frequency        Likelihood    Scale      Magnitude
 Direct and    Short-term     Regional        Intermittent     Likely        Moderate   Large
 cumulative
 Receptor                                                                               Sensitivity
 Gee stream, Subin River                                                                Medium
                                         Significance of Impact
                                                  Major



8.5.2.5 Flooding of Upstream Sections due to Diversion/ Blockage of Gee Stream
Diversion and blockage of the Gee stream and lining of the channel to join the Subin River
upstream of the treatment plant site could result in flooding upstream during increased surface
runoffs from rainfall events.

However, the diversion activities will not be carried out during stormy conditions, but rather
during low tide periods. Secondly storm drain will be construction as the carriage course for
the diversion and this will prevent land erosion and siltation effects from the embankment.




Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP                                                           160
8.5.2.6 Land Degradation and Impact on Soil Resources
The excavation works will be carried out in the RoWs already accommodating the sewer
pipelines and at the treatment plant site for the Gee stream diversion. Without adequate
protection measures, during heavy rainfalls and windy occasions, soil erosion could occur on
excavated sites for construction. Soil contamination will occur if spillages of fuel/oil occur
from construction equipment during the works. However, mitigation measures readily exist to
check land degradation and soil erosion and they will be applied i.e., pegging of active
construction areas, avoiding construction works during stormy conditions, backfilling and
regrassing of disturbed areas at the construction site, etc.

8.5.2.7 Waste Generation and Sanitation Concerns
Waste to be generated includes sludge/ spoil or excavated material from the sewer pipeline
RoWs, biomass from clearing activities within the RoW; general waste including food wastes,
water and food packaging/ containers, demolished concrete debris/paved materials, pieces of
pipes; waste from maintenance of equipment/machinery such as waste oils, scrap metals,
among others. The construction activities will lead to significant generation of solid waste. In
addition to solid waste, the construction workers will also generate liquid waste/ wastewater in
the form of human waste. The generation and management of both solid and liquid waste in an
urban built-up residential community is of significant sanitation concern.

8.5.2.8 Visual Intrusion/ Attraction
Site preparation and civil works/ general construction activities, movement of materials and
equipment/machines to and from subproject sites and the presence of vehicles, trucks,
construction / earthmoving machinery and equipment, and workers will attract the attention of
local residents.


Heaps of excavated materials, stockpiles of aggregates, sand and gravels, as well as the parking
of construction machinery and trucks will intrude into the visual quality of the Abinkyi and
Asafo areas.


Concrete batching plants and will have significant visual effects because of their size and
height.

Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP                                                                          162
8.5.2.9 Labor Influx
Job seekers, mainly unskilled youth and some skilled persons will throng the Asafo and
Abinkyi communities to look for employment and may end up engaging in illicit behaviors in
the communities as the character of these people may not be known.


This will put pressure on the existing social amenities and environmental sanitation resources
in the communities and could induce anti-social behaviors such sexual promiscuity leading to
early pregnancy among local teenagers, sexual exploitation and sexual harassment and open
defecation. However, during the employment of unskilled, labour from the local communities
will be prioritized rather than labour from outside the construction community. Also, no labour
camps will not be established to house workers during construction, rather the workers will go
to their various homes and return to work the next day.


Indirect labor influx will result from traders, especially women who will bring food/water and
goods to sell to construction workers. The traders would generate wastes and contribute to
sanitation issues in the communities.


The impact evaluation is summarized below:
                                              Labour Influx

8.5.2.10           Occupational Health, Safety and Labor issues
Workers will be exposed to risks during construction works. The risks include hazards from
operation of construction machinery/ equipment, transportation of construction materials,
inhalation of dust and fumes, noise from machinery, accidents from falling objects, cuts, slips,
fall from high heights, falling into the ponds/drowning in ponds etc.



Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP                                                         163
Unhygienic working conditions, discriminatory practices, engagement of child labor could
bring about social and labor conflicts and may trigger labor rights concerns.


Poor management of waste and improper housekeeping could significantly affect safety in the
workplace.


The improper handling of hazardous materials such as lubricants is also a health threat to
workers.


There is also risk of exposing the workers to dangerous reptiles such as snakes. However,
construction workers will be provided with appropriate protective equipment (PPE) such as
hard boots, gloves, hard hats, etc. to wear to safeguard their health and safety during the works.
Furthermore, the contractor will be required to prepare and implement their health and safety
strategy for the works which will be enforced by the supervising consultant, KMA and GAMA
PCU.

8.5.2.11          Public/ community health, safety and security
Dust and exhaust fumes and noise nuisance
Residents, businesses, traders, and RoW users may be exposed to dust and exhaust fumes from
excavations and other construction works and vehicular/equipment movement especially
during the dry season.




Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP                                                        164
Persons in the subproject communities, particularly those close to the construction sites would
be exposed to noise nuisance and vibration from construction works and operation of
equipment and machinery.


Exposure to Hazards Such as Excavations of the Sewer Pipelines
Persons in the subproject communities, particularly those close to the construction sites would
be exposed to hazards from excavation works of chambers/manholes and sewer pipelines.
People may get hurt from slipping and falling into the excavated trenches.


Spread of HIV/AIDS and other Sexually Transmitted Diseases (STDs)
Construction activities may result in the movement of workers, mainly able-bodied young men,
to the subproject area in search of job opportunities. The influx of these workers during the
construction period may promote irresponsible sexual behaviour which could lead to teenage
pregnancies, HIV/AIDS and other STD infections. The impact may be permanent or
irreversible in nature.


Public Health Issues
Poor sanitation conditions at the subproject sites and open defecation by construction workers
at obscure places will pollute the environment and ultimately the Subin River if adequate toilet
facilities are not provided for construction workers.
Improperly covered trenches or excavations may result in stagnant water which could serve as
breeding grounds for water-borne disease vectors such as mosquitoes.


Unsecured excavations/ trenches may compromise public/ community safety.


Security concerns
The influx of labour into the subproject communities will be a security concern for local
communities. Thieves may take advantage as job seekers to also come into the subproject
communities to steal or rob residents and workers.

etc.


8.5.2.12          Traffic Impacts on Asafo and Abinkyi Roads
The proposed subproject has a good and well-planned layout of road network providing access
for vehicles and pedestrians. The road network evaluation comprised Yaa Asantewaa Road,
O.A. Street, Subinso Road and Osei Tutu I Avenue. The intersections assessed comprised Yaa
Asantewaa Road / O.A. Street intersection and Osei Tutu I Avenue / Subinso Road intersection.
The traffic assessment confirmed the excessive traffic on the Osei Tutu I Avenue and the
slowness of the traffic at the Osei Tutu I Avenue / Subinso Road intersection, which leads to
the treatment plant site. Similar left turning slowness of traffic exist on the Yaa Asantewaa
Road / O.A. Street intersection. Hence the disruption of the existing traffic during the execution
of the works must be managed to reduce delays and congestion. Accordingly, the contractors
will be required to prepare traffic management plan which must be adapted to the proposed
traffic management plan issues provided in the ESMP to address traffic impacts during the
works.


The impact evaluation is summarized below:
                      Traffic impacts on major roads and major intersections
 Activity
 Trenching of Sewer Lines and Chambers/ Manholes and Works at the Sewage Treatment Plant site

8.5.2.13          Disruption of Utility Service Supplies
There may be buried utility service lines for residents within the RoWs that will not be noticed
and could be damaged by the sewer pipeline trenching works. The excavations around and
construction of manholes/ chambers may affect water supply pipelines and telephone/
electricity supply poles/ wires in the right of way, which serves a residence, business and may
require relocation during the construction phase. This would result in a break in the supply of
the utility be it telephone, water or electricity to the affected businesses or residents. On this
point, the client and contractors will collaborate with the utility service companies during the
construction period to ensure adequate protection, appropriate relocation where the need be
and an uninterrupted utility service provision.


The impact evaluation is summarized below:

infringed on during the construction. It is however possible for the contractor to break a taboo
in the community knowingly or unknowingly.

8.5.2.15          Presence of Asbestos Containing Materials (ACMs)
Asbestos refers to a family of six naturally occurring silicate minerals that are fibrous in
character.     Asbestos Containing Materials (ACMs) are employed mainly for insulation,
fireproofing materials, automobile brake pad lining and for roofing and pipes due to its strength
and resistance to heat. The use of ACMs has sharply declined in recent years due to the
recognition that it is a human carcinogen.


ACM pipes and materials were not deployed in the construction of the existing sewerage
system. Furthermore, ACMs have not been sighted in the subproject communities during the
assessment. The existing sewer pipelines to be excavated for rehabilitation consist a network
of PVC pipes while the sewer manholes and chambers were also made from standard materials
i.e., cement, chippings, sand and water. The proposed rehabilitation/replacement of the
existing defective sewer lines and sewer manholes and chambers will involve µPVC/HDPE
pipes, and cement, chippings, sand and water for the reconstruction of the sewer manholes and
chambers. However, in the unlikely event that some ACMs are encountered during the
excavation works, the project’s response will include immediate notification of the
Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) through whom certified asbestos removal experts
who possess the necessary training and equipment to safely handle ACM would be engaged to
address the issue.


The impact evaluation is summarized below:
                                          Presence of ACMs
 Activity



Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP                                                       168
 Excavations

 Impact characteristics

 Type         Duration      Extent        Frequency       Likelihood    Scale         Magnitude
 Direct       Short-term    Local         Intermittent    Unlikely      minimal       Small
 Receptor                                                                             Sensitivity
 local communities                                                                    High
                                        Significance of Impact
                                                 Minor



8.5.2.16          Emergency Events such as Workplace flooding and fires, personal and
           vehicular accidents and leakage/spills of sewage due to damage of sewer pipes
Flooding, fires, spillage of concrete and aggregates, spillage of sewage due to breakage or
damage of sewer lines during construction, personal and vehicular accidents can occur at the
subproject sites. There are drains near the subproject sites (roadside drains, and Subin/Gee
stream). The subproject area and Kumasi in particular is known to experience heavy rainfalls
during the rainy season. During heavy downpours or rain, these drains can overflow their banks
and flood the workplaces and disturb construction activities. Excavation/trenching activities
will be undertaking along the existing sewer lines, and if care is not taken, sections of the
existing sewer lines could be damaged causing leakage of sewage into soils and water bodies.
The use of construction machinery, presence of fuel, and misconduct of workers such as
improper disposal of cigarette butts after smoking, or creating naked fire or burning at or near
the construction sites can cause unfriendly fires. Vehicular accidents can occur during transport
of materials to the project sites and materials such as concrete or aggregates can spill and affect
traffic or block drains. Workplace and vehicular accidents can cause serious injurious to
workers or medical emergencies for that matter.


The impact assessment summary is provided below:
 Emergency events such as workplace flooding, workplace fires, personal and vehicular accidents, leakage of

handling of pond contents leading to sewage spills

           Communities
Failure of the treatment plant would result in emissions of bad odour and kill of fish and other
aquatic organisms inhabiting especially the maturation ponds.


The occurrence of natural disasters such as earthquakes/ tremors may be unlikely due to the
location of Kumasi. Although flooding may be a possibility, its occurrence may be unlikely
due to the flood mitigation measures provided at the treatment plant site as part of the
ASSSPWP.

8.5.3.2 Deterioration of Surrounding Air Quality Due to Odor
The operation of the treatment plant would generate gaseous emissions: Carbon Monoxide
(CO); Nitrogen Oxides (NOX); hydrocarbons (HC); Sulphur Dioxide (SO2); Carbon Dioxide
(CO2); Ammonia (NH3), Hydrogen Sulphide (H2S) and Particulate Matter (PM). These


Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP                                                             170
compounds can damage human health and/or the environment. The baseline air quality
assessment at the treatment plant site showed that:
 Noxious gases (NO2, SO2 and CO) emissions were all within the respective GS values and
    the WHO guideline values.
 PM concentrations were all within the respective GS values and the WHO/ WBG EHS
    guideline values.
 Opening of Chambers/ manholes for maintenance work may generated odor that would
    impact nearby homes and businesses as well as passersby.

Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP                                                           175
8.5.4.4 Loss of Employment
Consultants and individuals who worked on the subproject during the preparatory phase will
have to look for new jobs. The estimated labour force of 50 - 75 people to be involved during
the peak construction period will lose their jobs as such engagements are temporary. All
labourers will be affected.

8.5.5 Potential Adverse Impact Evaluation – Post Operational Phase Decommissioning
8.5.5.1 Loss of job/ Employment
Staff (circa 10) to be engaged during the operation and maintenance phase will be laid off after
operation/ maintenance and decommissioning activities are over, and will include all
maintenance contractors and consultants.

                                        Significance of Impact
                                              Moderate



8.5.5.2 Other impacts
Decommissioning if it happens will occur in the distant future when baseline conditions and
the subproject facilities would have changed or modified. A detailed ESIA will be carried out
for approval and permitting by the KMA or any other entity before the final decommissioning
of the facilities to confirm the significance of all identified impacts. The major recipients of
impacts will include the bio-physical and social environments, Asafo and Abinkyi
communities, subproject workers and the general public.




Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP                                                 177
9.0      PROPOSED ENHANCEMENT AND MITIGATION MEASURES FOR THE
POTENTIAL            ENVIRONMENTAL            AND    SOCIAL      IMPACTS        AND      RISKS
IDENTIFIED


This chapter presents a description of enhancement measures for the positive impacts and the
various mitigation and management measures for adverse impacts and risks, which were
characterized as moderate and major in chapter 8. These measures cover the preconstruction,
construction, operations and maintenance, and decommissioning phases of the proposed
ASSSPWP.


9.1      Proposed Enhancement of Potential Positive Impacts


The implementation of the proposed ASSSPWP will create employment opportunities for some
Ghanaians in general and residents of Asafo and Abinkyi in particular. The contractor(s) will
put in measures to enhance local employment and business opportunities in the subproject area.
The contractor will continue with the stakeholder engagement and involvement programmed
during the construction phase to enhance better relationships between the contractor(s) and the
community.


The KMA/ GAMA-SWP PCU will encourage the subproject contractor(s) to adopt a
recruitment policy that is geared towards giving priority to residents of the Asafo and Abinkyi
communities in its recruitment of labor. Employment of the youth of the Asafo and Abinkyi
communities would be prioritized in the hiring of unskilled labor. The recruitment policy will
seek to promote gender equality and guard against GBV/ SEA/ SH. Qualified women will not
be discriminated against and would be encouraged to apply for suitable vacancies when they
become available.


The positive impacts of the proposed ASSSPWP on the local and national economy will
continue to be sustained through sourcing of materials and equipment from local or Ghanaian
suppliers and employment of local subcontractors if required in the delivery of the
interventions. With improvement in sanitation in the communities, a healthy and conducive
environment would be created for individuals and organizations to operate their businesses
efficiently to make profit and to halt the pollution of the Subin and the annual flood events.



Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP                                                  178
9.2      Proposed Adverse Environmental and Social Impacts Mitigation And
Management


This section presents a description of various mitigation and management measures for
adverse environmental and social impacts which were identified as moderate and major in the
preceding chapter. These measures cover the construction, operations and maintenance, and
decommissioning phases of each of the proposed subproject under each of the 3 Lots which
are to be managed by separate contractors i.e., sewer pipelines route/ chambers and manholes
rehabilitation (Table 43); rehabilitation of treatment ponds (Table 44); and perimeter fencing
and flood protection (Table 45).


Separate mitigation and monitoring plans are provided for each of the three (3) Lots subproject
components of the sewerage treatment system rehabilitation priority project. The decision to
provide separate mitigation plans for each of the three components rather than a single
common mitigation plan, is based on several key considerations:
 By providing separate mitigation plans, the plan tailors the mitigation measures to the
      specific risks and challenges faced by each contractor;
 A separate mitigation plan for each component enables more effective monitoring and
      compliance. It allows for targeted assessment of the environmental and social impacts
      relevant to each contractor's activities, facilitating better oversight and ensuring that
      mitigation measures are properly implemented and effective;
 It prevents important issues from being overlooked or overshadowed by broader concerns
      that may be addressed in a single, overarching mitigation plan. Separating the mitigation
      plans ensures clear accountability for each contractor. It delineates their respective
      responsibilities regarding environmental and social mitigation in the ESIA Report,
      reducing ambiguity and potential disputes over who is responsible for addressing specific
      impacts; and
 Separate mitigation plans allow for greater adaptability and flexibility in responding to the
      evolving needs and circumstances of each component of the project. It enables adjustments
      to mitigation measures based on ongoing monitoring and feedback, ensuring that
      environmental and social risks are effectively managed throughout the project lifecycle.

Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP                                                                                                     286
10.0      PROVISIONAL ENVIRONMENTAL AND SOCIAL IMPACTS MONITORING
          PLAN

10.1      Environmental Monitoring Plan
The monitoring of various environmental and social parameters will help to confirm any predicted
impact or otherwise and assess the effectiveness of the implementation of the mitigation measures
outlined. By way of monitoring, a change in a predicted impact can be reviewed. Where observed
impact levels exceed the expected levels, additional appropriate mitigation measures will then be
instituted. Monitoring will also identify and confirm any residual impacts, which are normal with
the development of such a subproject and ensure that these do not escalate to significant adverse
levels.


10.1.1 Monitoring Objectives
The KMA/ GAMA PCU is committed to ensuring effective protection of the environment, the
construction sites, workers and the general public. The objectives of the monitoring program are
to:
  i.   Confirm any predicted impact or otherwise made from the environmental and social
       assessment during subproject implementation;
 ii.   Ensure that all mitigation and control measures are operating efficiently and with desired
       effect;
iii.   Provide information to develop improved practices and procedures for environmental
       protection, community health/safety and worker safety, if necessary;
iv.    Detect changes in the receiving environment and enable analysis of their causes; and
 v.    Enable effective liaison with stakeholders and community members, including addressing
       complaints and concerns.


The Plans are also expected to provide useful guidance for the successful planning and
implementation of similar projects that will be undertaken by the GAMA Project, KMA and other
MMAs.


10.1.2 Environmental Monitoring Program
A monitoring programme will be instituted and carried out and relevant records will be kept to
ensure compliance with sound environmental and social practices. The major environmental and
social issues for which monitoring will focus on include:


Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP                                                   287
 Ambient       air quality, in conformity with the Ghana Standards on Ambient air Quality
   Requirements GS 1236: 2019;
 Ambient noise levels, in conformity with the Ghana Standards on Ambient Noise Control

   Requirements GS 1212:2018
 Effluent Quality, in conformity with the Ghana Standards on Effluent Discharge Requirements

   GS:1212: 2019
 Surface water quality;

 Loss of vegetation/habitat and impact on fauna;

 Waste generation and disposal;

 Use of personal protective equipment (PPE);

 Accidents, worker injury and health/safety;

 Labour issues such as engaging underage persons and labour influx at construction sites/local

   communities;
 Emergency Flooding.

 Community/ public safety/health/ security and traffic;

 RoW issues/security; and

 Stakeholder engagement and public/community complaints.



The environmental monitoring program/Plan for each of the subproject components in Lot 1, Lot
2 and Lot 3 is set out in Table46, Table 47 and Table 48 respectively.
