Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP                                                                                                                         408
10.1.3 Key Responsibilities for the Monitoring Program
The primary responsibility for implementation of the environmental and social monitoring
program during the construction phase is the Contractor. The Contractor will be required to
hire an Environment, Health and Safety (EHS) Officer or Manager (Environmental and Social
Safeguards Officer) responsible for implementing the EHS mitigation and management
actions, including the EPA permit schedule of conditions and other lending covenants.


Through the Safeguards Officer, the Contractor must ensure day-to-day monitoring of all
environmental and social impacts mitigation measures implementation by the workers and
provide regular standalone bi-weekly safeguards monitoring reports to the Client including
violation of any approved procedures i.e., Code of Conduct, etc.


All the reports should capture progress and status on implementation of the mitigation measures
arising from the ESIA implementation, considering the monitoring indicators but not limited
to the following:
 Contractors’ performance on implementing environmental and social safeguards;
 Progress on mitigation measures in relation to identified risks and impacts;
 Emerging impacts and proposed mitigation measures (if encountered);
 A presentation on parameters monitored in the reporting period;
 Activities to be taken in the following period;
 Capacity building needs that may be required
Relevant pictures should be included in the report.


Supervising Consultant
A supervising consultant to be employed by the GAMA-PCU will provide oversight
responsibility to ensure that the contractor is fulfilling his mitigation measures implementation
responsibility under the ESIA. The contractor will submit his/her monitoring reports through
the supervising consultant who will validate and forward these to the PCU and KMA through
the GAMA Safeguards Specialists. The supervising consultant will coordinate the day-to-day
monitoring of the implementation of the Client’s ESIA, Permits, and the contractors’
safeguards commitment documents.


GAMA Safeguards Specialists and KMA Safeguards Focal Point




Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP                                                  409
GAMA-PCU and KMA Safeguards Focal Person will regularly conduct monitoring field visits
to the construction sites to inspect activities and verify the reports presented by the contractor
and supervising consultant and make their findings. They will provide guidance for any
remedial actions where the need be to prevent non-compliance and recurrence of inaction on
the part of any stakeholder. The ESIA monitoring results will be continuously evaluated by the
GAMA PCU as part of the project supervision and this will allow for corrective actions to be
taken when needed. The PCU will compile a regular safeguard monitoring reports for
submission to the World Bank and EPA.


A Joint/ Common Coordination Committee of the 3 separate contractors
Recognizing that the 3 components of the priority works i.e., 1) replacement of defective sewer
pipelines and sewer manholes 2) desludging and rehabilitation of the existing sewerage
treatment ponds, and 3) fencing of the perimeter of the treatment ponds, are not situated too far
distance apart and that their construction periods may coincide at some point during
construction phase, KMA and GAMA will constitute a common committee for the three
separate contractors involved in rehabilitating the sewerage treatment system to facilitate
coordination, communication and               improved project outcomes. The committee will be
constituted by three representatives from each contractor. The members will include the
Contractor, Project Engineer, Safeguards Officer, together with the Client’s representatives
comprising the Safeguards Specialists, Engineers and Supervising Consultant, the committee
will focus on the following key issues:
 Hold regular meetings where representatives from each contractor and the client can
    discuss progress, challenges, and coordination efforts.
 Ensure that schedules, activities, and resource requirements are synchronized to minimize
    conflicts and maximize efficiency.
 Review and ensure that all contractors are adhering to the environmental and social
    management plans developed for their respective components.
 Share best practices, lessons learned, and strategies for mitigating potential impacts on the
    environment and local.
 Identify and assess potential risks and impacts that may arise from the interaction of the
    three components.
 Develop strategies to mitigate and manage risks effectively, including emergency response
    protocols and contingency plans.




Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP                                                   410
 Coordinate community engagement efforts to ensure consistent messaging and outreach
    across all project components.
 Address community concerns, feedback, and grievances in a unified manner to enhance
    transparency and trust
 Establish common health and safety protocols and standards to promote a safe working
    environment for all workers involved in the project.
 Share information on safety incidents, near misses, and lessons learned to enhance safety
    performance across the project.


By addressing these key issues through a common committee or regular coordination
meetings, the GAMA PCU and KMA will foster collaboration, synergy, and alignment
among the contractors involved in rehabilitating the sewerage treatment system and ensure an
enhanced environmental and social performance across all the components of the subproject,
and greater overall efficiency in project implementation.


Although the primary responsibility for the implementation of the monitoring program during
the construction phase is the Contractor, it is preferable that the Contractor engages an
independent specialist or company to undertake the air quality, noise level and water/
wastewater quality monitoring to ensure objectivity of the results/recommendations.


The KMA has the primary responsibility for environmental and social monitoring during the
operational and maintenance phase of the ASSSPWP.


During the construction and operational phases, the EPA will periodically carry out site visits
and review monitoring reports received from the ASSSPWP to verify compliance with the
monitoring program and its schedule to the environmental permit conditions.


10.2     Contractors Environmental and Social Safeguards Commitment Strategy


Documents Requirements
Prior to their selection and commencement of the construction works and in line with
international good practices, the Contractors will be required to prepare a few safeguards
commitment documents which will provide their (contractors) specific strategies for dealing
with the potential environmental and social impacts mitigation requirements (for the works



Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP                                                411
they will be responsible for) as proffered in the this ESIA document. The documents shall be
reviewed and assessed for their adequacy by the supervising consultant. Together with the
ESIA, the contractors’ strategies will be monitored for their compliance during the construction
period. The strategies shall describe the resources allocated to and the personnel responsible
for the execution of each task and requirements contained therein, roles and responsibilities of
key construction staff including construction supervisors, the EHS Officer/ and the Subproject
Manager in the monitoring and management of key environmental and social impacts
mitigation activities. These documents are outlined below:


10.2.1 Contractor’s Health and Safety Plan/Strategy
The contractor’s health and safety plan will provide information on the contractor’s procedures
relating to occupational health and safety of his workers and public health and safety for the
work he/she is responsible for under the contract. The Plan shall be guided by the World
Bank’s Health and Safety Guidelines as well as the Ghana Factories, Offices and Shops Act
328 (1970), etc.
The Contractor shall appoint an Environment Social Health and Safety (ESHS) Manager for
the ASSSPWP, who will report directly to the Project Manager (PM). The responsibilities of
the ESHS Manager include, among other things:
 Implement the environmental, health and safety measures on the subproject.
 Enforce the environmental permit conditions and mitigation, monitoring and management
    measures.
 Liaise with the PM to ensure all required PPEs, waste bins and other logistics are provided
    for the works;
 Identify appropriate training programs in ESHS for the workers.
 Ensure all machinery and equipment are in good working condition and are well serviced;
 Ensure all operators adhere to environment, health and safety procedures;
 Liaise with regulatory institutions such as EPA on all ESHS matters relating to the
    execution of the proposed subproject;
 Keep records and reports of all incidents/accidents and illnesses.
 Report all complaints from the community and other stakeholders/workers concerning
    environmental, social, health and safety issues to the PM;
 Report all non-compliances of environmental, social, health and safety procedures to the
    PM for appropriate action; and




Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP                                                 412
 Enforce disciplinary actions against workers who don’t comply with health and safety
    procedures.


10.2.2 Contractor’s Environmental and Social Management Plan (C-ESMP)
The C-ESMP shall be based on this ESIA with a focus on construction activities. The C-ESMP
shall, among other things, identify the construction phase activities, risks/impacts, take into
consideration the mitigation and monitoring measures and their management arrangements
captured in the ESIA, describe resource allocation and assign roles and responsibilities for the
execution of each task.


10.2.3 Contractor’s Waste Management Plan
The contractor’s Waste Management Plan should cover both solid and liquid waste that will be
generated or encountered to be managed during the construction activities to ensure
environmental protection and a clean environment. The Plan should include specific
procedures for tracking of loads, disposal sites and protocols for the maintenance of records of
the quantities of wastes generated, reused, and disposed.


10.2.4 Contractor’s Traffic Management Plan
The Contractor’s Traffic Management Plan should address keys issues including but not
limited to strategies for ensuring safety of workers, pedestrians, and motorists while
minimizing disruptions to traffic flow within the construction corridors. The Plan should
identify and establish safety zones for workers, pedestrians, and vehicles, clearly marked areas
with signage and barriers to prevent unauthorized access, designation of alternative
routes/walkways, temporary pedestrian crossings, and clear signage indicating alternative
routes among others.


The Traffic Management Plan presented hereunder is to be adopted by the subproject
contractors.


Introduction
Traffic management plan for the subproject is the way of minimizing the interface wherever
possible between public and site traffic, and reducing the number of deliveries as practicable,
including the staging of deliveries such that the volume of traffic is kept as even as possible
avoiding peaks and controlling vehicular movements.



Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP                                                 413
Daily, the roads in the subproject area are always busy with vehicular and pedestrian
movement, making it necessary to put in place plans to manage the traffic during construction
to avoid accidents. Therefore, practical guidelines for planning these issues are highlighted for
consideration and required actions.


Signs/ Flagging
Road signs alongside flaggers will guide the flow of traffic during the works by the contractor
such as thrust boring or road cutting to make way for the laying of the sewer pipelines. In a
situation where there is the need for thrust boring the effect of the construction to traffic will
be minimal since the thrust boring will be done at the side of the road. However, there will be
the need to block part of the road when there is the need to cut the road and this will be possible
by diverting or blocking one side of the road which will result in traffic hence the need for road
signs and flaggers to help in giving direction to the motorists and the public, show them the
diversion directions and control traffic at construction zones. They will play an imperative role
in the awareness of traffic, and they will always be observant for moving plant and machinery
on site. At least two flaggers will be provided at each traffic control point.


The flaggers will be highly visible from a distance to permit appropriate response to the
flagger’s instructions by a vehicle operator. They will wear work suits and reflective vests for
high visibility to road users.


Vehicle Speed
A general speed limit of 20km/h will be displayed to direct road users to reduce their speed to
when using the Road during construction to avoid accidents.


Alternative Routes
For the treatment plant site, the alternative route proposed for use is a parallel route to the
Subinso road that goes through the Abinkyi Market at the north side and for the KsTU area,
the alternative route suggested is the Br I B Asafu-Adjaye Street that is parallel to the O.A
street.


10.2.5 Contractor’s Code of Conduct/Ethics
The company’s Code of Ethics/Conduct shall contain obligations on the company to foster a
well-organized, respectful, and collaborative environment at the workplace and in the project



Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP                                                    414
communities during the period of the contract. Code of Conduct shall be provided to include
but not limited to the following:
i.         Compliance with applicable laws, rules, and regulations of the jurisdiction;
ii.        Compliance with applicable health and safety requirements (including wearing
           prescribed personal protective equipment (PPE), preventing avoidable accidents and
           at duty to report conditions or practices that pose a safety hazard or threaten the
           environment.
iii.       Zero tolerance to the use of illegal substances (such as alcohol and narcotics during
           working hours);
iv.        Non-Discrimination (e.g. on the basis of family status, ethnicity, race, gender,
           religion, language, disability, or political conviction);
v.         Attitude of respect and non-discrimination during interactions with community
           members (e.g. to convey an attitude of respect and non- discrimination);
vi.        Sexual harassment, sexual exploitation (e.g. to prohibit use of language or behavior,
           in particular towards women or children, that is inappropriate, harassing, abusive,
           sexually provocative, demeaning or culturally inappropriate);
vii.       Violence or exploitation (e.g. the prohibition of the exchange of money, employment,
           goods, or services for sex, including sexual favors or other forms of humiliating,
           degrading or exploitative behavior);
viii.      Protection of children (including prohibitions against abuse, defilement, or otherwise
           unacceptable behavior with children, limiting interactions with children, child labor
           and ensuring their safety in project areas);
ix.        Protection and proper use of property (e.g., to prohibit theft, carelessness or waste);
x.         Duty to report violations of this Code;
xi.        No retaliation against workers who report violations of the Code, if that report is
           made in good faith.
xii.       The Code must include sanctions against violations of the Code
xiii.      Provision that all Company Managers and individual construction workers shall sign
           the Code of Conduct Declaration Form as below:


       Code of Conduct Declaration Form
       I hereby acknowledge receipt of my copy of the Contractor’s code of ethics/conduct which
       has been written in plain language and explained to me. I acknowledge that adherence to
       this Code of ethics/conduct is a condition of my employment and I understand that violation



Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP                                                      415
    of this code can result in serious consequences, up to and including dismissal, referral to
    legal authorities, forfeiting payments, termination of contract and eventually may have
    debarments implications.
    •    Name of Employee
    •    Name………………………………………………..…………….….…….Position
    •    Signature………………………………………………….………………
         Date…………………………………………….


In addition to the Contractor’s own Code of Ethics/Conduct, he/she shall adopt wholly and
implement the requirements of the Client’s own Code of Conduct for Preventing Gender-
Based Violence (GBV)/Sexual Exploitation and Abuse (SEA)/Sexual Harassment (SH) and
Violence Against Children (VAC) provided in Annex 6 of this document. All requirements
therein are obligatory and bidding on the Contractor under the contract of the works he/she is
responsible for.


10.3     Environmental Committee for the Operational Phase


The KMA will constitute an environmental and safety committee comprising staff from its
relevant departments/units including the planning and waste management departments and
representatives of relevant regulatory institutions such as EPA, WRC and DUR. The main task
of this committee is to formulate and implement policies to address environmental and social
impacts during the operational phase.


10.4     Annual Environmental and Safety Audit and Reviews


The KMA in collaboration will undertake an annual environmental, social, health and safety
audit of the ASSPWP interventions. Issues or gaps identified will be referred and for redress
by the Assembly. The Assembly will also carry out quarterly reviews of its environmental
performance. The monitoring program will also provide relevant information for effective
auditing and reviews.


10.5     Compliance with Statutory Obligations


The KMA and/or the Service Provider will comply with all relevant statutory obligations



Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP                                                416
including:
  i.   Obtaining an Environmental Permit from the EPA, through submission of this ESIA and
       paying the relevant processing and permit fees.
 ii.   Compliance with the schedule of conditions attached to the EPA’s environmental permit
       for the ASSSPWP including.
        a. Submitting necessary monthly/quarterly monitoring reports to EPA or as provided
             in the permit schedule to be issued.
        b. Submitting an Annual Environmental Report (AER) of the ASSSPWP activities
             after 12 months from the commencement of works in accordance with Regulation
             25 of LI 1652.
iii.   Obtaining a WRC Stream Diversion Permit.
iv.    Complying with the requirements of KMA by-laws.


Submission of ESMP During Operations By KMA
The KMA will submitting an Environmental and Social Management Plan (ESMP) of the
ASSSPWP activities to the EPA within 18 months of commencement of operations in
accordance with Regulation 24 of LI 1652.


The KMA and Contractor will also be required to comply with all WB monitoring and reporting
requirements.


10.6     Document Control and Tracking


Documentation
KMA will keep records on all environmental, health and safety (EHS) data including,
environmental emergencies. EHS data will be kept in both electronic and hard copy formats.
A format for documentation of information in electronic form will be developed to capture
daily/weekly information on environmental sampling/monitoring, environmental quality
results, waste generation and disposal, environmental incidences and emergencies, training and
awareness creation programs such as community fora/ workshops and meetings.


Document Tracking and Control
KMA will establish and maintain procedures to control all documents and permits that are
required to ensure compliance and to make sure that:



Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP                                               417
      All documents and permits are easily traceable;
      All statutory documents are periodically reviewed, revised as necessary and approved as
       adequate by the relevant regulatory agencies;
      All permits and approvals are renewed as and when necessary; and
      Current versions of relevant documents are available on site.


10.7      Subproject Facilities Management


The facilities will be maintained and managed in accordance with international good practices.
The potential environmental/technical concerns that will be addressed include:
  i.    quality of the constructional materials to ensure the safety and security of their usage;
 ii.    provision of quality assurance backstopping by the Supervising Engineer/ consultant; and
iii.    response to emergencies.


Measures adopted to manage these concerns include:
  i.    Monthly Inspections and Damage Control;
 ii.    Quality Assurance and Security; and
iii.    Provisional Emergency Response Action.


Monthly Inspections and Damage Control
The ASSSPWP team made up of KMA will carry out its monthly inspections with the aim of
correcting any defects at every stage of construction.


Quality Assurance
In order to avoid any substandard construction works, all materials/facilities will be pretested
to the required standard before approval for installation/ construction.


Housekeeping
A disorderly or dirty workplace can introduce its own hazards in addition to those associated
with the construction works. Good housekeeping is the first principle of health, safety and fire
prevention. The following measures will be ensured to improve housekeeping at all work sites:
  i.    All areas will be kept clear of non-essential equipment and materials;
 ii.    All equipment will be kept clean and leakages sealed;




Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP                                                     418
iii.        Management will ensure that all equipment and materials are in their assigned place and
            that no loose or unnecessary tools are left lying about in the workplace; and
iv.         Caution notices such as “Do Not Litter” will be provided at vantage areas.


10.8         Continued Public Participation


The doors of KMA will always be opened to the general public for complaints, suggestions
and advice on environmental and social related issues and they will be quickly addressed.


10.9         Emergency Response Action


Responsibility
The proposed emergency response plan for the construction phase is provided. Emergency
situations will be managed by the EHS Unit of the Contractor. The Subproject Manager or
Supervisors can declare an emergency and inform the EHS Manager. The EHS Manager after
review of a situation can also declare an emergency in consultation with the Subproject
Manager or Supervisors.


Emergency Service Providers and Communication Channels
After declaration of an emergency, the following organizations or emergency service providers
will be notified immediately, depending on the type of emergency:
       i.    GNFS- call 999 or 192
   ii.       EPA Kumasi – call 032 202 0052/ 0501301611
  iii.       KMA -call 0328000312/0322 023707
  iv.        Ghana Police Service -call 18555 or 191
   v.        National Disaster Management Organization (NADMO)-Kumasi -call 0244575219
  vi.        Ghana Ambulance Service- call 112


Emergency services (e.g. fire services, medical services, spillage etc) can be contacted by
phone call, using the contact numbers. The EHS Manager or Subproject Manager or
Supervisors can contact emergency services and provide them with all appropriate information.
Contact with the emergency services provider must be kept until they arrive on site.




Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP                                                    419
Stakeholder Communication Roles
All information on the emergency to the media and other stakeholders will be sent from one
focal person who is the responsible person for emergency coordination. This will be the
Subproject Manager or in his absence the EHS Manager.


In case of all emergencies, all workers are to note the following:
    DO NOT CALL the local media to report the emergency (It is the responsibility of the
     Subproject Manager and in his absence the EHS Manager)
    DO NOT CALL the family or friends of the persons involved in the emergency (It is the
     responsibility of the Subproject Manager and in his absence the EHS Manager)


System for Raising Alarm
Construction workers will be informed and educated on the system for raising alarms at the
workplaces. Generally, workers are expected to shout at least three times using the nature of
the emergency. Example if the emergency is fire, the worker who first sees or observes it will
shout FIRE! FIRE! FIRE! If the emergency is an accident, worker will shout ACCIDENT!
ACCIDENT! ACCIDENT! If the emergency is spillage, worker should shout SPILLAGE!
SPILLAGE! SPILLAGE!. If the emergency occurs offsite, the worker is expected to call the
Subproject Manager or EHS Manager immediately if he/she is able to do so.


Emergency Assembly Point at the Treatment Plant Site
The contractor for the rehabilitation of the treatment plant site and fencing of the site will create
an emergency assembly point at the site. The Emergency Assembly Point (EAP) is where all
staff and visitors at the treatment plant site will assemble during the occurrence of an
emergency which requires all workers and visitors to be accounted for.


Equipment and Resources
The contractor will ensure that there are adequate equipment and resources as well as
appropriate measures in place for its preparedness for an emergency. The equipment/resources
and measures to be put in place include:
    Availability of PPEs including safety googles, hand gloves, reflective jackets, raincoats,
     life jackets, wellington boots/safety shoes, for use at the treatment plant site among others;
    Provision of fire extinguishers;




Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP                                                      420
    Availability of equipment/tools (e.g. vehicle, wheel barrows, shovels etc) for emergencies;
    Creation of an emergency response team;
    Provision of mobile phones to supervisors to enable relevant stakeholders to be promptly
     informed and reached during emergencies.


Specific Emergency Situations
Fuel will not be stored on site. In the event of accidental spillage of concrete, a recovery truck
will be dispatched to the scene quickly to recover the product. Table 49 provides specific
emergency situations envisaged and the response measures proposed, while a general
emergency response flow chart has been developed for use as shown in Figure 18.


Table 49: Specific Emergency Situations and Proposed Responses
 WORKPLACE FIRES
 Response                   Any discovering fire should shout FIRE! FIRE! FIRE!
                            Assess fire before contacting GNFS immediately.
                            Isolate fire where possible using appropriate extinguishers.
                            Call the Fire Service emergency number 192 or general emergency line 999 if
                            extinguishers are not helpful
                            Evacuate all items and equipment in danger and ensure workers move to safe place
                            Assist Fire Service if needed to control fire
                            If there are injuries, provide first aid and send victims to nearest clinics/hospital
                            Complete an incident report.
 Preventive and             Have strategically placed and properly serviced firefighting equipment especially fire
 control measures           extinguishers at vantage points at the construction sites in the community.
                            Remove or protect combustible or flammable materials from heat sources.
                            Suppress and control sparks on site.
                            Suppress and control heat/fire (e.g. no burning or naked fire on or around site; idling
                            engines should be put off etc).
                            Routing checking and supervision of works/site.
 PERSONAL ACCIDENT/INJURY
 Response                   Work to be stopped if accident occurs at work camp/ construction sites.
                            Apply first aid.
                            Assess condition of the injured, and contact clinic / ambulance if required.
                            Complete accident report.
 Prevention and             Well-stocked first aid kits to be maintained.
 control measures           Provide clear signage onsite.
                            Provide appropriate PPES for workers and ensure its use.
                            Educate and train workers on the use of PPEs and relevance of signage.




Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP                                                                         421
                            Evaluate hazards at workplaces.
                            Carry out regular inspection of work activities and workers behaviour
 FLOOD
 Response                   Where possible move machinery and workers to higher ground.
 Prevention and             Monitor weather data and flood warnings for advice.
 control measures           Inspect nearby drains close to workplaces, and if choked with waste, remove waste
                            appropriately to ensure free flow of water
                            Identify which materials or equipment need protective measures in the event of a flood and
                            move such materials/equipment to safety in stormy weather.
                            Identify where to easily get a stormwater pump to buy and use in the event of a flood.
                            Continue to check the media for emergency information before and during floods
 SPILL QUARRY DUST, AGGREGATES AND CONCRETE
 Response                   Remove remaining quarry dust, aggregates and/ or concrete from spill area.
                            Clean up spillage.
                            Rectify the source of spillage.
                            Complete incident report.
 Preventive and             Regularly inspect potential points of spills.
 control measures           Monitor levels of storage of products
                            Do not sweep or hose concrete spillage or aggregates into storm water drains
                            Do not stockpile spill aggregates on roads or walkways
 VEHICLE ACCIDENT
 Response                   EHS Manager must be informed immediately by staff available or the driver of the vehicle
                            who will intend report to the Subproject Manager/ Resident Engineer
                            Driver or staff on board vehicle should erect reflective triangles at a safe distance in front
                            and behind vehicle
                            The EHS Manager shall visit the scene of the accident, inform and invite the Police to the
                            accident scene or spot.
                            The EHS Manager shall investigate the cause of the accident and submit a report to the
                            Contractor.
                            Where casualties are involved, the EHS Manager must ensure they are sent to the nearest
                            clinic or hospital.
                            Complete an accident report
 Prevention and             All drivers shall observe the specified speed limits of 20 km/h - 30km/h through Asafo and
 control measures           Abinkyi communities, and at the construction sites.
                            All drivers on major roads shall observe a highway speed limit of 60km/h and the
                            mandatory speed limit of 50km/h in communities.
                            Make sure the accident area is safe and be alert for physical dangers such as traffic or fires
                            or smoke and if fire is suspected call fire service on 192 immediately.
                            Ensure cars are park safely and well away from the accident spot.
                            Make vehicles safe by switching off the ignition of all damaged and affected cars




Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP                                                                     422
                            Call emergency line 999 or 112 for ambulance service if there are injuries or medical
                            emergencies.
                            Assess the casualties quickly and provide first aid if possible
 WATER – BURST PIPE
 Response                   The person who first sees this shall immediately report to the EHS Manager or the
                            Supervising Engineer/ Consultant.
                            The EHS Manager shall appoint a plumber to work on the situation who will identify and
                            shut the nearest valve controlling water supply to the burst pipe.
                            The Plumber shall initiate remedial action and complete an incident report.
 Prevention and             Ensure all water pipelines are safe and in good working condition.
 Control Measures           If the affected water pipe at the workplace is a distribution pipeline, inform the GWCL
                            immediately.
                            Do not allow trucks or vehicles to pass over exposed PVC water pipes
 SEWAGE LEAKAGE/SPILLS – DAMAGE OR BREAKAGE OF SEWER PIPES
 Response                   The person who first sees this shall immediately report to the Site Supervisor or EHS
                            Manager or the Supervising Engineer
                            The EHS Manager or the Supervising Engineer shall call KMA immediately
                            The EHS Manager shall also call a professional plumber to start working on the situation
                            and make the necessary repairs.
                            KMA Waste Management Team shall lead in the cleaning up and disposal of spilled
                            sewage
                            The EHS Manager shall prepare an incident report
 Prevention and             Supervising engineer shall confirm depth/location of sewer pipes from KMA before
 control measures           instructing contractor to work on any section of sewer lines
                            Contractor shall tread cautiously when trenching or excavating along existing sewer lines
                            (i.e. gradual removal of top soils/surface materials until existing sewer pipe is exposed)
                            Keep spills contained in the spill area and out of gutters/ storm drains.
                            Use sand or some excavated materials from the trenches to contain the spilled sewage
                            KMA shall dispose of spilled sewage
 ELECTRIC SHOCK AND ASSOCIATED INJURIES
 Response                   Isolate power line/ power, if safe to do so. If not, use a dry, nonconducting object made of
                            cardboard, plastic or wood to move the source away from you and the injured person.
                            Provide injured from shock with first aid and then to nearest clinic/ hospital for medical
                            care or call ambulance on 112
                            Complete incident report
 Prevention and             Ensure all electrical power sources and cables are properly insulated
 Control Measures           Avoid loose hanging wires
                            Never use a damaged extension cord or defective electrical device.
                            Keep wet hands away from outlets.




Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP                                                                    423
                            Do not trench or cut through grounds that may have buried electric wires.
                            Avoid working near or close to electrical or transmission lines and pylons.
                            Check and report all electrical hazards.
                            Train employees on electrical safety.

11.0     GRIEVANCE REDRESS MECHANISM

11.1     Basis for Grievance Redress Mechanism


Even though this ESIA preparation processes, stakeholder consultation was carried out in a
consultative and participatory manner, experience has shown that grievances are further raised
sometimes by project-affected persons during the project implementation. In the light of this,
grievance resolution procedures for projects are necessary to resolve disputes that may arise
from an aggrieved person.


A Grievance Redress Mechanism (GRM) is a system by which queries or clarifications or
problems that arise out of implementation of a project are resolved and addressed efficiently
and effectively. When addressed, the grievances are expected to ensure support, as well as help
achieve results and sustainability of project activities.


As part of this ESIA, a GRM with multiple avenues or channels for lodging complaints and
their resolution in a way that is transparent, prompt and timely and with clear procedures is
established as below. The establishment of the GRM on all the GAMA subprojects is a
requirement by the World Bank to ensure resolution of project related conflicts or complaints.


11.2     Objectives of the Grievance Redress Mechanism


The objectives of the Grievance Redress Mechanism among others is to:
     Resolve grievances or complaints from affected persons, groups and institutions
         promptly, fairly and in a manner, that to extent possible acceptable to all parties;
     Provide affected people with avenues for making a complaint or resolving any dispute
         that may arise during the implementation of the project;
     Ensure that appropriate and mutually acceptable redress actions are identified and
         implemented to the satisfaction of complainants; and
     Avoid the need to resort to judicial proceedings.




Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP                                                     429
11.3     Grievance Redress Structures During Construction Phase


The grievance redress structures will use the already existing three-tier GAMA S&W Project
Grievance Redress framework to address complaints that may arise as a result of the proposed
construction interventions.


The grievance redress structures will comprise three (3) tiers:
    1. Local/Community Mediation Committee (Grievance Redress Team)
    2. KMA level redress committee; and
    3. GAMA PCU/MSWR


Aggrieved individuals, groups and institutions have the right to access any of the three tiers at
all times and at no cost. During the ESIA preparation, consultation with the community
members/PAPs, it was highlighted that the three-tier redress system established by the project
has been generally successful in resolving conflicts or complaints in timely manner and in an
amicable way rather than resort to the courts. Although the redress process has been explained
to community members/PAPs during the stakeholder consultations, the grievance redress
process shall be disclosed to PAPs, Community members, the Metropolitan Assembly and
other key stakeholders in advance to the commencement of the construction works. Continuous
awareness creation and sensitization will be conducted to educate community people/PAPs and
institutions about the redress system and the process to ensure respect for rights and anonymity
of complainant and the system is compliant with both national and World Bank safeguard
policy requirements.


First Tier: Local/Community Mediation Committee
The first tier of the redress system will be at the local community level and will use localized
redress structures that take into account specific project issues, cultural context and local
customs that are accessible and cheaper for dispute resolution.


Second Tier: KMA Level Redress Committees
The second-tier redress process will be managed by the Kumasi Metropolitan Assembly
(KMA) to address project specific complaints that may be reported to the Assembly or referred
from first tier redress process if resolution fails at that level.



Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP                                                  430
Third Tier: GAMA PCU/MSWR
The third-tier redress process will be at the PCU/MSWR under the auspices of the Ministry of
Sanitation and Water Resources to address project specific complaints that may be referred by
the second and third tier redress process as shown in Figure 19.


11.4      The Law Court


Where resolution of complaints or disputes failed be resolved at all the three tiers described
above, then the complainnat can take the matter to the law courts. However, it is expecetd that
this may not be the case as the three levels above would be able to amicably resolve the
complaints/disputes.


11.5      Membership and Functions of the Grievance Redress Committee


The Grievance Redress Committee will include the following:


First- tier redress committee members include:
      KMA Development Planning Officer (Chair)
      Contractor representative
      Consultant representative
      PCU Safeguards Officer
      The elected Assembly member of the electoral area;
      Unit committee member;
      Community Opinion leader;
      Representatives of PAPs; and
      A co-opted member- optional (depending upon the nature of the complaint if necessary)


Gender consideration will be factored into the selection of members to the committees. As the
subproject activities will occur in three major zones, each section or zone of the subproject will
have a specific local community Grievance Redress Team (GRT) consisting of the above
composition.


The following specific local community grievance redress teams will be formed under Tier 1:



Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP                                                   431
    i.      KsTU via OA Terminal to Labor Roundabout Sewer Line GRT;
  ii.       Asafo Market to Church of Pentecost Sewer Line GRT
 iii.       Treatment Plant Site GRT


Second tier redress committee members:
        KMA Coordinating Director or representative (chair);
        KMA Project Coordinator/Safeguard Focal Person at the Assembly- Secretary;
        PCU Safeguards Specialist;
        The elected Assembly member of the area;
        Representative of the Supervising Consultant;
        PAP or his/her representative; and
        Other co-opted members when necessary.


Third tier redress committee members
        Chief Director/ designated representative (chair);
        GAMA/GKMA Project Coordinator- Secretary;
        Safeguards Specialists at the PCU;
        GAMA Sanitary Engineer in charge of the Asafo subproject;
        GKMA Project Coordinator at KMA; and
        Representative of the PAPs.


The key functions of the Grievance Redress Committees are:
a) Recording, screening and investigating grievances;
b) Resolving the grievances in consultation with the affected persons/ parties and other
      stakeholders;
c) Informing the affected persons/ parties about the resolution of the grievances;
d) Forwarding any unresolved cases to higher authorities/levels; and
e) Providing periodic reports on the grievance resolutions to the project management and
      other stakeholders as required.


In addition to the main function of resolving grievances, disputes, complaints and conflicts, the
Grievance Redress Teams will also:
            I.    assist to ensure smooth implementation of the project;



Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP                                                  432
          II.   establish dialogue with the PAPs; and
         III.   ensure that their concerns and suggestions are incorporated and implemented
                during the project


11.6      Steps of the Grievance Process


The general steps of the grievance process comprise:
      Registration of complaints;
      Verifying of complaints
      Determining and implementing the redress action;
      Verifying the redress action; and
      Monitoring and Evaluation.


Registration of Complaints
Complaints can be lodged verbally or in writing or through phone calls to any of the redress
committees. At the local community level, the elected Assembly Member for the area or his/her
Unit Committee member will receive complaints and log in a dedicated logbook since the
Assembly Member or the Unit Committee member lives within the local community and is
easily accessible. This will ensure simple and straightforward complaints process. The
Assembly member/Unit Committee member will initially screen the grievance to confirm if it
is related or linked to the proposed project. At the Metropolitan Assembly level, the KMA
Project Coordinator/Safeguards Focal Person shall receive all complaints and shall officially
log these complaints in a dedicated logbook for that purpose. At the local community level, the
Assembly member or Unit Committee member will inform the team leader/chair for the GRT
within 24 hours on any complaint lodged. For the second tier at the Assembly level, the KMA
Project Coordinator will inform the Coordinating Director/team leader for the Grievance
Redress Committee within 24 hours on any complaint lodged. A sample grievance redress form
to guide the logging and resolution of complaints is provided in Annex 7.


Where the complaint or complainant does not directly go to the GRT member receiving and
logging the complaint, the Team leader shall ensure that the Committee calls or send a message
to the complainant to acknowledge receipt of his/her complaint within 48 hours, informing




Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP                                                433
him/her that the complaint/grievance has been received and logged. Logging and
acknowledging complaints should be done within 2 working days of receipt of the complaint.


Complaints hotlines will be provided to the public/community. Contacts of the Assembly
members, Unit Committee members and the current KMA Toilet number (0553298039)
provided by the project will be used. The existing GRM Hotlines at the GKMA- SWP office
in Danyame-Kumasi 0207811616 and (0)322499618 will also be used.


Verifying of Complaints
Once a complaint and/or suggestion / comment has been forwarded to the Committee, the
Chairperson of the Committee will nominate a committee member to verify the issue prior to
a thorough investigation, and to prepare a report based on findings for consideration of the
Committee. The investigations will include collecting and review of relevant documents,
making site visits if necessary, consulting appropriate staff, contacting external stakeholders
(if required) and interviewing the complainant as appropriate. Verifying of complaints should
be done within 3 working days after receipt of complaint.


Determining and Implementing the Redress Action
When a grievance/dispute is recorded as per above-mentioned registration procedures, the
grievance redress team will be called into action, and mediation meetings will be organized
with interested parties. Minutes of meetings will be recorded. The grievance redress team will
determine the redress action in consultation with the complainant if necessary. The proposed
redress action and the timeframe will be discussed within 5 working days of receipt of the
grievance. The grievance issue will be resolved, and feedback will be provided within 7
working days of receipt of complaints.


Verifying the Redress Action
The grievance redress team in company with the complainant, will visit the complaint area to
verify if satisfactory redress action has been carried out. If the complainant is not satisfied with
the outcome of the redress action, additional steps will be taken as agreed with the complainant
to resolve the issue or reach an amicable agreement. Verification will be completed within 3
days of the execution of the redress action.


Redress Decision and Feedback


Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP                                                     434
The redress committee will make a decision on all matters received, in consultation with the
complainant, and provide feedback on the necessary actions taken. When the complainant is
satisfied with the redress action, the complaint will be closed and logged in the grievance
logbook and endorsed by the complainant.


Second Tier/KMA Level Redress Team
If the complainant is not satisfied with the decision of the local community level grievance
redress team, he/she can escalate the issue to the Assembly level by bringing it to the attention
of the KMA Project Coordinator. The chairperson of the local Community GRT can also refer
the issue to the KMA level. The KMA level redress team has 10 working days after receipt of
the complaint from Tier 1 to resolve the matter.


Additional Steps and Court of Law
If the complainant is not satisfied with the decision of the KMA grievance redress team, he/she
can escalate the issue to the attention of the Chief Director at the Ministry of Sanitation and
Water Resources, or the GAMA PCU Coordinator will draw the attention of the Chief Director
at the MSWR about the unresolved grievance. The Chief Director at the Ministry will mediate
on the issue within 10 working days from the date of receipt of such complaint by the Ministry.
If such a timeline is not possible, the Chief Director shall inform the PCU accordingly giving
reasons and possible new date. Below is the diagrammatic presentation of the process.


If the complainant remains dissatisfied with the mediation effort of the Ministry, the
complainant has the right to pursue other redress options or appropriate recourse via a
competent court of jurisdiction of Ghana. The Constitution allows any aggrieved person the
right of access to Court of law.




Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP                                                  435

11.7     Monitoring and Evaluation of the GRM Implementation


The Monitoring and Evaluation Team from the GAMA PCU Office will monitor the activities
of the Grievance Redress Teams to ensure that grievances lodged by complainants are
followed-up and resolved amicably as much as possible during the construction period until
when the project is completed and handed over to the Assembly. The GAMA PCU monitoring
cost is already part of the GAMA AF Project cost.


11.8     Cost for Grievance Redress Activities


It is estimated that a lump sum of Eighty Thousand Cedis (Ghc80,000.00), should be able to
cover the Grievance Redress activities at the local community and the Kumasi Metropolitan
Assembly levels during the construction period of 8 months. This cost includes mainly phone
call credits to deserving committee members, transport allowance for the representatives of
complainants to attend meetings at the Assembly, fuel for field verifications/investigations by
the team and lunch for members for extended meeting hours.


11.9     Grievance Redress during the Project Operational Phase


The grievance redress process during the operational phase will follow the existing Assembly
complaints management arrangement which is handled by the Public Relations and Complaints
Committee of the Assembly.




Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP                                                437
12.0 CAPACITY BUILDING PLAN FOR STAKEHOLDERS FOR THE ESIA
IMPLEMENTATION

To effectively implement this ESIA document, training will be undertaken by the GAMA PCU
and KMA to equip key stakeholders who will be involved in the implementation, monitoring,
and reporting on the ESIA. The capacity building will be carried out prior to the
commencement of the civil works. The training is aimed at providing knowledge and
understanding on the ESIA requirements, the skills required, and roles and responsibilities. A
proposed plan for the capacity building is presented in Table 51. The Plan is estimated to cost
One Hundred and Fifty Thousand Ghana Cedis (GHS150,000.00).

Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP                                                              443
14.0       DECOMMISSIONING PLAN


14.1       Post-Construction Phase


14.1.1 Equipment and other Site Facilities
The Contractor will dismantle and relocate all equipment and other facilities and leftover
materials such as pieces of wood, iron rods, spent concrete and chipping, broken manholes,
scrap metals, debris, obsolete construction equipment, tec., at the subproject sites upon
completion of the works. Besides wastes that can be given out or sold out for reuse or recycling,
all other wastes generated will be disposed of at KMA approved waste disposal site at the Oti
Landfill after the decommissioning.


Decommissioning of the equipment and other facilities including mobile toilet units will take
into consideration the intended use and in compliance with both Ghanaian and international
policies governing decommissioning of such facilities.


Guidelines to help in decommissioning any site office and other subproject installations are
presented in Table 53.

Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP                                                                           445
14.1.2 Subproject Equipment/Machinery and Materials
The subproject equipment such as bulldozers, hydraulic excavators, pumps, generators,
vehicles and other machinery will be relocated to new or other work sites in the country. Any
leftover materials like sand, chippings will be removed from the site.


14.2     Post-Operational Phase – Subproject Facilities


The Contractor is expected to handover the subproject facilities to MSWR/GAMA PCU/KMA
after construction for operation of the improved ASSS. The KMA and other stakeholder will
ensure that they last for the period it has been designed for and even beyond. The sewage
treatment facilities will not be decommissioned entirely after the designed period but
rehabilitation and expansion works will be carried out as appropriate for further improvement
using modern and appropriate technologies.


Any such large-scale rehabilitation and improvement works to be carried out in the long term
will be undertaken in line with the environmental assessment procedures of the country. The
intended rehabilitation and improvement works will be registered with the EPA to enable the
Agency advise on the level of environmental assessment and reporting to be carried out in
accordance with the Environmental Assessment Regulations 1999, LI 1652.


Other stakeholders and relevant institutions will be informed prior to the commencement of
any major rehabilitation work on the sewage treatment facilities, and these include:
  KMA – Local government authority in charge of the sewage treatment facility at Abinkyi/
       Asafo;
  DUR – Government Agency responsible for the roads and securing the RoWs; and
  Utility Companies – ECG/ GRIDCo, GWCL and the Telecommunication companies who
       are mandated by law to use road reservations for their utility and service lines.




Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP                                                446
15.0     CONCLUSION


The implementation of the proposed ASSSPWP will satisfy the relevant national
environmental protection laws and international good practice requirements especially the
triggered WB OPs. The major potential environmental and social risks and impacts associated
with the proposed ASSSPWP have been identified and duly assessed in this ESIS. The ESIA
Study has thus assessed identified issues of biophysical and social environmental importance,
some positive and others negative. The proposed subproject will help improve the function of
the ASSS and discharge of treated wastewater into the Subin River.


The potential positive impacts to accrue from the proposed subproject will include:
 Discharge of all sewage from the Asafo community into the waste stabilization ponds at
    Abinkyi for treatment of the wastewater and discharge of treated effluent into the Subin
    River that meets the GS 1212:2019 values for discharge of treated sewage into a natural
    water body;
 Improvement in the quality of effluent discharged to the environment to enhance the water
    quality in the Subin River;
 Creation of direct and indirect jobs for the unemployed; and
 Ridding the area of squatters to prevent any major epidermic or disasters that can endanger
    life and property.


The key potential negative impacts during the construction and the operation and maintenance
phases of the sewerage system will result from:
 wastewater management and disposal;
 sludge management and disposal at a designated waste treatment facility;
 ambient air quality deterioration;
 occupational health and safety administration;
 public safety and health risk;
 transportation of equipment and products to and from the site; and
 Security.


Implementation of the proposed mitigation and management measures for the identified
adverse impacts by the Contractor and KMA with support from GAMA-SWP PCU during the
construction and operation/ maintenance phases of the ASSSPWP will ensure that significant


Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP                                              447
impacts are reduced to acceptable limits, and the resulting residual impacts significance will
mostly be minor or negligible. Prior commencement of the subprojects, the GAMA-PCU and
KMA will provide adequate training for all stakeholders who will be engaged in the
implementation and monitoring of the ESIA. The training will provide the stakeholders the
necessary knowledge and skills to be able to comply with the safeguards requirements during
the project implementation. It is estimated that a total of GHS 485,000 will be required to
implement the ESIA/ESMP during the construction phase.


A provisional environmental and social management and monitoring programme to help
manage and monitor the impacts and effectiveness of the mitigation measures, which will help
sustain environmental quality within acceptable guidelines/standards has also been provided
supra for implementation.


Various stakeholders were engaged during the ESIA study and their concerns have been
included in the ESIS. Stakeholders and residents of the community are generally receptive and
open for discussions on the subproject. All stakeholders, including government agencies, local
groupings and community leaders the team interacted with showed strong need for
improvement of the ASSS and welcomed the proposed interventions. They are therefore
willing to work and cooperate with GAMA- SWP PCU for the implementation of the
interventions that will improve the living conditions of the subproject communities and the city
of Kumasi generally.


Some private properties and assets will be impacted by the proposed works and in fulfilment
of the World Bank Safeguards Policy on Involuntary Resettlement (OP 4.12) requirements
together with our own national laws on compensation, a standalone Resettlement Action Plan
(RAP) was prepared by the MRWR and KMA which took care of the compensation for all
persons who may lose assets or livelihoods before implementation of the proposed ASSSPWP.


The KMA will comply with all regulatory requirements by securing relevant statutory permits
for the construction and operation of the interventions. KMA and with assistance of GAMA-
PCU will work with the Contractor to ensure compliance with the conditions of the various
permits and authorisations.




Revised Draft Report- ESIA Study of ASSSPWP                                                 448
The KMA will ensure that the ASSSPWP is implemented in line with all relevant national as
well as international policies, laws and standards/ guidelines to enhance the intended benefits
and reduce potential negative impacts to acceptable levels. A stakeholder engagement program
and grievance redress mechanism will be implemented during the subproject implementation
to ensure that stakeholder concerns and grievances are managed effectively to minimize
potential conflicts during construction and operation and ensure sustainability.
